AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility of a repeatable, quantitative experimental framework for evaluating photogrammetry pipelines and digital-twin reconstructions using synthetic data.
AI.conclusion: Provides a controllable benchmark and toolchain for objective comparison of photogrammetry approaches, enabling systematic study of design choices and parameter estimation accuracy.

title: Bi-Level Prompt Optimization for Multimodal LLM-as-a-Judge
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have become widely adopted as automated judges for evaluating AI-generated content. Despite their success, aligning LLM-based evaluations with human judgments remains challenging. While supervised fine-tuning on human-labeled data can improve alignment, it is costly and inflexible, requiring new training for each task or dataset. Recent progress in auto prompt optimization (APO) offers a more efficient alternative by automatically improving the instructions that guide LLM judges. However, existing APO methods primarily target text-only evaluations and remain underexplored in multimodal settings. In this work, we study auto prompt optimization for multimodal LLM-as-a-judge, particularly for evaluating AI-generated images. We identify a key bottleneck: multimodal models can only process a limited number of visual examples due to context window constraints, which hinders effective trial-and-error prompt refinement. To overcome this, we propose BLPO, a bi-level prompt optimization framework that converts images into textual representations while preserving evaluation-relevant visual cues. Our bi-level optimization approach jointly refines the judge prompt and the I2T prompt to maintain fidelity under limited context budgets. Experiments on four datasets and three LLM judges demonstrate the effectiveness of our method.

AI.tldr: BLPO: a bi-level prompt optimization method for multimodal LLM judges that converts images to text while preserving cues, jointly optimizing judge and image-to-text prompts within a constrained context budget to better align AI evaluation with human judgments across datasets.
AI.motivation: To reliably align multimodal LLM-based evaluations with human judgments while avoiding costly supervised fine-tuning; address limitations of existing auto prompt optimization (APO) in multimodal settings and the context window bottleneck that hampers prompt refinement.
AI.method: Propose BLPO, which converts visual content into text representations that retain evaluation-relevant cues and performs bi-level optimization to jointly refine the judge prompt and the I2T prompt under fixed context budgets.
AI.result: Empirical validation on four datasets and three LLM judges shows that BLPO improves evaluation effectiveness and aligns better with human judgments under constrained context windows.
AI.conclusion: BLPO effectively mitigates the context-window bottleneck in multimodal LLM-as-a-judge and offers a scalable approach for multimodal evaluation; demonstrates strong potential for broader multimodal prompt optimization with limited context.

title: HyperDet: 3D Object Detection with Hyper 4D Radar Point Clouds
summary: 4D mmWave radar provides weather-robust, velocity-aware measurements and is more cost-effective than LiDAR. However, radar-only 3D detection still trails LiDAR-based systems because radar point clouds are sparse, irregular, and often corrupted by multipath noise, yielding weak and unstable geometry. We present HyperDet, a detector-agnostic radar-only 3D detection framework that constructs a task-aware hyper 4D radar point cloud for standard LiDAR-oriented detectors. HyperDet aggregates returns from multiple surround-view 4D radars over consecutive frames to improve coverage and density, then applies geometry-aware cross-sensor consensus validation with a lightweight self-consistency check outside overlap regions to suppress inconsistent returns. It further integrates a foreground-focused diffusion module with training-time mixed radar-LiDAR supervision to densify object structures while lifting radar attributes (e.g., Doppler, RCS); the model is distilled into a consistency model for single-step inference. On MAN TruckScenes, HyperDet consistently improves over raw radar inputs with VoxelNeXt and CenterPoint, partially narrowing the radar-LiDAR gap. These results show that input-level refinement enables radar to better leverage LiDAR-oriented detectors without architectural modifications.

AI.tldr: HyperDet is a detector-agnostic radar-only 3D detector that builds a richer 4D radar point cloud from multi-frame surround-view radars, applies cross-sensor consensus validation, and uses a diffusion-based densification with mixed radar-LiDAR supervision, distilled for fast single-step inference. It improves radar-only detection on MAN TruckScenes, narrowing the radar–LiDAR gap without changing LiDAR-oriented detectors.
AI.motivation: Radar 4D mmWave data are weather-robust but yield sparse, irregular clouds prone to multipath noise, causing weaker 3D geometry than LiDAR. To leverage LiDAR-oriented detectors and reduce the radar–LiDAR gap, input-level refinement and cross-sensor strategies are proposed.
AI.method: 1) Construct a task-aware hyper 4D radar point cloud by aggregating returns from multiple surround-view 4D radars over consecutive frames to increase coverage and density. 2) Apply geometry-aware cross-sensor consensus validation with a lightweight self-consistency check outside overlap regions to suppress inconsistent returns. 3) Integrate a foreground-focused diffusion module with training-time mixed radar-LiDAR supervision to densify object structures and elevate radar attributes (e.g., Doppler, RCS). 4) Distill the model into a consistency model for single-step inference.
AI.result: On MAN TruckScenes, HyperDet consistently improves over raw radar inputs when paired with LiDAR-oriented detectors like VoxelNeXt and CenterPoint, partially narrowing the radar-LiDAR performance gap.
AI.conclusion: Input-level refinement enables radar to leverage LiDAR-oriented detectors without architectural changes, offering detector-agnostic, improved radar-only 3D detection and practical path toward broader radar adoption.

title: Predicting the post-wildfire mudflow onset using machine learning models on multi-parameter experimental data
summary: Post-wildfire mudflows are increasingly hazardous due to the prevalence of wildfires, including those on the wildland-urban interface. Upon burning, soil on the surface or immediately beneath becomes hydrophobic, a phenomenon that occurs predominantly on sand-based hillslopes. Rainwater and eroded soil blanket the downslope, leading to catastrophic debris flows. Soil hydrophobicity enhances erosion, resulting in post-wildfire debris flows that differ from natural mudflows in intensity, duration, and destructiveness. Thus, it is crucial to understand the timing and conditions of debris-flow onset, driven by the coupled effects of critical parameters: varying rain intensities (RI), slope gradients, water-entry values, and grain sizes (D50). Machine Learning (ML) techniques have become increasingly valuable in geotechnical engineering due to their ability to model complex systems without predefined assumptions. This study applies multiple ML algorithms: multiple linear regression (MLR), logistic regression (LR), support vector classifier (SVC), K-means clustering, and principal component analysis (PCA) to predict and classify outcomes from laboratory experiments that model field conditions using a rain device on various soils in sloped flumes. While MLR effectively predicted total discharge, erosion predictions were less accurate, especially for coarse sand. LR and SVC achieved good accuracy in classifying failure outcomes, supported by clustering and dimensionality reduction. Sensitivity analysis revealed that fine sand is highly susceptible to erosion, particularly under low-intensity, long-duration rainfall. Results also show that the first 10 minutes of high-intensity rain are most critical for discharge and failure. These findings highlight the potential of ML for post-wildfire hazard assessment and emergency response planning.

AI.tldr: ML techniques applied to lab-scale rainfall experiments on various soils under sloped conditions can predict discharge and classify debris-flow failure; MLR excels in discharge, LR/SVC in failure classification; fine sands are highly erosive; early high-intensity rainfall is critical; ML shows promise for hazard assessment.
AI.motivation: Post-wildfire debris flows pose hazards due to soil hydrophobicity; understanding onset conditions and predicting outcomes is essential for risk mitigation. Machine learning offers modeling of complex, coupled factors without predefined physical equations.
AI.method: Laboratory experiments with a rain device on soils placed on sloped flumes, modeling field conditions. Variables include rain intensity (RI), slope gradient, water-entry value, and grain size (D50). Applied ML algorithms: multiple linear regression (MLR), logistic regression (LR), support vector classifier (SVC), K-means clustering, and principal component analysis (PCA). Evaluated discharge and erosion predictions, and failure classification; used clustering and dimensionality reduction to interpret results; conducted sensitivity analysis.
AI.result: MLR accurately predicted total discharge; erosion predictions were less accurate, notably for coarse sand. LR and SVC achieved good accuracy in classifying failure outcomes. Clustering and PCA supported the interpretations. Sensitivity analysis showed fine sand is highly susceptible to erosion, especially under low-intensity, long-duration rainfall. The first 10 minutes of high-intensity rainfall were identified as the most critical period for discharge and failure.
AI.conclusion: ML approaches show promise for post-wildfire hazard assessment and emergency response planning, offering data-driven insight into when and where debris flows may initiate and their potential severity.

title: Selective Prior Synchronization via SYNC Loss
summary: Prediction under uncertainty is a critical requirement for the deep neural network to succeed responsibly. This paper focuses on selective prediction, which allows DNNs to make informed decisions about when to predict or abstain based on the uncertainty level of their predictions. Current methods are either ad-hoc such as SelectiveNet, focusing on how to modify the network architecture or objective function, or post-hoc such as softmax response, achieving selective prediction through analyzing the model's probabilistic outputs. We observe that post-hoc methods implicitly generate uncertainty information, termed the selective prior, which has traditionally been used only during inference. We argue that the selective prior provided by the selection mechanism is equally vital during the training stage. Therefore, we propose the SYNC loss which introduces a novel integration of ad-hoc and post-hoc method. Specifically, our approach incorporates the softmax response into the training process of SelectiveNet, enhancing its selective prediction capabilities by examining the selective prior. Evaluated across various datasets, including CIFAR-100, ImageNet-100, and Stanford Cars, our method not only enhances the model's generalization capabilities but also surpasses previous works in selective prediction performance, and sets new benchmarks for state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: SYNC loss blends softmax-based uncertainty into training of SelectiveNet, boosting selective prediction and achieving new SOTA on several datasets.
AI.motivation: Selective prediction under uncertainty is critical for reliable DNN deployment. Existing approaches are either ad-hoc architectural/ objective changes or post-hoc analyses of probabilistic outputs. The selective prior from post-hoc methods should also inform training.
AI.method: Introduce SYNC loss that integrates the softmax response into the training of SelectiveNet, unifying ad-hoc and post-hoc approaches by leveraging the selective prior during training.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on CIFAR-100, ImageNet-100, and Stanford Cars shows improved generalization and superior selective-prediction performance, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.conclusion: Training with the selective prior via the SYNC loss provides a principled integration of post-hoc uncertainty into training, enhancing selective prediction capabilities and generalization beyond prior methods.

title: AgentNoiseBench: Benchmarking Robustness of Tool-Using LLM Agents Under Noisy Condition
summary: Recent advances in large language models have enabled LLM-based agents to achieve strong performance on a variety of benchmarks. However, their performance in real-world deployments often that observed on benchmark settings, especially in complex and imperfect environments. This discrepancy largely arises because prevailing training and evaluation paradigms are typically built on idealized assumptions, overlooking the inherent stochasticity and noise present in real-world interactions. To bridge this gap, we introduce AgentNoiseBench, a framework for systematically evaluating the robustness of agentic models under noisy environments. We first conduct an in-depth analysis of biases and uncertainties in real-world scenarios and categorize environmental noise into two primary types: user-noise and tool-noise. Building on this analysis, we develop an automated pipeline that injects controllable noise into existing agent-centric benchmarks while preserving task solvability. Leveraging this pipeline, we perform extensive evaluations across a wide range of models with diverse architectures and parameter scales. Our results reveal consistent performance variations under different noise conditions, highlighting the sensitivity of current agentic models to realistic environmental perturbations.

AI.tldr: AgentNoiseBench proposes a framework to evaluate LLM-based agents under noisy real-world conditions by injecting controllable user and tool noise into benchmarks, revealing model sensitivity across architectures.
AI.motivation: Real-world deployments of agentic LLMs diverge from benchmark performance due to inherent stochasticity and noise; current training/evaluation paradigms rely on idealized assumptions. A principled, automated robustness evaluation framework is needed to bridge this gap and guide improvements.
AI.method: 1) Analyze biases and uncertainties in real-world scenarios; classify environmental noise into user-noise and tool-noise. 2) Develop an automated pipeline to inject controllable noise into existing agent-centric benchmarks while preserving task solvability. 3) Conduct extensive cross-model evaluations across diverse architectures and parameter scales.
AI.result: The noisy benchmarks induce consistent performance variations across noise conditions, demonstrating the sensitivity of current agentic models to realistic perturbations; the pipeline enables controlled, comparable evaluations across models and setups.
AI.conclusion: AgentNoiseBench provides a principled framework for robustness assessment of agentic models in realistic environments and highlights their fragility to stochastic perturbations, informing future robustness-oriented research and model design.

title: ReaDy-Go: Real-to-Sim Dynamic 3D Gaussian Splatting Simulation for Environment-Specific Visual Navigation with Moving Obstacles
summary: Visual navigation models often struggle in real-world dynamic environments due to limited robustness to the sim-to-real gap and the difficulty of training policies tailored to target deployment environments (e.g., households, restaurants, and factories). Although real-to-sim navigation simulation using 3D Gaussian Splatting (GS) can mitigate this gap, prior works have assumed only static scenes or unrealistic dynamic obstacles, despite the importance of safe navigation in dynamic environments. To address these issues, we propose ReaDy-Go, a novel real-to-sim simulation pipeline that synthesizes photorealistic dynamic scenarios for target environments. ReaDy-Go generates photorealistic navigation datasets for dynamic environments by combining a reconstructed static GS scene with dynamic human GS obstacles, and trains policies robust to both the sim-to-real gap and moving obstacles. The pipeline consists of three components: (1) a dynamic GS simulator that integrates scene GS with a human animation module, enabling the insertion of animatable human GS avatars and the synthesis of plausible human motions from 2D trajectories, (2) navigation dataset generation for dynamic environments that leverages the simulator, a robot expert planner designed for dynamic GS representations, and a human planner, and (3) policy learning using the generated datasets. ReaDy-Go outperforms baselines across target environments in both simulation and real-world experiments, demonstrating improved navigation performance even after sim-to-real transfer and in the presence of moving obstacles. Moreover, zero-shot sim-to-real deployment in an unseen environment indicates its generalization potential. Project page: https://syeon-yoo.github.io/ready-go-site/.

AI.tldr: ReaDy-Go: a real-to-sim dynamic navigation pipeline using Gaussian Splatting to synthesize photorealistic dynamic scenes with animatable human avatars, enabling robust, zero-shot sim-to-real navigation in dynamic environments.
AI.motivation: To close the sim-to-real gap in dynamic, real-world navigation by providing photorealistic, dynamic training data and robust policies that handle moving obstacles, targeted at households, restaurants, and factories.
AI.method: Three-part pipeline: (1) dynamic Gaussian Splatting (GS) simulator with a static GS scene plus animatable human GS avatars derived from 2D trajectories, (2) dataset generation for dynamic environments using a robot expert planner for dynamic GS and a human planner, and (3) policy learning trained on the generated datasets.
AI.result: ReaDy-Go achieves superior performance to baselines in both simulated and real-world tests across target environments, showing improved navigation under sim-to-real transfer and moving obstacles; zero-shot sim-to-real deployment in an unseen environment demonstrates strong generalization potential.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dynamic, photorealistic real-to-sim pipeline improves robustness and generalization for dynamic navigation tasks, offering a viable path to safer, more reliable deployment in real-world settings.

title: AM-FM: A Foundation Model for Ambient Intelligence Through WiFi
summary: Ambient intelligence, continuously understanding human presence, activity, and physiology in physical spaces, is fundamental to smart environments, health monitoring, and human-computer interaction. WiFi infrastructure provides a ubiquitous, always-on, privacy-preserving substrate for this capability across billions of IoT devices. Yet this potential remains largely untapped, as wireless sensing has typically relied on task-specific models that require substantial labeled data and limit practical deployment. We present AM-FM, the first foundation model for ambient intelligence and sensing through WiFi. AM-FM is pre-trained on 9.2 million unlabeled Channel State Information (CSI) samples collected over 439 days from 20 commercial device types deployed worldwide, learning general-purpose representations via contrastive learning, masked reconstruction, and physics-informed objectives tailored to wireless signals. Evaluated on public benchmarks spanning nine downstream tasks, AM-FM shows strong cross-task performance with improved data efficiency, demonstrating that foundation models can enable scalable ambient intelligence using existing wireless infrastructure.

AI.tldr: AM-FM is a WiFi-based foundation model for ambient intelligence trained on 9.2M unlabeled CSI samples; uses contrastive learning, masked reconstruction, and physics-informed objectives; demonstrates cross-task data-efficient performance on nine benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address the reliance on task-specific, labeled sensing models by learning general-purpose representations from existing WiFi infrastructure to enable scalable ambient intelligence.
AI.method: Pre-train on 9.2 million unlabeled Channel State Information (CSI) samples collected over 439 days from 20 commercial device types; leverage self-supervised objectives (contrastive learning, masked reconstruction) plus physics-informed losses tailored to wireless signals; evaluate cross-task transfer on nine downstream tasks.
AI.result: Shows strong cross-task performance and improved data efficiency across nine benchmarks, indicating effective generalization and the viability of a foundation-model approach for ambient intelligence via WiFi.
AI.conclusion: Foundation models can enable scalable ambient intelligence using existing wireless infrastructure, reducing labeling needs and expanding the practical deployment of WiFi-based sensing.

title: MDE-VIO: Enhancing Visual-Inertial Odometry Using Learned Depth Priors
comment: 6 pages, 2 figures, 3 tables. Submitted to ICIP 2026
summary: Traditional monocular Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO) systems struggle in low-texture environments where sparse visual features are insufficient for accurate pose estimation. To address this, dense Monocular Depth Estimation (MDE) has been widely explored as a complementary information source. While recent Vision Transformer (ViT) based complex foundational models offer dense, geometrically consistent depth, their computational demands typically preclude them from real-time edge deployment. Our work bridges this gap by integrating learned depth priors directly into the VINS-Mono optimization backend. We propose a novel framework that enforces affine-invariant depth consistency and pairwise ordinal constraints, explicitly filtering unstable artifacts via variance-based gating. This approach strictly adheres to the computational limits of edge devices while robustly recovering metric scale. Extensive experiments on the TartanGround and M3ED datasets demonstrate that our method prevents divergence in challenging scenarios and delivers significant accuracy gains, reducing Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) by up to 28.3%. Code will be made available.

AI.tldr: Dense monocular depth priors are integrated into VINS-Mono to enhance pose estimation on edge devices, using affine-invariant depth consistency, pairwise ordinal constraints, and variance-based artifact gating; designed to fit real-time constraints while recovering metric scale, achieving up to 28.3% ATE reduction on TartanGround and M3ED.
AI.motivation: Monocular VIO struggles in low-texture scenes due to sparse visual features. Dense depth priors can provide complementary geometric information, but state-of-the-art depth models (ViT-based) are too heavy for real-time edge deployment.
AI.method: Integrate learned depth priors into the VINS-Mono optimization backend. Enforce affine-invariant depth consistency and pairwise ordinal constraints. Use variance-based gating to filter unstable depth artifacts. Ensure computational efficiency compatible with edge devices while recovering metric scale.
AI.result: Experiments on TartanGround and M3ED show improved robustness against divergence and substantial accuracy gains, with up to 28.3% reduction in Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE). Code availability planned.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a lightweight, edge-friendly depth-informed VIO framework that improves accuracy and stability without heavy models, maintaining metric scale and real-time feasibility.

title: Pushing Forward Pareto Frontiers of Proactive Agents with Behavioral Agentic Optimization
summary: Proactive large language model (LLM) agents aim to actively plan, query, and interact over multiple turns, enabling efficient task completion beyond passive instruction following and making them essential for real-world, user-centric applications. Agentic reinforcement learning (RL) has recently emerged as a promising solution for training such agents in multi-turn settings, allowing interaction strategies to be learned from feedback. However, existing pipelines face a critical challenge in balancing task performance with user engagement, as passive agents can not efficiently adapt to users' intentions while overuse of human feedback reduces their satisfaction. To address this trade-off, we propose BAO, an agentic RL framework that combines behavior enhancement to enrich proactive reasoning and information-gathering capabilities with behavior regularization to suppress inefficient or redundant interactions and align agent behavior with user expectations. We evaluate BAO on multiple tasks from the UserRL benchmark suite, and demonstrate that it substantially outperforms proactive agentic RL baselines while achieving comparable or even superior performance to commercial LLM agents, highlighting its effectiveness for training proactive, user-aligned LLM agents in complex multi-turn scenarios. Our website: https://proactive-agentic-rl.github.io/.

AI.tldr: BAO: an agentic RL framework that combines behavior enhancement and regularization to train proactive, user-aligned LLM agents, outperforming baselines and rivaling commercial agents on multi-turn tasks in UserRL.
AI.motivation: Proactive LLM agents need to balance task performance and user engagement; existing agentic RL pipelines struggle with adapting to user intent without excessive human feedback.
AI.method: BAO integrates (1) behavior enhancement to enrich proactive reasoning and information-gathering, and (2) behavior regularization to suppress inefficient or redundant interactions and align with user expectations; evaluated on UserRL benchmark.
AI.result: BAO substantially outperforms proactive agentic RL baselines and achieves comparable or better performance than commercial LLM agents on multi-turn UserRL tasks.
AI.conclusion: BAO is effective for training proactive, user-aligned LLM agents capable of complex multi-turn interactions; shows trade-off management between task performance and user satisfaction.

title: ABot-N0: Technical Report on the VLA Foundation Model for Versatile Embodied Navigation
summary:
Embodied navigation has long been fragmented by task-specific architectures. We introduce ABot-N0, a unified Vision-Language-Action (VLA) foundation model that achieves a ``Grand Unification'' across 5 core tasks: Point-Goal, Object-Goal, Instruction-Following, POI-Goal, and Person-Following. ABot-N0 utilizes a hierarchical ``Brain-Action'' architecture, pairing an LLM-based Cognitive Brain for semantic reasoning with a Flow Matching-based Action Expert for precise, continuous trajectory generation.
  To support large-scale learning, we developed the ABot-N0 Data Engine, curating 16.9M expert trajectories and 5.0M reasoning samples across 7,802 high-fidelity 3D scenes (10.7 $\text{km}^2$). ABot-N0 achieves new SOTA performance across 7 benchmarks, significantly outperforming specialized models. Furthermore, our Agentic Navigation System integrates a planner with hierarchical topological memory, enabling robust, long-horizon missions in dynamic real-world environments.

AI.tldr: ABot-N0 is a Vision-Language-Action foundation model that unifies Point-Goal, Object-Goal, Instruction-Following, POI-Goal, and Person-Following via a Brain-Action architecture; trained on 16.9M trajectories and 5.0M reasoning samples across 7,802 scenes; achieves SOTA on 7 benchmarks and supports long-horizon tasks through a planner and hierarchical memory.
AI.motivation: Fragmentation in embodied navigation architectures; need for a unified model that can handle multiple navigation tasks with scalable learning signals.
AI.method: Hierarchical architecture with an LLM-based Cognitive Brain for semantic reasoning and a Flow Matching-based Action Expert for precise trajectory generation; ABot-N0 Data Engine curating 16.9M expert trajectories and 5.0M reasoning samples across 7,802 high-fidelity 3D scenes; Agentic Navigation System integrating a planner with hierarchical topological memory for long-horizon missions.
AI.result: Achieves new SOTA across 7 benchmarks, significantly outperforming specialized models; robust long-horizon performance in dynamic real-world environments.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a unified VLA foundation model for embodied navigation with large-scale data and integrated planning-memory components, enabling broad task coverage and robust real-world capabilities.

title: Zero-Sacrifice Persistent-Robustness Adversarial Defense for Pre-Trained Encoders
summary: The widespread use of publicly available pre-trained encoders from self-supervised learning (SSL) has exposed a critical vulnerability: their susceptibility to downstream-agnostic adversarial examples (DAEs), which are crafted without knowledge of the downstream tasks but capable of misleading downstream models. While several defense methods have been explored recently, they rely primarily on task-specific adversarial fine-tuning, which inevitably limits generalizability and causes catastrophic forgetting and deteriorates benign performance. Different with previous works, we propose a more rigorous defense goal that requires only a single tuning for diverse downstream tasks to defend against DAEs and preserve benign performance. To achieve this defense goal, we introduce Zero-Sacrifice Persistent-Robustness Adversarial Defense (ZePAD), which is inspired by the inherent sensitivity of neural networks to data characteristics. Specifically, ZePAD is a dual-branch structure, which consists of a Multi-Pattern Adversarial Enhancement Branch (MPAE-Branch) that uses two adversarially fine-tuned encoders to strengthen adversarial resistance. The Benign Memory Preservation Branch (BMP-Branch) is trained on local data to ensure adversarial robustness does not compromise benign performance. Surprisingly, we find that ZePAD can directly detect DAEs by evaluating branch confidence, without introducing any adversarial exsample identification task during training. Notably, by enriching feature diversity, our method enables a single adversarial fine-tuning to defend against DAEs across downstream tasks, thereby achieving persistent robustness. Extensive experiments on 11 SSL methods and 6 datasets validate its effectiveness. In certain cases, it achieves a 29.20% improvement in benign performance and a 73.86% gain in adversarial robustness, highlighting its zero-sacrifice property.

AI.tldr: ZePAD introduces a dual-branch defense against downstream-agnostic adversarial examples for self-supervised learning encoders. It uses a Multi-Pattern Adversarial Enhancement Branch (dual adversarially fine-tuned encoders) and a Benign Memory Preservation Branch (local data) to achieve robust performance with a single tuning across multiple downstream tasks. It detects DAEs via branch confidence, aiming for zero-sacrifice robustness and persistent defense across 11 SSL methods and 6 datasets, reporting notable gains in benign and adversarial robustness.
AI.motivation: Publicly available SSL encoders are vulnerable to DAEs crafted without task knowledge, and existing defenses rely on task-specific adversarial fine-tuning, causing poor generalization and forgetting. A defense that requires only one tuning and preserves benign accuracy across diverse downstream tasks is highly desirable.
AI.method: ZePAD is a dual-branch architecture. The MPAE-Branch uses two adversarially fine-tuned encoders to diversify adversarial resistance. The BMP-Branch is trained on local benign data to preserve benign performance. DAEs are detected via branch confidence without explicit adversarial-sample identification tasks during training. The framework purportedly enables a single-tuning defense across downstream tasks by enriching feature diversity.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on 11 SSL methods and 6 datasets show effectiveness; claims include up to 29.20% improvement in benign performance and up to 73.86% gain in adversarial robustness, illustrating the zero-sacrifice property and persistent robustness across tasks.
AI.conclusion: ZePAD proposes a practically appealing defense against DAEs for SSL encoders by combining diversified adversarial exposure with benign-precision preservation and intrinsic DAE detection, achieving persistent robustness with a single tuning across multiple downstream tasks.

title: Exploring Real-Time Super-Resolution: Benchmarking and Fine-Tuning for Streaming Content
summary:
Recent advancements in real-time super-resolution have enabled higher-quality video streaming, yet existing methods struggle with the unique challenges of compressed video content. Commonly used datasets do not accurately reflect the characteristics of streaming media, limiting the relevance of current benchmarks. To address this gap, we introduce a comprehensive dataset - StreamSR - sourced from YouTube, covering a wide range of video genres and resolutions representative of real-world streaming scenarios. We benchmark 11 state-of-the-art real-time super-resolution models to evaluate their performance for the streaming use-case.
  Furthermore, we propose EfRLFN, an efficient real-time model that integrates Efficient Channel Attention and a hyperbolic tangent activation function - a novel design choice in the context of real-time super-resolution. We extensively optimized the architecture to maximize efficiency and designed a composite loss function that improves training convergence. EfRLFN combines the strengths of existing architectures while improving both visual quality and runtime performance.
  Finally, we show that fine-tuning other models on our dataset results in significant performance gains that generalize well across various standard benchmarks. We made the dataset, the code, and the benchmark available at https://github.com/EvgeneyBogatyrev/EfRLFN.

AI.tldr: A dataset-and-model paper proposing StreamSR for real-time SR on compressed streaming video, introducing EfRLFN (efficient real-time SR with Efficient Channel Attention and tanh) and showing fine-tuning benefits; releases code and benchmark.
AI.motivation: Address the mismatch between real-world streaming content and existing SR benchmarks; provide a realistic dataset and comprehensive benchmarking to guide real-time SR research.
AI.method: Construct StreamSR from YouTube samples across genres and resolutions; benchmark 11 state-of-the-art real-time SR models; propose EfRLFN with Efficient Channel Attention and tanh activation; optimize architecture for speed; design a composite loss for better training convergence; evaluate fine-tuning benefits on standard benchmarks; release dataset, code, and benchmark.
AI.result: StreamSR dataset and a comprehensive benchmark for real-time SR on streaming content; EfRLFN delivering improved visual quality and runtime efficiency; demonstrated gains from fine-tuning other models on StreamSR that generalize across benchmarks; dataset and code publicly released at the provided link.
AI.conclusion: StreamSR provides a realistic benchmark for streaming SR; EfRLFN offers an efficient real-time SR solution with novel activation design; fine-tuning on the dataset can enhance performance across benchmarks, promoting reproducibility and broader generalization.

title: ReplicatorBench: Benchmarking LLM Agents for Replicability in Social and Behavioral Sciences
summary: The literature has witnessed an emerging interest in AI agents for automated assessment of scientific papers. Existing benchmarks focus primarily on the computational aspect of this task, testing agents' ability to reproduce or replicate research outcomes when having access to the code and data. This setting, while foundational, (1) fails to capture the inconsistent availability of new data for replication as opposed to reproduction, and (2) lacks ground-truth diversity by focusing only on reproducible papers, thereby failing to evaluate an agent's ability to identify non-replicable research. Furthermore, most benchmarks only evaluate outcomes rather than the replication process. In response, we introduce ReplicatorBench, an end-to-end benchmark, including human-verified replicable and non-replicable research claims in social and behavioral sciences for evaluating AI agents in research replication across three stages: (1) extraction and retrieval of replication data; (2) design and execution of computational experiments; and (3) interpretation of results, allowing a test of AI agents' capability to mimic the activities of human replicators in real world. To set a baseline of AI agents' capability, we develop ReplicatorAgent, an agentic framework equipped with necessary tools, like web search and iterative interaction with sandboxed environments, to accomplish tasks in ReplicatorBench. We evaluate ReplicatorAgent across four underlying large language models (LLMs), as well as different design choices of programming language and levels of code access. Our findings reveal that while current LLM agents are capable of effectively designing and executing computational experiments, they struggle with retrieving resources, such as new data, necessary to replicate a claim. All code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/CenterForOpenScience/llm-benchmarking.

AI.tldr: ReplicatorBench is proposed as an end-to-end benchmark for AI agents to mimic human replication activities in social/behavioral sciences, including replicable and non-replicable claims across three stages. ReplicatorAgent, tested with four LLMs and varying code access, excels at designing/executing experiments but struggles to retrieve new data. The work provides a baseline and publicly available resources to advance AI-assisted research replication.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks focus on reproducing results given data/code and overlook the variability of data availability and non-replicable claims, as well as the replication process itself. There is a need to evaluate agents on extraction, design, execution, and interpretation of replication tasks in real-world settings.
AI.method: Introduce ReplicatorBench with human-verified replicable and non-replicable claims in social/behavioral sciences. Evaluate AI agents across three stages: data extraction/retrieval, design/execution of computational experiments, and interpretation. Develop ReplicatorAgent, an agentic framework with tools (web search, sandboxed execution) and test across four LLMs and coding choices. Public code/data provided.
AI.result: Agents can effectively design and run computational experiments but have difficulty retrieving new data/resources required to replicate a claim, indicating a bottleneck in data acquisition within replication workflows.
AI.conclusion: ReplicatorBench fills a gap by covering replication processes and non-replicable claims, establishing a baseline with ReplicatorAgent, and providing public resources to advance AI-assisted research replication.

title: ViTaS: Visual Tactile Soft Fusion Contrastive Learning for Visuomotor Learning
summary: Tactile information plays a crucial role in human manipulation tasks and has recently garnered increasing attention in robotic manipulation. However, existing approaches mostly focus on the alignment of visual and tactile features and the integration mechanism tends to be direct concatenation. Consequently, they struggle to effectively cope with occluded scenarios due to neglecting the inherent complementary nature of both modalities and the alignment may not be exploited enough, limiting the potential of their real-world deployment. In this paper, we present ViTaS, a simple yet effective framework that incorporates both visual and tactile information to guide the behavior of an agent. We introduce Soft Fusion Contrastive Learning, an advanced version of conventional contrastive learning method and a CVAE module to utilize the alignment and complementarity within visuo-tactile representations. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in 12 simulated and 3 real-world environments, and our experiments show that ViTaS significantly outperforms existing baselines. Project page: https://skyrainwind.github.io/ViTaS/index.html.

AI.tldr: ViTaS is a simple framework that fuses visual and tactile information using Soft Fusion Contrastive Learning and a CVAE to exploit alignment and complementarity, improving manipulation performance, including in occluded scenarios, across 12 simulated and 3 real-world environments.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of current visuo-tactile fusion methods that rely on direct feature alignment/concatenation and struggle with occlusions; leverage the complementary nature of vision and touch to improve robustness in manipulation tasks.
AI.method: ViTaS incorporates Soft Fusion Contrastive Learning to better align and fuse visuo-tactile representations and uses a CVAE module to capture the alignment and complementarity between modalities.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows ViTaS significantly outperforms existing baselines across 12 simulated and 3 real-world environments; project page provided.
AI.conclusion: ViTaS offers a simple yet effective approach for visuo-tactile integration, exploiting both alignment and complementarity to enhance manipulation performance, with strong robustness to occlusions and good real-world transfer.

title: UltraLIF: Fully Differentiable Spiking Neural Networks via Ultradiscretization and Max-Plus Algebra
summary: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) offer energy-efficient, biologically plausible computation but suffer from non-differentiable spike generation, necessitating reliance on heuristic surrogate gradients. This paper introduces UltraLIF, a principled framework that replaces surrogate gradients with ultradiscretization, a mathematical formalism from tropical geometry providing continuous relaxations of discrete dynamics. The central insight is that the max-plus semiring underlying ultradiscretization naturally models neural threshold dynamics: the log-sum-exp function serves as a differentiable soft-maximum that converges to hard thresholding as a learnable temperature parameter $\eps \to 0$. Two neuron models are derived from distinct dynamical systems: UltraLIF from the LIF ordinary differential equation (temporal dynamics) and UltraDLIF from the diffusion equation modeling gap junction coupling across neuronal populations (spatial dynamics). Both yield fully differentiable SNNs trainable via standard backpropagation with no forward-backward mismatch. Theoretical analysis establishes pointwise convergence to classical LIF dynamics with quantitative error bounds and bounded non-vanishing gradients. Experiments on six benchmarks spanning static images, neuromorphic vision, and audio demonstrate improvements over surrogate gradient baselines, with gains most pronounced in single-timestep ($T{=}1$) settings on neuromorphic and temporal datasets. An optional sparsity penalty enables significant energy reduction while maintaining competitive accuracy.

AI.tldr: UltraLIF/UltraDLIF use ultradiscretization to replace surrogate gradients, yielding differentiable SNNs with convergence guarantees and energy-saving sparsity; achieves improvements over baselines across six benchmarks, especially at single-timestep (T=1).
AI.motivation: Surrogate gradient methods are heuristic and can cause forward–backward mismatch due to non-differentiable spike generation; a principled continuous relaxation that preserves threshold dynamics is desired.
AI.method: Derive two ultradiscretized models: UltraLIF from the LIF ODE (temporal dynamics) and UltraDLIF from a diffusion-based spatial model of gap junction coupling. Use the max-plus semiring via ultradiscretization; the log-sum-exp acts as a differentiable soft max with a learnable temperature ε, converging to hard thresholding as ε→0. Provide convergence guarantees to classical LIF behavior with bounded gradients; trainable with standard backpropagation. Include an optional sparsity penalty for energy efficiency.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on six benchmarks spanning static images, neuromorphic vision, and audio shows improvements over surrogate-gradient baselines; gains are most pronounced in single-timestep (T=1) settings on neuromorphic and temporal data. Sparsity penalty enables substantial energy reductions while preserving accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Ultradiscretization offers a principled, differentiable alternative to surrogate gradients for SNNs, with theoretical convergence and practical performance/energy benefits, suggesting a viable path toward forward–backward-consistent training of SNNs.

title: ArtContext: Contextualizing Artworks with Open-Access Art History Articles and Wikidata Knowledge through a LoRA-Tuned CLIP Model
summary: Many Art History articles discuss artworks in general as well as specific parts of works, such as layout, iconography, or material culture. However, when viewing an artwork, it is not trivial to identify what different articles have said about the piece. Therefore, we propose ArtContext, a pipeline for taking a corpus of Open-Access Art History articles and Wikidata Knowledge and annotating Artworks with this information. We do this using a novel corpus collection pipeline, then learn a bespoke CLIP model adapted using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to make it domain-specific. We show that the new model, PaintingCLIP, which is weakly supervised by the collected corpus, outperforms CLIP and provides context for a given artwork. The proposed pipeline is generalisable and can be readily applied to numerous humanities areas.

AI.tldr: A domain-adapted CLIP model (PaintingCLIP) trained with LoRA on Open-Access Art History texts and Wikidata enhances artwork annotation by leveraging contextual knowledge, outperforming standard CLIP.
AI.motivation: Art History discourse is dispersed across articles; practitioners struggle to aggregate what has been said about a work. A pipeline that combines literature and knowledge graphs can provide contextualized annotations.
AI.method: Develop ArtContext pipeline: collect OA Art History articles and Wikidata; annotate artworks; train a domain-specific CLIP via Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) using weak supervision from curated corpus.
AI.result: PaintingCLIP outperforms CLIP on artwork-context annotation tasks and yields contextual annotations for artworks.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline is generalizable to other humanities domains and can be readily applied to different corpora and knowledge bases.

title: Causal-JEPA: Learning World Models through Object-Level Latent Interventions
summary: World models require robust relational understanding to support prediction, reasoning, and control. While object-centric representations provide a useful abstraction, they are not sufficient to capture interaction-dependent dynamics. We therefore propose C-JEPA, a simple and flexible object-centric world model that extends masked joint embedding prediction from image patches to object-centric representations. By applying object-level masking that requires an object's state to be inferred from other objects, C-JEPA induces latent interventions with counterfactual-like effects and prevents shortcut solutions, making interaction reasoning essential. Empirically, C-JEPA leads to consistent gains in visual question answering, with an absolute improvement of about 20\% in counterfactual reasoning compared to the same architecture without object-level masking. On agent control tasks, C-JEPA enables substantially more efficient planning by using only 1\% of the total latent input features required by patch-based world models, while achieving comparable performance. Finally, we provide a formal analysis demonstrating that object-level masking induces a causal inductive bias via latent interventions. Our code is available at https://github.com/galilai-group/cjepa.

AI.tldr: C-JEPA introduces object-level masking in an object-centric world model to enforce interaction-focused learning, yielding better counterfactual reasoning and planning efficiency.
AI.motivation: Object-centric representations alone miss interaction-dependent dynamics; robust relational understanding is needed for prediction, reasoning, and control.
AI.method: Extend masked joint embedding prediction from patches to object-centric representations; apply object-level masking requiring inference of an object's state from others; induce latent interventions and counterfactual-like effects; provide formal causal analysis; open-source code.
AI.result: Improved visual QA counterfactual reasoning by ~20 percentage points over architecture without object-level masking; agent control tasks show planning efficiency with only 1% of latent features versus patch-based models; comparable performance.
AI.conclusion: Object-level masking provides a causal inductive bias through latent interventions, improving interaction reasoning; method is simple and flexible; code released.

title: Human-Like Gaze Behavior in Social Robots: A Deep Learning Approach Integrating Human and Non-Human Stimuli
summary: Nonverbal behaviors, particularly gaze direction, play a crucial role in enhancing effective communication in social interactions. As social robots increasingly participate in these interactions, they must adapt their gaze based on human activities and remain receptive to all cues, whether human-generated or not, to ensure seamless and effective communication. This study aims to increase the similarity between robot and human gaze behavior across various social situations, including both human and non-human stimuli (e.g., conversations, pointing, door openings, and object drops). A key innovation in this study, is the investigation of gaze responses to non-human stimuli, a critical yet underexplored area in prior research. These scenarios, were simulated in the Unity software as a 3D animation and a 360-degree real-world video. Data on gaze directions from 41 participants were collected via virtual reality (VR) glasses. Preprocessed data, trained two neural networks-LSTM and Transformer-to build predictive models based on individuals' gaze patterns. In the animated scenario, the LSTM and Transformer models achieved prediction accuracies of 67.6% and 70.4%, respectively; In the real-world scenario, the LSTM and Transformer models achieved accuracies of 72% and 71.6%, respectively. Despite the gaze pattern differences among individuals, our models outperform existing approaches in accuracy while uniquely considering non-human stimuli, offering a significant advantage over previous literature. Furthermore, deployed on the NAO robot, the system was evaluated by 275 participants via a comprehensive questionnaire, with results demonstrating high satisfaction during interactions. This work advances social robotics by enabling robots to dynamically mimic human gaze behavior in complex social contexts.

AI.tldr: Neural models (LSTM, Transformer) predict human-like gaze in both human and non-human stimuli; accuracies ~67.6–72%; validated on NAO with high user satisfaction, enabling dynamic gaze mimicry in social contexts.
AI.motivation: Gaze direction is critical for social communication; robots must adapt to human cues and also respond to non-human stimuli to maintain natural interaction, addressing a gap in prior work.
AI.method: Gaze data from 41 participants collected via VR while they viewed scenarios simulated in Unity (3D animation) and 360° video. Preprocessed data used to train LSTM and Transformer models to predict gaze based on individual patterns. Scenarios include conversations, pointing, door openings, and object drops. Real-world deployment on the NAO robot with 275 participants and a questionnaire to assess interaction satisfaction.
AI.result: In animation: LSTM 67.6%, Transformer 70.4%. In real-world: LSTM 72%, Transformer 71.6%. Models outperform existing approaches and handle non-human stimuli. NAO deployment yielded high satisfaction across participants (275).
AI.conclusion: The study advances social robotics by enabling robots to dynamically mimic human gaze across diverse social contexts, including non-human stimuli, and demonstrates robust predictive models suitable for real-time gaze adaptation.

title: Adaptive Physics Transformer with Fused Global-Local Attention for Subsurface Energy Systems
summary: The Earth's subsurface is a cornerstone of modern society, providing essential energy resources like hydrocarbons, geothermal, and minerals while serving as the primary reservoir for $CO_2$ sequestration. However, full physics numerical simulations of these systems are notoriously computationally expensive due to geological heterogeneity, high resolution requirements, and the tight coupling of physical processes with distinct propagation time scales. Here we propose the \textbf{Adaptive Physics Transformer} (APT), a geometry-, mesh-, and physics-agnostic neural operator that explicitly addresses these challenges. APT fuses a graph-based encoder to extract high-resolution local heterogeneous features with a global attention mechanism to resolve long-range physical impacts. Our results demonstrate that APT outperforms state-of-the-art architectures in subsurface tasks across both regular and irregular grids with robust super-resolution capabilities. Notably, APT is the first architecture that directly learns from adaptive mesh refinement simulations. We also demonstrate APT's capability for cross-dataset learning, positioning it as a robust and scalable backbone for large-scale subsurface foundation model development.

AI.tldr: Introduces Adaptive Physics Transformer (APT), a geometry-, mesh-, and physics-agnostic neural operator for subsurface simulations that combines a graph-based encoder with global attention to handle high-resolution heterogeneity and adaptive mesh refinement, achieving superior accuracy and transferability across grids and datasets.
AI.motivation: Full-physics subsurface simulations are computationally expensive due to geological heterogeneity, high-resolution requirements, and tight coupling across processes and scales. There is a need for efficient, scalable surrogate models that generalize across regular/irregular grids and adapt mesh refinement.
AI.method: APT uses a graph-based encoder to extract high-resolution local heterogeneous features and a global attention mechanism to capture long-range physical interactions. It is designed to be geometry-, mesh-, and physics-agnostic and is reported to be the first architecture to learn directly from adaptive mesh refinement (AMR) simulations; it also supports cross-dataset learning.
AI.result: The method outperforms state-of-the-art architectures on subsurface tasks for both regular and irregular grids and exhibits robust super-resolution capabilities. It demonstrates capability for cross-dataset learning and is positioned as a robust, scalable backbone for large-scale subsurface foundation model development.
AI.conclusion: APT provides a versatile, efficient neural operator for subsurface modeling, enabling cross-domain transfer and compatibility with varying mesh configurations. It has potential as a foundation-model backbone for subsurface science and engineering, including AMR-informed simulations.

title: Latent Forcing: Reordering the Diffusion Trajectory for Pixel-Space Image Generation
summary: Latent diffusion models excel at generating high-quality images but lose the benefits of end-to-end modeling. They discard information during image encoding, require a separately trained decoder, and model an auxiliary distribution to the raw data. In this paper, we propose Latent Forcing, a simple modification to existing architectures that achieves the efficiency of latent diffusion while operating on raw natural images. Our approach orders the denoising trajectory by jointly processing latents and pixels with separately tuned noise schedules. This allows the latents to act as a scratchpad for intermediate computation before high-frequency pixel features are generated. We find that the order of conditioning signals is critical, and we analyze this to explain differences between REPA distillation in the tokenizer and the diffusion model, conditional versus unconditional generation, and how tokenizer reconstruction quality relates to diffusability. Applied to ImageNet, Latent Forcing achieves a new state-of-the-art for diffusion transformer-based pixel generation at our compute scale.

AI.tldr: Latent Forcing enables end-to-end diffusion on raw images by jointly processing latents and pixels with tuned noise schedules, preserving latent diffusion efficiency and achieving SOTA results on ImageNet with diffusion transformer-based pixel generation.
AI.motivation: Latent diffusion models are efficient but lose end-to-end modeling by encoding images and relying on separate decoders and auxiliary distributions to raw data. This work aims to combine the efficiency of latent diffusion with end-to-end raw-image modeling.
AI.method: Introduce Latent Forcing: a simple architectural modification that orders the denoising trajectory by jointly processing latents and pixels, using independently tuned noise schedules. Latents act as an intermediate scratchpad for computation before high-frequency pixel details are produced. The approach emphasizes the importance of the conditioning signal order and includes analysis comparing tokenizer-based REPA distillation with diffusion models and exploring conditional vs unconditional generation.
AI.result: On ImageNet, Latent Forcing achieves a new state-of-the-art for diffusion transformer-based pixel generation at the given compute scale.
AI.conclusion: Latent Forcing demonstrates that end-to-end latent-pixel diffusion can match latent-diffusion efficiency while operating on raw images, highlighting the critical role of conditioning order and providing insights into tokenizer vs diffusion dynamics and diffusability. The method yields SOTA performance at large scale.

title: GHOST: Unmasking Phantom States in Mamba2 via Grouped Hidden-state Output-aware Selection & Truncation
summary: While Mamba2's expanded state dimension enhances temporal modeling, it incurs substantial inference overhead that saturates bandwidth during autoregressive generation. Standard pruning methods fail to address this bottleneck: unstructured sparsity leaves activations dense, magnitude-based selection ignores runtime dynamics, and gradient-based methods impose prohibitive costs. We introduce GHOST (Grouped Hidden-state Output-aware Selection and Truncation), a structured pruning framework that approximates control-theoretic balanced truncation using only forward-pass statistics. By jointly measuring controllability and observability, GHOST rivals the fidelity of gradient-based methods without requiring backpropagation. As a highlight, on models ranging from 130M to 2.7B parameters, our approach achieves a 50\% state-dimension reduction with approximately 1 perplexity point increase on WikiText-2. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/mamba2_ghost-7BCB/.

AI.tldr: GHOST is a structured pruning method for Mamba2 that halves hidden-state dimensionality with about 1 perplexity point cost, using forward-pass controllability/observability to approximate balanced truncation without backpropagation.
AI.motivation: Reduce autoregressive inference overhead for large Mamba2 language models by compressing hidden-state dimensions without incurring backpropagation costs, and without relying on unstructured sparsity or gradient-based pruning.
AI.method: Grouped Hidden-state Output-aware Selection and Truncation (GHOST)—a structured pruning framework that uses forward-pass statistics to jointly measure controllability and observability and approximate balanced truncation, avoiding backpropagation.
AI.result: Achieves ~50% state-dimension reduction on models from 130M to 2.7B parameters with ~1 perplexity point increase on WikiText-2; maintains fidelity close to gradient-based methods; code available.
AI.conclusion: GHOST provides an effective, backpropagation-free pruning approach that addresses the bottleneck of bandwidth and inference in autoregressive models by leveraging forward-pass statistics to obtain substantial dimensionality reductions with modest accuracy loss.

title: AC-MASAC: An Attentive Curriculum Learning Framework for Heterogeneous UAV Swarm Coordination
summary: Cooperative path planning for heterogeneous UAV swarms poses significant challenges for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL), particularly in handling asymmetric inter-agent dependencies and addressing the risks of sparse rewards and catastrophic forgetting during training. To address these issues, this paper proposes an attentive curriculum learning framework (AC-MASAC). The framework introduces a role-aware heterogeneous attention mechanism to explicitly model asymmetric dependencies. Moreover, a structured curriculum strategy is designed, integrating hierarchical knowledge transfer and stage-proportional experience replay to address the issues of sparse rewards and catastrophic forgetting. The proposed framework is validated on a custom multi-agent simulation platform, and the results show that our method has significant advantages over other advanced methods in terms of Success Rate, Formation Keeping Rate, and Success-weighted Mission Time. The code is available at \textcolor{red}{https://github.com/Wanhao-Liu/AC-MASAC}.

AI.tldr: AC-MASAC: an attentive curriculum learning framework for heterogeneous UAV swarms in MARL that uses a role-aware attention mechanism to model asymmetric inter-agent dependencies and a structured curriculum with hierarchical knowledge transfer and stage-proportional experience replay to mitigate sparse rewards and catastrophic forgetting. Validated on a custom simulation platform with favorable metrics; code released.
AI.motivation: Address key MARL challenges in heterogeneous UAV swarms: asymmetric inter-agent dependencies, sparse rewards, and catastrophic forgetting during training.
AI.method: Introduce AC-MASAC with (1) a role-aware heterogeneous attention mechanism to capture asymmetric dependencies among agents, and (2) a structured curriculum strategy combining hierarchical knowledge transfer and stage-proportional experience replay to alleviate sparse rewards and forgetting.
AI.result: Empirical results show significant advantages over advanced baselines in Success Rate, Formation Keeping Rate, and Success-weighted Mission Time on a custom simulation platform; code available at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses asymmetric dependencies and training challenges in MARL for heterogeneous UAV swarms, outperforming baselines and providing a path toward more robust cooperative path planning.

title: Towards Compressive and Scalable Recurrent Memory
summary: Transformers face a quadratic bottleneck in attention when scaling to long contexts. Recent approaches introduce recurrent memory to extend context beyond the current window, yet these often face a fundamental trade-off between theoretical principles and practical scalability. To address this, we introduce Elastic Memory, a novel memory architecture grounded in the HiPPO framework for online function approximation. Elastic Memory treats historical sequence as samples from continuous signals, applying optimal online compression to encode them into a fixed-size memory state. For retrieval, we propose a flexible \textit{polynomial sampling} mechanism that reconstructs a history summary from this compressed state. Elastic Memory consistently outperformed baselines on long-context (32k+) datasets across three domains. With equal parameters, it beat Memorizing Transformer by 16x memory and outperformed Melodi at all memory sizes, even when Melodi had 30% more parameters. When scaling model size, Elastic Memory stayed ahead of all baselines and was significantly faster than Melodi at 4x size. Furthermore, its decoupled design allows for injecting inductive biases at test-time to boost performance.

AI.tldr: Elastic Memory is a HiPPO-based fixed-size online memory for long-context transformers, paired with a polynomial sampling retrieval, achieving strong improvements over baselines (Memorizing Transformer, Melodi) on 32k+ contexts, with better scalability and speed, plus test-time inductive-bias flexibility.
AI.motivation: Address the quadratic attention bottleneck in transformers when handling very long contexts, where recurrent memory methods often sacrifice theoretical grounding or scalability. A principled, scalable memory architecture is needed.
AI.method: Proposes Elastic Memory, a memory architecture grounded in the HiPPO framework for online function approximation. It treats historical sequences as samples from continuous signals and applies optimal online compression to encode them into a fixed-size memory state. For retrieval, it uses a flexible polynomial sampling mechanism to reconstruct a history summary from the compressed state.
AI.result: Elastic Memory consistently outperforms baselines on long-context (32k+) datasets across three domains. With equal parameters, it beats Memorizing Transformer by 16x memory and outperforms Melodi at all memory sizes (Melodi with 30% more parameters). As model size grows, Elastic Memory remains ahead of all baselines and is significantly faster than Melodi at 4x size.
AI.conclusion: Elastic Memory offers a principled, scalable long-context memory solution with a decoupled design that allows injecting inductive biases at test-time to boost performance, achieving strong results across domains and scales.

title: Fighting MRI Anisotropy: Learning Multiple Cardiac Shapes From a Single Implicit Neural Representation
summary: The anisotropic nature of short-axis (SAX) cardiovascular magnetic resonance imaging (CMRI) limits cardiac shape analysis. To address this, we propose to leverage near-isotropic, higher resolution computed tomography angiography (CTA) data of the heart. We use this data to train a single neural implicit function to jointly represent cardiac shapes from CMRI at any resolution. We evaluate the method for the reconstruction of right ventricle (RV) and myocardium (MYO), where MYO simultaneously models endocardial and epicardial left-ventricle surfaces. Since high-resolution SAX reference segmentations are unavailable, we evaluate performance by extracting a 4-chamber (4CH) slice of RV and MYO from their reconstructed shapes. When compared with the reference 4CH segmentation masks from CMRI, our method achieved a Dice similarity coefficient of 0.91 $\pm$ 0.07 and 0.75 $\pm$ 0.13, and a Hausdorff distance of 6.21 $\pm$ 3.97 mm and 7.53 $\pm$ 5.13 mm for RV and MYO, respectively. Quantitative and qualitative assessment demonstrate the model's ability to reconstruct accurate, smooth and anatomically plausible shapes, supporting improvements in cardiac shape analysis.

AI.tldr: Cross-modality single neural implicit function trained on CTA to reconstruct SAX CMRI cardiac shapes at arbitrary resolution; evaluated on RV and MYO, using 4-chamber slices for assessment.
AI.motivation: Address anisotropic SAX CMRI limitations in cardiac shape analysis by leveraging near-isotropic, high-resolution CTA data.
AI.method: Train a single neural implicit representation to jointly model cardiac shapes from CMRI (any resolution) using CTA-derived shapes as training data; reconstruct RV and myocardium (MYO) surfaces, with MYO modeling both endocardial and epicardial LV surfaces; evaluation uses 4-chamber slices extracted from predicted shapes.
AI.result: Dice: RV 0.91 ± 0.07; MYO 0.75 ± 0.13. Hausdorff distance: RV 6.21 ± 3.97 mm; MYO 7.53 ± 5.13 mm. 4CH-based evaluation shows accurate, smooth, anatomically plausible reconstructions.
AI.conclusion: The method can produce accurate, smooth, and plausible cardiac shapes from CMRI by leveraging high-quality CTA data, potentially improving cardiac shape analysis.

title: TRACER: Trajectory Risk Aggregation for Critical Episodes in Agentic Reasoning
summary: Estimating uncertainty for AI agents in real-world multi-turn tool-using interaction with humans is difficult because failures are often triggered by sparse critical episodes (e.g., looping, incoherent tool use, or user-agent miscoordination) even when local generation appears confident. Existing uncertainty proxies focus on single-shot text generation and therefore miss these trajectory-level breakdown signals. We introduce TRACER, a trajectory-level uncertainty metric for dual-control Tool-Agent-User interaction. TRACER combines content-aware surprisal with situational-awareness signals, semantic and lexical repetition, and tool-grounded coherence gaps, and aggregates them using a tail-focused risk functional with a MAX-composite step risk to surface decisive anomalies. We evaluate TRACER on $τ^2$-bench by predicting task failure and selective task execution. To this end, TRACER improves AUROC by up to 37.1% and AUARC by up to 55% over baselines, enabling earlier and more accurate detection of uncertainty in complex conversational tool-use settings. Our code and benchmark are available at https://github.com/sinatayebati/agent-tracer.

AI.tldr: TRACER introduces a trajectory-level uncertainty metric for dual-control Tool-Agent-User interactions, combining content-aware surprisal, situational signals, repetition patterns, and tool-grounded coherence gaps with a tail-risk aggregation (MAX composite step risk) to detect decisive anomalies. Evaluated on τ^2-bench, it improves AUROC by up to 37.1% and AUARC by up to 55% over baselines, enabling earlier/accurate uncertainty detection; code available at GitHub.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty estimation for AI agents in real-world, multi-turn, tool-using interactions with humans is challenging because failures often arise from rare trajectory-level breakdowns, not captured by single-shot generation proxies.
AI.method: TRACER computes a trajectory-level uncertainty score by fusing content-aware surprisal, situational-awareness signals, semantic and lexical repetition, and tool-grounded coherence gaps. These signals are aggregated using a tail-focused risk functional with a MAX-composite step risk to highlight decisive anomalies across a trajectory.
AI.result: On the τ^2-bench, TRACER achieves up to 37.1% improvement in AUROC and up to 55% improvement in AUARC relative to baselines for predicting task failure and selective task execution.
AI.conclusion: TRACER enables earlier and more accurate detection of uncertainty in complex conversational tool-use scenarios; code and benchmark are available at the authors’ GitHub repository.

title: HAIC: Humanoid Agile Object Interaction Control via Dynamics-Aware World Model
summary: Humanoid robots show promise for complex whole-body tasks in unstructured environments. Although Human-Object Interaction (HOI) has advanced, most methods focus on fully actuated objects rigidly coupled to the robot, ignoring underactuated objects with independent dynamics and non-holonomic constraints. These introduce control challenges from coupling forces and occlusions. We present HAIC, a unified framework for robust interaction across diverse object dynamics without external state estimation. Our key contribution is a dynamics predictor that estimates high-order object states (velocity, acceleration) solely from proprioceptive history. These predictions are projected onto static geometric priors to form a spatially grounded dynamic occupancy map, enabling the policy to infer collision boundaries and contact affordances in blind spots. We use asymmetric fine-tuning, where a world model continuously adapts to the student policy's exploration, ensuring robust state estimation under distribution shifts. Experiments on a humanoid robot show HAIC achieves high success rates in agile tasks (skateboarding, cart pushing/pulling under various loads) by proactively compensating for inertial perturbations, and also masters multi-object long-horizon tasks like carrying a box across varied terrain by predicting the dynamics of multiple objects.

AI.tldr: HAIC introduces a dynamics predictor from proprioceptive history to enable robust, end-to-end humanoid interaction with underactuated and non-holonomic objects without external state estimation.
AI.motivation: Address control challenges arising when interacting with underactuated objects having independent dynamics and non-holonomic constraints, which cause coupling forces and occlusions and are not handled by fully actuated-object approaches.
AI.method: A unified HAIC framework comprising a high-order dynamics predictor that estimates velocity/acceleration from proprioceptive history, projection of predictions onto static geometric priors to form a spatial dynamic occupancy map, and asymmetric fine-tuning where the world model adapts to the student policy. This enables learning a policy that infers collision boundaries and contact affordances in blind spots.
AI.result: Empirical demonstrations on a humanoid robot show high success rates in agile tasks (skateboarding, cart pushing/pulling with varying loads) and mastery of multi-object long-horizon tasks (carrying a box across varied terrain) by proactively compensating inertial perturbations and predicting dynamics of multiple objects.
AI.conclusion: HAIC provides a unified framework for robust interaction across diverse object dynamics without external state estimation, enabling proactive collision/affordance reasoning and adaptable state estimation under distribution shifts.

title: Charting Empirical Laws for LLM Fine-Tuning in Scientific Multi-Discipline Learning
summary: While large language models (LLMs) have achieved strong performance through fine-tuning within individual scientific domains, their learning dynamics in multi-disciplinary contexts remains poorly understood, despite the promise of improved generalization and broader applicability through cross-domain knowledge synergy. In this work, we present the first systematic study of multi-disciplinary LLM fine-tuning, constructing a five-discipline corpus and analyzing learning patterns of full fine-tuning, LoRA, LoRA-MoE, and LoRA compositions. Particularly, our study shows that multi-disciplinary learning is substantially more variable than single-discipline training and distills four consistent empirical laws: (1) Balance-then-Diversity: low-resource disciplines degrade performance unless mitigated via diversity-aware upsampling; (2) Merge-then-Align: restoring instruction-following ability is critical for cross-discipline synergy; (3) Optimize-then-Scale: parameter scaling offers limited gains without prior design optimization; and (4) Share-then-Specialize: asymmetric LoRA-MoE yields robust gains with minimal trainable parameters via shared low-rank projection. Together, these laws form a practical recipe for principled multi-discipline fine-tuning and provide actionable guidance for developing generalizable scientific LLMs.

AI.tldr: A systematic study of multi-disciplinary LLM fine-tuning across five disciplines reveals four empirical laws—Balance-then-Diversity, Merge-then-Align, Optimize-then-Scale, and Share-then-Specialize—and offers a practical recipe for cross-domain generalization using full fine-tuning, LoRA, LoRA-MoE, and LoRA compositions.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in understanding how cross-domain or multi-disciplinary fine-tuning dynamics differ from single-domain training, and how to leverage cross-domain synergy to improve generalization of scientific LLMs.
AI.method: Construct a five-discipline corpus and empirically analyze learning dynamics under four training paradigms (full fine-tuning, LoRA, LoRA-MoE, LoRA compositions) to identify robust patterns and laws.
AI.result: Multi-disciplinary learning exhibits substantially higher variability than single-domain training; four consistent empirical laws are distilled, providing a principled recipe for multi-discipline fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: The laws enable principled, practical guidance for developing generalizable scientific LLMs, with asymmetric LoRA-MoE offering robust gains at low trainable-parameter cost.

title: Ctrl&Shift: High-Quality Geometry-Aware Object Manipulation in Visual Generation
summary: Object-level manipulation, relocating or reorienting objects in images or videos while preserving scene realism, is central to film post-production, AR, and creative editing. Yet existing methods struggle to jointly achieve three core goals: background preservation, geometric consistency under viewpoint shifts, and user-controllable transformations. Geometry-based approaches offer precise control but require explicit 3D reconstruction and generalize poorly; diffusion-based methods generalize better but lack fine-grained geometric control. We present Ctrl&Shift, an end-to-end diffusion framework to achieve geometry-consistent object manipulation without explicit 3D representations. Our key insight is to decompose manipulation into two stages, object removal and reference-guided inpainting under explicit camera pose control, and encode both within a unified diffusion process. To enable precise, disentangled control, we design a multi-task, multi-stage training strategy that separates background, identity, and pose signals across tasks. To improve generalization, we introduce a scalable real-world dataset construction pipeline that generates paired image and video samples with estimated relative camera poses. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Ctrl&Shift achieves state-of-the-art results in fidelity, viewpoint consistency, and controllability. To our knowledge, this is the first framework to unify fine-grained geometric control and real-world generalization for object manipulation, without relying on any explicit 3D modeling.

AI.tldr: Ctrl&Shift is a diffusion-based framework enabling geometry-consistent object manipulation in images/videos without explicit 3D models, via two-stage object removal and reference-guided inpainting with camera pose control, unified within a multi-task diffusion process and trained on a scalable real-world dataset.
AI.motivation: There is a need to simultaneously preserve background, maintain geometric consistency under viewpoint changes, and offer user-controlled object manipulation. Geometry-based approaches require explicit 3D reconstruction and generalize poorly; diffusion approaches generalize but lack fine-grained geometric control. A unified framework is needed to achieve both real-world generalization and precise geometric control.
AI.method: An end-to-end diffusion framework that splits manipulation into two stages—object removal and reference-guided inpainting with explicit camera pose control—encoded within a unified diffusion process. A multi-task, multi-stage training strategy disentangles background, identity, and pose signals across tasks. A scalable data pipeline generates paired image/video samples with estimated relative camera poses for real-world generalization.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art performance in fidelity, viewpoint consistency, and controllability. Ctrl&Shift is claimed to be the first framework to unify fine-grained geometric control with real-world generalization for object manipulation without explicit 3D modeling.
