In online A/B testing, CADET achieves a 11.04\% CTR lift compared to the production LiRank baseline model, a hybrid ensemble of DCNv2 and sequential encoders. The system has been successfully deployed on LinkedIn's advertising platform, serving the main traffic for homefeed sponsored updates.

AI.tldr: CADET is a context-conditioned decoder-only transformer for ads CTR prediction, addressing post-scoring signals, stability, temporal relations, and production-scale concerns; achieves 11.04% CTR lift in online tests and deployed in LinkedIn ads.
AI.motivation: Ad CTR modeling faces offline-online consistency, post-click/post-scoring signals, and scale; generative transformer-based recommenders show promise but require adaptation for ads.
AI.method: Context-conditioned decoding with multi-tower heads for post-scoring signals; self-gated attention; timestamp RoPE; session masking to prevent reliance on unavailable in-session events; production engineering: tensor packing, sequence chunking, and custom Flash Attention kernels for efficiency.
AI.result: 11.04% CTR lift over LiRank baseline in online A/B; deployment on LinkedIn homefeed sponsored updates; demonstrates production viability in large-scale ad systems.
AI.conclusion: Shows that decoder-only transformers with explicit post-score conditioning and efficient engineering can outperform traditional CTR models in large-scale advertising platforms; provides design patterns for production-ready generative CTR models.

title: RI-Mamba: Rotation-Invariant Mamba for Robust Text-to-Shape Retrieval
summary: 3D assets have rapidly expanded in quantity and diversity due to the growing popularity of virtual reality and gaming. As a result, text-to-shape retrieval has become essential in facilitating intuitive search within large repositories. However, existing methods require canonical poses and support few object categories, limiting their real-world applicability where objects can belong to diverse classes and appear in random orientations. To address this challenge, we propose RI-Mamba, the first rotation-invariant state-space model for point clouds. RI-Mamba defines global and local reference frames to disentangle pose from geometry and uses Hilbert sorting to construct token sequences with meaningful geometric structure while maintaining rotation invariance. We further introduce a novel strategy to compute orientational embeddings and reintegrate them via feature-wise linear modulation, effectively recovering spatial context and enhancing model expressiveness. Our strategy is inherently compatible with state-space models and operates in linear time. To scale up retrieval, we adopt cross-modal contrastive learning with automated triplet generation, allowing training on diverse datasets without manual annotation. Extensive experiments demonstrate RI-Mamba's superior representational capacity and robustness, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the OmniObject3D benchmark across more than 200 object categories under arbitrary orientations. Our code will be made available at https://github.com/ndkhanh360/RI-Mamba.git.

AI.tldr: Rotationally invariant state-space model for point clouds enabling robust text-to-shape retrieval across arbitrary orientations with scalable linear-time processing and cross-modal contrastive training.
AI.motivation: Address rotation/pose diversity and category variety in text-to-shape retrieval, removing reliance on canonical poses and expanding beyond a small set of object categories.
AI.method: Define global/local reference frames to disentangle pose from geometry; use Hilbert sorting to construct geometry-aware token sequences; compute orientational embeddings and reintegrate them via feature-wise linear modulation (FiLM); integrate with state-space models for linear-time processing; employ cross-modal contrastive learning with automated triplet generation to scale training without manual annotations.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on OmniObject3D across more than 200 object categories under arbitrary orientations; robust and scalable; code release forthcoming.
AI.conclusion: First rotation-invariant state-space model for point clouds enabling robust text-to-shape retrieval across diverse object classes and arbitrary orientations; demonstrates strong representational capacity and practicality; open avenues for broader 3D retrieval and downstream tasks.

title: Text2GQL-Bench: A Text to Graph Query Language Benchmark [Experiment, Analysis & Benchmark]
summary: Graph models are fundamental to data analysis in domains rich with complex relationships. Text-to-Graph-Query-Language (Text-to-GQL) systems act as a translator, converting natural language into executable graph queries. This capability allows Large Language Models (LLMs) to directly analyze and manipulate graph data, posi-tioning them as powerful agent infrastructures for Graph Database Management System (GDBMS). Despite recent progress, existing datasets are often limited in domain coverage, supported graph query languages, or evaluation scope. The advancement of Text-to-GQL systems is hindered by the lack of high-quality benchmark datasets and evaluation methods to systematically compare model capabilities across different graph query languages and domains. In this work, we present Text2GQL-Bench, a unified Text-to-GQL benchmark designed to address these limitations. Text2GQL-Bench couples a multi-GQL dataset that has 178,184 (Question, Query) pairs spanning 13 domains, with a scalable construction framework that generates datasets in different domains, question abstraction levels, and GQLs with heterogeneous resources. To support compre-hensive assessment, we introduce an evaluation method that goes beyond a single end-to-end metric by jointly reporting grammatical validity, similarity, semantic alignment, and execution accuracy. Our evaluation uncovers a stark dialect gap in ISO-GQL generation: even strong LLMs achieve only at most 4% execution accuracy (EX) in zero-shot settings, though a fixed 3-shot prompt raises accuracy to around 50%, the grammatical validity remains lower than 70%. Moreover, a fine-tuned 8B open-weight model reaches 45.1% EX, and 90.8% grammatical validity, demonstrating that most of the performance jump is unlocked by exposure to sufficient ISO-GQL examples.

AI.tldr: Text2GQL-Bench provides a unified benchmark for Text-to-GQL across multiple GQLs and domains; reveals a strong ISO-GQL dialect gap: low zero-shot execution accuracy, modest few-shot gains, and substantial gains with an 8B open-weight model; dataset/framework enable cross-domain evaluation.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of high-quality, domain-spanning benchmarks and evaluation methods for Text-to-GQL systems to compare capabilities across graph query languages and domains.
AI.method: Constructs Text2GQL-Bench by aggregating a multi-GQL dataset with 178,184 (Question, Query) pairs across 13 domains and a scalable generation framework enabling data in different domains, abstraction levels, and GQLs with heterogeneous resources. Proposes an evaluation suite that reports grammatical validity, similarity, semantic alignment, and execution accuracy, and analyzes zero-shot, few-shot (3-shot), and fine-tuned model performance.
AI.result: Finds a prominent dialect gap in ISO-GQL generation: zero-shot execution accuracy (EX) ≤ 4%; 3-shot prompts raise EX to ~50% while grammatical validity remains <70%. An 8B open-weight model, when fine-tuned, achieves 45.1% EX and 90.8% grammatical validity, indicating most gains come from exposure to ISO-GQL examples.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark reveals dialect sensitivity in Text-to-GQL and provides a framework for cross-domain evaluation. Greater ISO-GQL coverage and exposure are essential for domain-robust translation, and future work should focus on expanding dialect resources and refining multi-maceted evaluation metrics.

title: TimeSynth: A Framework for Uncovering Systematic Biases in Time Series Forecasting
summary: Time series forecasting is a fundamental tool with wide ranging applications, yet recent debates question whether complex nonlinear architectures truly outperform simple linear models. Prior claims of dominance of the linear model often stem from benchmarks that lack diverse temporal dynamics and employ biased evaluation protocols. We revisit this debate through TimeSynth, a structured framework that emulates key properties of real world time series,including non-stationarity, periodicity, trends, and phase modulation by creating synthesized signals whose parameters are derived from real-world time series. Evaluating four model families Linear, Multi Layer Perceptrons (MLP), Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), and Transformers, we find a systematic bias in linear models: they collapse to simple oscillation regardless of signal complexity. Nonlinear models avoid this collapse and gain clear advantages as signal complexity increases. Notably, Transformers and CNN based models exhibit slightly greater adaptability to complex modulated signals compared to MLPs. Beyond clean forecasting, the framework highlights robustness differences under distribution and noise shifts and removes biases of prior benchmarks by using independent instances for train, test, and validation for each signal family. Collectively, TimeSynth provides a principled foundation for understanding when different forecasting approaches succeed or fail, moving beyond oversimplified claims of model equivalence.

AI.tldr: TimeSynth benchmarking shows nonlinear models generally beat linear models on complex, non-stationary time series; linear models collapse to simple oscillations. CNNs and Transformers adapt better to modulated signals than MLPs. The framework enables fair, robust evaluation with independent train/val/test splits and tests against distribution and noise shifts.
AI.motivation: Address persistent claims that complex nonlinear models universally outperform simple linear models in time-series forecasting by correcting biased benchmarks and incorporating realistic, diverse temporal dynamics.
AI.method: Develop TimeSynth to generate synthetic time series with key real-world properties (non-stationarity, periodicity, trends, phase modulation) derived from real data. Compare four model families—Linear, MLP, CNN, Transformer—across independent train/validation/test splits per signal family, and assess robustness under distribution and noise shifts.
AI.result: Nonlinear models avoid collapse to simple oscillations as signal complexity grows; linear models fail to capture complexity. Transformers and CNNs show slightly better adaptability to modulated signals than MLPs. TimeSynth exposes biases in prior benchmarks and supports robust evaluation under shifts.
AI.conclusion: TimeSynth provides a principled framework to understand when forecasting approaches succeed or fail, enabling fair comparisons across diverse time-series dynamics and moving beyond oversimplified claims of model equivalence.

title: Semantically Conditioned Diffusion Models for Cerebral DSA Synthesis
summary: Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) plays a central role in the diagnosis and treatment of cerebrovascular disease, yet its invasive nature and high acquisition cost severely limit large-scale data collection and public data sharing. Therefore, we developed a semantically conditioned latent diffusion model (LDM) that synthesizes arterial-phase cerebral DSA frames under explicit control of anatomical circulation (anterior vs.\ posterior) and canonical C-arm positions. We curated a large single-centre DSA dataset of 99,349 frames and trained a conditional LDM using text embeddings that encoded anatomy and acquisition geometry. To assess clinical realism, four medical experts, including two neuroradiologists, one neurosurgeon, and one internal medicine expert, systematically rated 400 synthetic DSA images using a 5-grade Likert scale for evaluating proximal large, medium, and small peripheral vessels. The generated images achieved image-wise overall Likert scores ranging from 3.1 to 3.3, with high inter-rater reliability (ICC(2,k) = 0.80--0.87). Distributional similarity to real DSA frames was supported by a low median Fréchet inception distance (FID) of 15.27. Our results indicate that semantically controlled LDMs can produce realistic synthetic DSAs suitable for downstream algorithm development, research, and training.

AI.tldr: Semantically conditioned latent diffusion model synthesizes arterial-phase cerebral DSA frames with explicit control over anatomy (anterior vs posterior) and C-arm acquisition geometry; trained on a large single-centre dataset (~99k frames); expert evaluation shows plausible realism (Likert 3.1–3.3, ICC 0.80–0.87) and low Fréchet Inception Distance (FID median 15.27), supporting use in research/training while generalizability requires multi-centre validation.
AI.motivation: DSA is invasive and expensive, limiting data collection and public sharing. Generating realistic synthetic DSA data can facilitate algorithm development, validation, and training without patient risk.
AI.method: Train a conditional latent diffusion model (LDM) using text embeddings that encode anatomy and acquisition geometry. Conditioning variables include arterial circulation type (anterior vs posterior) and canonical C-arm positions. The model trained on a large single-centre dataset of 99,349 DSA frames. Evaluation used four experts rating 400 synthetic images on a 5-point Likert scale for proximal large, medium, and small peripheral vessels.
AI.result: 400 synthetic DSA images rated by four experts yielded image-wise Likert scores from 3.1 to 3.3 with high inter-rater reliability (ICC(2,k) = 0.80–0.87). Distributional similarity to real frames supported by a low median FID of 15.27. The study concludes that semantically controlled LDMs can produce realistic synthetic DSAs suitable for downstream algorithm development, research, and training.
AI.conclusion: Semantically controlled latent diffusion models can generate realistic arterial-phase cerebral DSA frames with controllable anatomy and acquisition geometry, offering a valuable resource for research, development, and training. Generalizability across centres and scanners warrants further validation.

title: AIR: Improving Agent Safety through Incident Response
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) agents are increasingly deployed in practice across a wide range of autonomous applications. Yet current safety mechanisms for LLM agents focus almost exclusively on preventing failures in advance, providing limited capabilities for responding to, containing, or recovering from incidents after they inevitably arise. In this work, we introduce AIR, the first incident response framework for LLM agent systems. AIR defines a domain-specific language for managing the incident response lifecycle autonomously in LLM agent systems, and integrates it into the agent's execution loop to (1) detect incidents via semantic checks grounded in the current environment state and recent context, (2) guide the agent to execute containment and recovery actions via its tools, and (3) synthesize guardrail rules during eradication to block similar incidents in future executions. We evaluate AIR on three representative agent types. Results show that AIR achieves detection, remediation, and eradication success rates all exceeding 90%. Extensive experiments further confirm the necessity of AIR's key design components, show the timeliness and moderate overhead of AIR, and demonstrate that LLM-generated rules can approach the effectiveness of developer-authored rules across domains. These results show that incident response is both feasible and essential as a first-class mechanism for improving agent safety.

AI.tldr: Introduces AIR, an incident response framework for LLM agents with a domain-specific language to detect, contain, recover from incidents, and generate guardrail rules; achieves >90% success in detection, remediation, and eradication across three agent types; validates design components and viability of LLM-generated rules.
AI.motivation: Current safety approaches emphasize preemptive safeguards but lack autonomous post-incident response capabilities. There is a need for timely detection, containment, and recovery to manage inevitable incidents in LLM agent systems.
AI.method: Developed AIR, a domain-specific language embedded in the agent execution loop to (1) detect incidents via semantic checks tied to environment and context, (2) guide containment/recovery actions through available tools, (3) synthesize guardrail rules during eradication. Evaluated across three representative agent types with analyses of timeliness and overhead, and compared LLM-generated rules to developer-authored rules.
AI.result: AIR achieves detection, remediation, and eradication success rates exceeding 90% across the tested agents. Experiments confirm the necessity of AIR’s design components, show moderate overhead and timely operation, and demonstrate that LLM-generated rules can approach the effectiveness of developer-authored rules across domains.
AI.conclusion: Incident response is feasible and essential as a first-class mechanism for improving safety in LLM agent systems; AIR demonstrates how autonomous detection, containment, and eradication can be integrated into the agent loop to enhance robustness.

title: Multi-Level Strategic Classification: Incentivizing Improvement through Promotion and Relegation Dynamics
summary: Strategic classification studies the problem where self-interested individuals or agents manipulate their response to obtain favorable decision outcomes made by classifiers, typically turning to dishonest actions when they are less costly than genuine efforts. While existing studies on sequential strategic classification primarily focus on optimizing dynamic classifier weights, we depart from these weight-centric approaches by analyzing the design of classifier thresholds and difficulty progression within a multi-level promotion-relegation framework. Our model captures the critical inter-temporal incentives driven by an agent's farsightedness, skill retention, and a leg-up effect where qualification and attainment can be self-reinforcing. We characterize the agent's optimal long-term strategy and demonstrate that a principal can design a sequence of thresholds to effectively incentivize honest effort. Crucially, we prove that under mild conditions, this mechanism enables agents to reach arbitrarily high levels solely through genuine improvement efforts.

AI.tldr: Shift from optimizing dynamic classifier weights to designing threshold progression in a multi-level framework; evidence that properly spaced thresholds can elicit honest effort and, under mild conditions, yield arbitrarily high performance through genuine improvement.
AI.motivation: Address long-horizon strategic behavior in classification, where agents weigh honest effort against manipulation over time; current work focuses on weights, here emphasis on thresholds and progressive difficulty to shape incentives.
AI.method: Model a principal-agent setting with a sequence of promotion-relegation levels; agent is farsighted, retains skills, and benefits from a leg-up effect; characterizes optimal policy for agent and proves sufficiency to achieve honest effort via thresholds; derives conditions for arbitrarily high levels.
AI.result: Demonstrates that a threshold-based promotion-relegation design can align incentives over time and lead to unbounded improvement with honest effort under mild assumptions.
AI.conclusion: Threshold sequencing in strategic classification offers a robust alternative to weight-centric designs, enabling scalable incentive design for long-term honesty and performance.

title: TG-Field: Geometry-Aware Radiative Gaussian Fields for Tomographic Reconstruction
comment: Accepted to AAAI 2026. Project page: https://vcc.tech/research/2026/TG-Field
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has revolutionized 3D scene representation with superior efficiency and quality. While recent adaptations for computed tomography (CT) show promise, they struggle with severe artifacts under highly sparse-view projections and dynamic motions. To address these challenges, we propose Tomographic Geometry Field (TG-Field), a geometry-aware Gaussian deformation framework tailored for both static and dynamic CT reconstruction. A multi-resolution hash encoder is employed to capture local spatial priors, regularizing primitive parameters under ultra-sparse settings. We further extend the framework to dynamic reconstruction by introducing time-conditioned representations and a spatiotemporal attention block to adaptively aggregate features, thereby resolving spatiotemporal ambiguities and enforcing temporal coherence. In addition, a motion-flow network models fine-grained respiratory motion to track local anatomical deformations. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that TG-Field consistently outperforms existing methods, achieving state-of-the-art reconstruction accuracy under highly sparse-view conditions.

AI.tldr: TG-Field advances sparse-view CT reconstruction by modeling geometry with 3D Gaussian splats, using a multi-resolution hash encoder, time-conditioned representations, spatiotemporal attention, and a motion-flow network to achieve state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Sparse-view CT yields severe artifacts and temporal inconsistencies; a geometry-aware, dynamic representation is needed to recover accurate structures and motion.
AI.method: Propose Tomographic Geometry Field (TG-Field) that uses geometry-aware Gaussian deformation with 3D Gaussian splats; a multi-resolution hash encoder regularizes local priors; extends to dynamic CT with time-conditioned features, spatiotemporal attention, and a motion-flow network modeling respiratory motion.
AI.result: Experimental results on synthetic and real datasets show TG-Field achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction accuracy under highly sparse-view conditions, outperforming existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: TG-Field offers a unified, scalable framework for static and dynamic CT reconstruction in ultra-sparse settings, effectively mitigating artifacts and preserving temporal coherence.

title: TSR: Trajectory-Search Rollouts for Multi-Turn RL of LLM Agents
summary: Advances in large language models (LLMs) are driving a shift toward using reinforcement learning (RL) to train agents from iterative, multi-turn interactions across tasks. However, multi-turn RL remains challenging as rewards are often sparse or delayed, and environments can be stochastic. In this regime, naive trajectory sampling can hinder exploitation and induce mode collapse. We propose TSR (Trajectory-Search Rollouts), a training-time approach that repurposes test-time scaling ideas for improved per-turn rollout generation. TSR performs lightweight tree-style search to construct high-quality trajectories by selecting high-scoring actions at each turn using task-specific feedback. This improves rollout quality and stabilizes learning while leaving the underlying optimization objective unchanged, making TSR optimizer-agnostic. We instantiate TSR with best-of-N, beam, and shallow lookahead search, and pair it with PPO and GRPO, achieving up to 15% performance gains and more stable learning on Sokoban, FrozenLake, and WebShop tasks at a one-time increase in training compute. By moving search from inference time to the rollout stage of training, TSR provides a simple and general mechanism for stronger multi-turn agent learning, complementary to existing frameworks and rejection-sampling-style selection methods.

AI.tldr: Repurposes test-time scaling ideas into training-time trajectory search (TSR) to improve multi-turn RL by generating high-quality rollouts via lightweight tree search; optimizer-agnostic; achieves up to ~15% gains on Sokoban, FrozenLake, WebShop with PPO/GRPO; incurs modest one-time training compute increase.
AI.motivation: Address sparse/delayed rewards, stochastic environments, and mode collapse in multi-turn RL; improve sample efficiency and stability without changing the optimization objective.
AI.method: Lightweight tree-style search during rollout generation; select high-scoring actions at each turn using task feedback; implements best-of-N, beam, and shallow lookahead; remains compatible with existing RL objectives and optimizers (PPO/GRPO).
AI.result: Up to 15% performance gains; more stable learning across Sokoban, FrozenLake, WebShop; one-time training compute overhead; optimizer-agnostic applicability; complementary to rejection-sampling strategies.
AI.conclusion: Shifts search from inference to training-time rollout as a general, simple mechanism to strengthen multi-turn agent learning; versatile across optimizers and tasks.

title: Hierarchical Concept Embedding & Pursuit for Interpretable Image Classification
summary: Interpretable-by-design models are gaining traction in computer vision because they provide faithful explanations for their predictions. In image classification, these models typically recover human-interpretable concepts from an image and use them for classification. Sparse concept recovery methods leverage the latent space of vision-language models to represent image embeddings as a sparse combination of concept embeddings. However, because such methods ignore the hierarchical structure of concepts, they can produce correct predictions with explanations that are inconsistent with the hierarchy. In this work, we propose Hierarchical Concept Embedding \& Pursuit (HCEP), a framework that induces a hierarchy of concept embeddings in the latent space and uses hierarchical sparse coding to recover the concepts present in an image. Given a hierarchy of semantic concepts, we construct a corresponding hierarchy of concept embeddings and, assuming the correct concepts for an image form a rooted path in the hierarchy, derive desirable conditions for identifying them in the embedded space. We show that hierarchical sparse coding reliably recovers hierarchical concept embeddings, whereas vanilla sparse coding fails. Our experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that HCEP outperforms baselines in concept precision and recall while maintaining competitive classification accuracy. Moreover, when the number of samples is limited, HCEP achieves superior classification accuracy and concept recovery. These results show that incorporating hierarchical structures into sparse coding yields more reliable and interpretable image classification models.

AI.tldr: HCEP introduces hierarchical concept embeddings with hierarchical sparse coding to align explanations with a semantic hierarchy, improving concept recovery and robustness while maintaining accuracy, especially in low-data settings.
AI.motivation: Existing sparse concept recovery methods ignore concept hierarchy, leading to explanations that may be semantically inconsistent with predictions. A hierarchy-aware approach is needed for faithful interpretability in image classification.
AI.method: Build a hierarchy of concept embeddings corresponding to semantic concepts. Assume the true concepts form a rooted path in this hierarchy. Derive identifiability conditions in the embedding space and apply hierarchical sparse coding to recover the path-based concepts, contrasting with vanilla sparse coding.
AI.result: Hierarchical sparse coding reliably recovers hierarchical concept embeddings; vanilla sparse coding fails. HCEP outperforms baselines in concept precision and recall while maintaining competitive classification accuracy; in low-sample regimes, it achieves superior accuracy and concept recovery.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating hierarchical structure into sparse coding yields more reliable and interpretable image classification models, with improved concept recovery and robustness under limited data.

title: LLM-Driven 3D Scene Generation of Agricultural Simulation Environments
comment: Accepted at IEEE Conference on Artificial Intelligence 2026
summary: Procedural generation techniques in 3D rendering engines have revolutionized the creation of complex environments, reducing reliance on manual design. Recent approaches using Large Language Models (LLMs) for 3D scene generation show promise but often lack domain-specific reasoning, verification mechanisms, and modular design. These limitations lead to reduced control and poor scalability. This paper investigates the use of LLMs to generate agricultural synthetic simulation environments from natural language prompts, specifically to address the limitations of lacking domain-specific reasoning, verification mechanisms, and modular design. A modular multi-LLM pipeline was developed, integrating 3D asset retrieval, domain knowledge injection, and code generation for the Unreal rendering engine using its API. This results in a 3D environment with realistic planting layouts and environmental context, all based on the input prompt and the domain knowledge. To enhance accuracy and scalability, the system employs a hybrid strategy combining LLM optimization techniques such as few-shot prompting, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), finetuning, and validation. Unlike monolithic models, the modular architecture enables structured data handling, intermediate verification, and flexible expansion. The system was evaluated using structured prompts and semantic accuracy metrics. A user study assessed realism and familiarity against real-world images, while an expert comparison demonstrated significant time savings over manual scene design. The results confirm the effectiveness of multi-LLM pipelines in automating domain-specific 3D scene generation with improved reliability and precision. Future work will explore expanding the asset hierarchy, incorporating real-time generation, and adapting the pipeline to other simulation domains beyond agriculture.

AI.tldr: A modular multi-LLM pipeline maps natural-language prompts to Unreal-based agricultural scenes, combining 3D asset retrieval, domain-knowledge injection, and code generation with RAG and validation. It yields realistic planting layouts, improved reliability and scalability, and time savings over manual design; future work includes expanded asset hierarchies and cross-domain adaptation.
AI.motivation: LLMs for 3D scene generation often lack domain-specific reasoning, verification, and modular design, limiting control and scalability in specialized domains like agriculture. A modular, verifiable pipeline is needed to reliably translate NL prompts into executable rendering code.
AI.method: Developed a modular multi-LLM pipeline integrating 3D asset retrieval, domain-knowledge injection, and Unreal Engine API code generation. The system uses few-shot prompting, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), finetuning, and validation. Its modular architecture enables structured data handling, intermediate verification, and easy expansion. Outputs are executable Unreal scenes generated from natural language prompts.
AI.result: The pipeline produced a 3D agricultural environment with realistic planting layouts and environmental context based on input prompts. Evaluation via structured prompts and semantic accuracy metrics showed improved reliability and precision. A user study found realism and familiarity comparable to real-world images, and expert reviewers reported significant time savings versus manual scene design.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that modular multi-LLM pipelines can automate domain-specific 3D scene generation with improved reliability and precision. The architecture supports scalability, verification, and expansion to other domains; future work includes expanding the asset hierarchy, enabling real-time generation, and adapting the pipeline to additional simulation domains beyond agriculture.

title: How to Optimize Multispecies Set Predictions in Presence-Absence Modeling ?
summary: Species distribution models (SDMs) commonly produce probabilistic occurrence predictions that must be converted into binary presence-absence maps for ecological inference and conservation planning. However, this binarization step is typically heuristic and can substantially distort estimates of species prevalence and community composition. We present MaxExp, a decision-driven binarization framework that selects the most probable species assemblage by directly maximizing a chosen evaluation metric. MaxExp requires no calibration data and is flexible across several scores. We also introduce the Set Size Expectation (SSE) method, a computationally efficient alternative that predicts assemblages based on expected species richness. Using three case studies spanning diverse taxa, species counts, and performance metrics, we show that MaxExp consistently matches or surpasses widely used thresholding and calibration methods, especially under strong class imbalance and high rarity. SSE offers a simpler yet competitive option. Together, these methods provide robust, reproducible tools for multispecies SDM binarization.

AI.tldr: MaxExp is a decision-driven binarization framework for multispecies SDMs that optimizes an evaluation metric to select the most probable assemblage without calibration data, while SSE offers a fast alternative based on expected species richness. Across three case studies, MaxExp outperforms traditional thresholding/calibration methods, particularly under imbalance and rarity; SSE is simpler yet competitive.
AI.motivation: Binarizing SDM outputs is commonly heuristic and can distort species prevalence and community composition; there is a need for calibration-free, metric-aligned binarization methods that work across diverse metrics and data conditions.
AI.method: MaxExp directly maximizes a chosen evaluation metric by selecting the assemblage with the highest score, without requiring calibration data; it supports multiple metrics. SSE estimates assemblages by projecting the expected species richness and selecting consistent assemblages accordingly, offering a computationally efficient alternative.
AI.result: MaxExp consistently matches or surpasses widely used thresholding and calibration methods, especially under strong class imbalance and high rarity. SSE provides a simpler yet competitive approach. Three case studies spanning diverse taxa and performance metrics demonstrate robustness and improved inference for multispecies SDMs.
AI.conclusion: The MaxExp framework and the SSE method enhance reproducibility and robustness in binarizing multispecies SDMs, enabling better ecological inference and conservation planning across varying datasets and evaluation criteria.

title: Assessing Low Back Movement with Motion Tape Sensor Data Through Deep Learning
summary: Back pain is a pervasive issue affecting a significant portion of the population, often worsened by certain movements of the lower back. Assessing these movements is important for helping clinicians prescribe appropriate physical therapy. However, it can be difficult to monitor patients' movements remotely outside the clinic. High-fidelity data from motion capture sensors can be used to classify different movements, but these sensors are costly and impractical for use in free-living environments. Motion Tape (MT), a new fabric-based wearable sensor, addresses these issues by being low cost and portable. Despite these advantages, novelty and variability in sensor stability make the MT dataset small scale and inherent to noise. In this work, we propose the Motion-Tape Augmentation Inference Model (MT-AIM), a deep learning classification pipeline trained on MT data. In order to address the challenges of limited sample size and noise present within the MT dataset, MT-AIM leverages conditional generative models to generate synthetic MT data of a desired movement, as well as predicting joint kinematics as additional features. This combination of synthetic data generation and feature augmentation enables MT-AIM to achieve state-of-the-art accuracy in classifying lower back movements, bridging the gap between physiological sensing and movement analysis.

AI.tldr: MT-AIM uses conditional generative models to augment Motion Tape data and predicts joint kinematics to classify lower back movements, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy despite small, noisy datasets.
AI.motivation: Enable remote monitoring of lower back movements with a low-cost, portable MT sensor, addressing limited data and noise to support effective physical therapy in free-living environments.
AI.method: A deep learning pipeline (MT-AIM) that (1) generates synthetic MT data conditioned on the target movement and (2) predicts joint kinematics as additional features to augment classification.
AI.result: The approach achieves state-of-the-art accuracy in classifying lower back movements on MT data, outperforming baselines through data synthesis and feature augmentation.
AI.conclusion: MT-AIM effectively links physiological sensing with movement analysis in low-resource settings, offering improved remote assessment of back-movement patterns and potential for scalable rehabilitation support.

title: GSO-SLAM: Bidirectionally Coupled Gaussian Splatting and Direct Visual Odometry
summary: We propose GSO-SLAM, a real-time monocular dense SLAM system that leverages Gaussian scene representation. Unlike existing methods that couple tracking and mapping with a unified scene, incurring computational costs, or loosely integrate them with well-structured tracking frameworks, introducing redundancies, our method bidirectionally couples Visual Odometry (VO) and Gaussian Splatting (GS). Specifically, our approach formulates joint optimization within an Expectation-Maximization (EM) framework, enabling the simultaneous refinement of VO-derived semi-dense depth estimates and the GS representation without additional computational overhead. Moreover, we present Gaussian Splat Initialization, which utilizes image information, keyframe poses, and pixel associations from VO to produce close approximations to the final Gaussian scene, thereby eliminating the need for heuristic methods. Through extensive experiments, we validate the effectiveness of our method, showing that it not only operates in real time but also achieves state-of-the-art geometric/photometric fidelity of the reconstructed scene and tracking accuracy.

AI.tldr: GSO-SLAM is a real-time monocular dense SLAM system that jointly optimizes VO-derived semi-dense depth and a Gaussian Splatting scene within an EM framework, aided by a VO-informed Gaussian Splat Initialization to avoid heuristics, achieving real-time operation with state-of-the-art geometry/photometry fidelity and tracking accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing SLAM approaches either tightly couple tracking and mapping, incurring high computational cost, or loosely couple them with potential redundancies. There is a need for efficient, bidirectional integration that refines depth and the Gaussian scene without extra overhead and without heuristic initialization.
AI.method: Formulates joint VO-depth and Gaussian Splatting scene optimization via Expectation-Maximization. The EM process alternates updating VO-derived semi-dense depth estimates and refining the Gaussian scene representation. Introduces Gaussian Splat Initialization using image data, keyframe poses, and VO pixel associations to approximate the final scene without heuristic methods.
AI.result: Experiments show real-time performance and state-of-the-art geometric/photometric fidelity of the reconstructed scene, along with high tracking accuracy on monocular dense SLAM benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Bidirectional EM-based coupling of VO and Gaussian Splatting, combined with a VO-informed initialization, yields accurate and efficient monocular dense SLAM, reducing overhead, avoiding heuristics, and delivering high-quality reconstructions and reliable tracking.

title: RELATE: A Reinforcement Learning-Enhanced LLM Framework for Advertising Text Generation
summary:
In online advertising, advertising text plays a critical role in attracting user engagement and driving advertiser value. Existing industrial systems typically follow a two-stage paradigm, where candidate texts are first generated and subsequently aligned with online performance metrics such as click-through rate(CTR). This separation often leads to misaligned optimization objectives and low funnel efficiency, limiting global optimality.
  To address these limitations, we propose RELATE, a reinforcement learning-based end-to-end framework that unifies generation and objective alignment within a single model. Instead of decoupling text generation from downstream metric alignment, RELATE integrates performance and compliance objectives directly into the generation process via policy learning. To better capture ultimate advertiser value beyond click-level signals, We incorporate conversion-oriented metrics into the objective and jointly model them with compliance constraints as multi-dimensional rewards, enabling the model to generate high-quality ad texts that improve conversion performance under policy constraints.
  Extensive experiments on large-scale industrial datasets demonstrate that RELATE consistently outperforms baselines. Furthermore, online deployment on a production advertising platform yields statistically significant improvements in click-through conversion rate(CTCVR) under strict policy constraints, validating the robustness and real-world effectiveness of the proposed framework.

AI.tldr: RelATE is an RL-based end-to-end ad text generation framework that unifies generation and objective alignment, incorporating conversion-oriented metrics and compliance constraints as multi-dimensional rewards. It achieves superior offline performance and, in production, improves click-through conversion rate under policy constraints.
AI.motivation: Two-stage ad text pipelines suffer from misaligned optimization objectives and inefficient funnel progression. There is a need to optimize directly for business-relevant metrics (e.g., conversions) while respecting compliance constraints within a single model.
AI.method: RELATE integrates generation and objective alignment via reinforcement learning. It directly optimizes multi-dimensional rewards that combine downstream performance (conversion-oriented metrics) and compliance constraints, enabling end-to-end policy learning for high-quality ad text that boosts conversion while adhering to policy constraints. Evaluations include large-scale offline experiments and online deployment on a production advertising platform.
AI.result: Across large-scale industrial datasets, RELATE consistently outperforms baselines. Online deployment yields statistically significant improvements in click-through conversion rate (CTCVR) under strict policy constraints, validating real-world effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: An end-to-end RL framework that jointly optimizes for performance and compliance can improve advertiser value and online funnel efficiency, demonstrating robustness and practical impact in industrial settings.

title: STVG-R1: Incentivizing Instance-Level Reasoning and Grounding in Videos via Reinforcement Learning
summary: In vision-language models (VLMs), misalignment between textual descriptions and visual coordinates often induces hallucinations. This issue becomes particularly severe in dense prediction tasks such as spatial-temporal video grounding (STVG). Prior approaches typically focus on enhancing visual-textual alignment or attaching auxiliary decoders. However, these strategies inevitably introduce additional trainable modules, leading to significant annotation costs and computational overhead. In this work, we propose a novel visual prompting paradigm that avoids the difficult problem of aligning coordinates across modalities. Specifically, we reformulate per-frame coordinate prediction as a compact instance-level identification problem by assigning each object a unique, temporally consistent ID. These IDs are embedded into the video as visual prompts, providing explicit and interpretable inputs to the VLMs. Furthermore, we introduce STVG-R1, the first reinforcement learning framework for STVG, which employs a task-driven reward to jointly optimize temporal accuracy, spatial consistency, and structural format regularization. Extensive experiments on six benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. STVG-R1 surpasses the baseline Qwen2.5-VL-7B by a remarkable margin of 20.9% on m_IoU on the HCSTVG-v2 benchmark, establishing a new state of the art (SOTA). Surprisingly, STVG-R1 also exhibits strong zero-shot generalization to multi-object referring video object segmentation tasks, achieving a SOTA 47.3% J&F on MeViS.

AI.tldr: Reframes STVG as an instance-level identification problem using visual prompts to embed temporally consistent IDs, avoiding coordinate alignment hassles; introduces STVG-R1, a reinforcement learning framework with task-driven rewards, achieving SOTA performance and strong zero-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: Misalignment between textual descriptions and visual coordinates causes hallucinations in vision-language models, especially in dense tasks like STVG. Existing fixes add trainable modules with high annotation and computation costs; a lightweight, interpretable prompting approach is desired to improve alignment and generalization.
AI.method: 1) Assign each object a unique, temporally consistent ID and embed these IDs as visual prompts in the video to guide VLMs, reframing per-frame coordinate prediction as a compact instance-level identification problem. 2) Propose STVG-R1, the first RL framework for STVG, optimizing a task-driven reward that balances temporal accuracy, spatial consistency, and structural format regularization.
AI.result: Empirical results on six benchmarks show STVG-R1 outperforms the baseline Qwen2.5-VL-7B by 20.9% in m_IoU on HCSTVG-v2, achieving SOTA. It also demonstrates strong zero-shot generalization to multiple-object referring video object segmentation, achieving 47.3% J&F on MeViS, a new SOTA for that task.
AI.conclusion: The combination of visual prompting for compact, interpretable object IDs and a reinforcement-learning objective tailored to STVG substantially improves accuracy, reduces reliance on additional trainable modules, and offers strong generalization across tasks.

title: FlowMind: Execute-Summarize for Structured Workflow Generation from LLM Reasoning
summary: LLMs can solve complex tasks through reasoning and tool use, but accurately translating these solutions into structured workflows remains challenging. We model workflows as sequences of tool use and reformulate the problem as designing a mechanism that can both solve tasks and reliably construct workflows. Prior approaches that build workflows during execution often suffer from inaccuracies due to interference between the two processes. We propose an Execute-Summarize(ES) framework that decouples task execution from workflow construction: the model first completes the task using available tools, then independently reconstructs a structured workflow from execution traces. This separation improves workflow accuracy and robustness. We introduce FlowBench and show through extensive experiments that our approach outperforms existing methods, providing a reliable paradigm for grounding free-form LLM reasoning into structured workflows.

AI.tldr: Decouples task execution from workflow construction in LLM-based tool use via an Execute-Summarize framework, improving workflow accuracy and robustness; introduces FlowBench for evaluation.
AI.motivation: LLMs can solve tasks using tools but translating solutions into reliable, structured workflows is error-prone when execution and workflow construction influence each other.
AI.method: Execute-Summarize (ES) framework: first execute the task with available tools, then independently reconstruct a structured workflow from execution traces; training/evaluation on FlowBench to compare against prior joint methods.
AI.result: Workflow reconstruction from traces yields higher accuracy and robustness; ES framework outperforms existing methods in grounding free-form LLM reasoning into structured workflows.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling execution and workflow construction is a reliable paradigm to convert LLM reasoning into structured workflows and can set a new standard for grounding in tool-use tasks.

title: JEPA-VLA: Video Predictive Embedding is Needed for VLA Models
summary: Recent vision-language-action (VLA) models built upon pretrained vision-language models (VLMs) have achieved significant improvements in robotic manipulation. However, current VLAs still suffer from low sample efficiency and limited generalization. This paper argues that these limitations are closely tied to an overlooked component, pretrained visual representation, which offers insufficient knowledge on both aspects of environment understanding and policy prior. Through an in-depth analysis, we find that commonly used visual representations in VLAs, whether pretrained via language-image contrastive learning or image-based self-supervised learning, remain inadequate at capturing crucial, task-relevant environment information and at inducing effective policy priors, i.e., anticipatory knowledge of how the environment evolves under successful task execution. In contrast, we discover that predictive embeddings pretrained on videos, in particular V-JEPA 2, are adept at flexibly discarding unpredictable environment factors and encoding task-relevant temporal dynamics, thereby effectively compensating for key shortcomings of existing visual representations in VLAs. Building on these observations, we introduce JEPA-VLA, a simple yet effective approach that adaptively integrates predictive embeddings into existing VLAs. Our experiments demonstrate that JEPA-VLA yields substantial performance gains across a range of benchmarks, including LIBERO, LIBERO-plus, RoboTwin2.0, and real-robot tasks.

AI.tldr: Predictive video embeddings (V-JEPA2) integrated into vision-language-action models via JEPA-VLA yield better sample efficiency and generalization, achieving strong gains on LIBERO, LIBERO-plus, RoboTwin2.0, and real-robot tasks.
AI.motivation: Current VLAs underperform due to inadequate pretrained visual representations that fail to capture task-relevant environment information and policy priors, impairing sample efficiency and generalization.
AI.method: Introduce JEPA-VLA that adaptively incorporates predictive embeddings pretrained on videos (V-JEPA2) into existing VLAs, enabling selective discarding of unpredictable factors and encoding task-relevant temporal dynamics.
AI.result: JEPA-VLA achieves substantial performance gains across diverse benchmarks including LIBERO, LIBERO-plus, RoboTwin2.0, and real-robot tasks.
AI.conclusion: Predictive video-based embeddings complement standard vision-language representations, addressing both environment understanding and policy priors; the approach is simple yet effective.

title: External Division of Two Bregman Proximity Operators for Poisson Inverse Problems
summary: This paper presents a novel method for recovering sparse vectors from linear models corrupted by Poisson noise. The contribution is twofold. First, an operator defined via the external division of two Bregman proximity operators is introduced to promote sparse solutions while mitigating the estimation bias induced by classical $\ell_1$-norm regularization. This operator is then embedded into the already established NoLips algorithm, replacing the standard Bregman proximity operator in a plug-and-play manner. Second, the geometric structure of the proposed external-division operator is elucidated through two complementary reformulations, which provide clear interpretations in terms of the primal and dual spaces of the Poisson inverse problem. Numerical tests show that the proposed method exhibits more stable convergence behavior than conventional Kullback-Leibler (KL)-based approaches and achieves significantly superior performance on synthetic data and an image restoration problem.

AI.tldr: Novel external-division operator for Poisson sparse recovery integrated into NoLips; two reformulations clarify primal/dual geometry; yields improved convergence stability and superior performance vs KL-based methods on synthetic and image restoration problems.
AI.motivation: Reduce bias from l1 regularization while recovering sparse vectors under Poisson noise; improve convergence stability and performance in Poisson inverse problems.
AI.method: Introduce an external-division operator defined as the ratio of two Bregman proximity operators to promote sparsity; embed into NoLips as a plug-and-play replacement for the Bregman prox; provide two reformulations exposing primal and dual interpretations.
AI.result: Demonstrates more stable convergence than KL-based approaches and superior performance on synthetic data and image restoration tasks.
AI.conclusion: The external-division operator within NoLips offers a principled and effective approach for Poisson sparse inverse problems, with interpretable geometry and empirical gains.

title: Adapting Vision-Language Models for E-commerce Understanding at Scale
summary: E-commerce product understanding demands by nature, strong multimodal comprehension from text, images, and structured attributes. General-purpose Vision-Language Models (VLMs) enable generalizable multimodal latent modelling, yet there is no documented, well-known strategy for adapting them to the attribute-centric, multi-image, and noisy nature of e-commerce data, without sacrificing general performance. In this work, we show through a large-scale experimental study, how targeted adaptation of general VLMs can substantially improve e-commerce performance while preserving broad multimodal capabilities. Furthermore, we propose a novel extensive evaluation suite covering deep product understanding, strict instruction following, and dynamic attribute extraction.

AI.tldr: Targeted adaptation of general Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to e-commerce data substantially boosts product understanding while preserving general multimodal capabilities; introduces an extensive evaluation suite for deep product understanding, instruction following, and dynamic attribute extraction.
AI.motivation: E-commerce data is inherently attribute-centric, multi-image, and noisy. General VLMs often fail to capture product-specific cues or reliably extract attributes, motivating a systematic adaptation strategy that preserves broad multimodal performance.
AI.method: Large-scale experimental study applying targeted adaptation of general VLMs to e-commerce tasks; development of an extensive evaluation suite to assess deep product understanding, strict instruction following, and dynamic attribute extraction, across multi-image product data.
AI.result: Adaptation yields substantial performance gains in e-commerce tasks without sacrificing broad multimodal capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Targeted adaptation is an effective approach to align general VLMs with the demands of e-commerce product understanding, and the proposed evaluation suite enables robust, multi-faceted assessment of model capabilities.

title: Beyond End-to-End Video Models: An LLM-Based Multi-Agent System for Educational Video Generation
comment: For more information, visit the project website: https://robitsg.github.io/LASEV/
summary: Although recent end-to-end video generation models demonstrate impressive performance in visually oriented content creation, they remain limited in scenarios that require strict logical rigor and precise knowledge representation, such as instructional and educational media. To address this problem, we propose LAVES, a hierarchical LLM-based multi-agent system for generating high-quality instructional videos from educational problems. The LAVES formulates educational video generation as a multi-objective task that simultaneously demands correct step-by-step reasoning, pedagogically coherent narration, semantically faithful visual demonstrations, and precise audio--visual alignment. To address the limitations of prior approaches--including low procedural fidelity, high production cost, and limited controllability--LAVES decomposes the generation workflow into specialized agents coordinated by a central Orchestrating Agent with explicit quality gates and iterative critique mechanisms. Specifically, the Orchestrating Agent supervises a Solution Agent for rigorous problem solving, an Illustration Agent that produces executable visualization codes, and a Narration Agent for learner-oriented instructional scripts. In addition, all outputs from the working agents are subject to semantic critique, rule-based constraints, and tool-based compilation checks. Rather than directly synthesizing pixels, the system constructs a structured executable video script that is deterministically compiled into synchronized visuals and narration using template-driven assembly rules, enabling fully automated end-to-end production without manual editing. In large-scale deployments, LAVES achieves a throughput exceeding one million videos per day, delivering over a 95% reduction in cost compared to current industry-standard approaches while maintaining a high acceptance rate.

AI.tldr: Proposes LAVES, a hierarchical multi-agent framework for scalable, high-fidelity instructional video generation by turning educational problems into executable video scripts; enables fully automated end-to-end production with substantial cost savings and high throughput.
AI.motivation: End-to-end video generation struggles with strict logical rigor, precise knowledge representation, pedagogy, and alignment required for instructional content. There is a need for controllability, procedural fidelity, and cost-effective production.
AI.method: A central Orchestrating Agent coordinates specialized agents—Solution Agent for problem solving, Illustration Agent for executable visualization codes, Narration Agent for pedagogy-driven scripts. Outputs undergo semantic critique, rule-based constraints, and tool-based compilation checks. The system produces a structured executable video script that is deterministically compiled into synchronized visuals and narration via template-driven assembly, avoiding direct pixel synthesis.
AI.result: Reported throughput exceeding one million videos per day and over 95% cost reduction compared to industry-standard approaches, with a high acceptance rate.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that a multi-agent orchestration with critique and checks can achieve scalable, controllable, and high-quality instructional video generation, reducing cost while maintaining fidelity and synchronization; suitable for large-scale educational media production.

title: DiffPlace: Street View Generation via Place-Controllable Diffusion Model Enhancing Place Recognition
summary: Generative models have advanced significantly in realistic image synthesis, with diffusion models excelling in quality and stability. Recent multi-view diffusion models improve 3D-aware street view generation, but they struggle to produce place-aware and background-consistent urban scenes from text, BEV maps, and object bounding boxes. This limits their effectiveness in generating realistic samples for place recognition tasks. To address these challenges, we propose DiffPlace, a novel framework that introduces a place-ID controller to enable place-controllable multi-view image generation. The place-ID controller employs linear projection, perceiver transformer, and contrastive learning to map place-ID embeddings into a fixed CLIP space, allowing the model to synthesize images with consistent background buildings while flexibly modifying foreground objects and weather conditions. Extensive experiments, including quantitative comparisons and augmented training evaluations, demonstrate that DiffPlace outperforms existing methods in both generation quality and training support for visual place recognition. Our results highlight the potential of generative models in enhancing scene-level and place-aware synthesis, providing a valuable approach for improving place recognition in autonomous driving

AI.tldr: Proposes DiffPlace, a place-ID controllable diffusion framework for multi-view image generation with place-consistent backgrounds and flexible foreground changes, enabling improved place recognition in autonomous driving.
AI.motivation: Need for place-aware, background-consistent 3D-aware scene synthesis to support visual place recognition and autonomous driving tasks; existing multi-view diffusion struggles with background consistency and place-controllability.
AI.method: Introduce place-ID controller that maps place-ID embeddings into CLIP space via linear projection, perceiver transformer, and contrastive learning; integrate with diffusion model to achieve place-controllable multi-view generation using text, BEV maps, and bounding boxes; training augmented with place-ID alignment.
AI.result: Outperforms existing methods in generation quality and training support for visual place recognition; demonstrates background building consistency and flexible editing in foreground and weather; quantitative comparisons and augmented training evaluations.
AI.conclusion: DiffPlace demonstrates the potential of place-aware diffusion models for scene-level synthesis; enhances performance of visual place recognition in autonomous driving and provides a framework for place-controlled image synthesis.

title: Exploring Multiple High-Scoring Subspaces in Generative Flow Networks
summary: As a probabilistic sampling framework, Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) show strong potential for constructing complex combinatorial objects through the sequential composition of elementary components. However, existing GFlowNets often suffer from excessive exploration over vast state spaces, leading to over-sampling of low-reward regions and convergence to suboptimal distributions. Effectively biasing GFlowNets toward high-reward solutions remains a non-trivial challenge. In this paper, we propose CMAB-GFN, which integrates a combinatorial multi-armed bandit (CMAB) framework with GFlowNet policies. The CMAB component prunes low-quality actions, yielding compact high-scoring subspaces for exploration. Restricting GFNs to these compact high-scoring subspaces accelerates the discovery of high-value candidates, while the exploration of different subspaces ensures that diversity is not sacrificed. Experimental results on multiple tasks demonstrate that CMAB-GFN generates higher-reward candidates than existing approaches.

AI.tldr: CMAB-GFN biases GFlowNets toward high-reward regions by integrating a combinatorial multi-armed bandit to prune low-quality actions, creating compact high-scoring subspaces for efficient, diverse exploration and improved high-value candidate generation.
AI.motivation: GFlowNets efficiently construct complex combinatorial objects but suffer from excessive exploration over large state spaces, leading to over-sampling of low-reward regions and suboptimal convergence. A bias toward high-reward solutions without sacrificing diversity remains challenging.
AI.method: Introduce a CMAB component that identifies and prunes low-quality actions, restricting GFlowNet exploration to compact high-scoring subspaces. This focuses search on promising regions while maintaining diversity by exploring multiple subspaces.
AI.result: Empirical results on multiple tasks show CMAB-GFN yields higher-reward candidates than existing approaches by combining pruning with constrained, diverse exploration.
AI.conclusion: Pruning via CMAB coupled with GFlowNets improves sample efficiency and quality of generated candidates while preserving diversity; the approach is readily applicable to various GFN-based combinatorial optimization problems.

title: Mask What Matters: Mitigating Object Hallucinations in Multimodal Large Language Models with Object-Aligned Visual Contrastive Decoding
summary: We study object hallucination in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) and improve visual contrastive decoding (VCD) by constructing an object-aligned auxiliary view. We leverage object-centric attention in self-supervised Vision Transformers. In particular, we remove the most salient visual evidence to construct an auxiliary view that disrupts unsupported tokens and produces a stronger contrast signal. Our method is prompt-agnostic, model-agnostic, and can be seamlessly plugged into the existing VCD pipeline with little computation overhead, i.e., a single cacheable forward pass. Empirically, our method demonstrates consistent gains on two popular object hallucination benchmarks across two MLLMs.

AI.tldr: Introduces an object-aligned auxiliary view to improve visual contrastive decoding and reduce object hallucinations in MLLMs, via removing salient evidence to create stronger contrast signals; is model- and prompt-agnostic and integrates with minimal overhead; shows empirical gains on two benchmarks across two MLLMs.
AI.motivation: Object hallucination in multimodal LLMs is a persistent challenge. Existing VCD methods can misinterpret spurious or salient features as objects, leading to hallucinations. A lightweight, generalizable mechanism that strengthens token-precision without requiring model retraining is needed.
AI.method: Construct an object-aligned auxiliary view by removing the most salient visual evidence using object-centric attention from self-supervised Vision Transformers. This perturbation disrupts unsupported tokens and yields a stronger contrast signal for the VCD pipeline. The approach is prompt-agnostic and model-agnostic, and can be plugged into existing VCD pipelines with a single cacheable forward pass.
AI.result: Empirical gains observed on two standard object-hallucination benchmarks across two MLLMs, with consistent improvements in hallucination metrics and minimal computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: The proposed auxiliary view method is effective, generalizable, and lightweight, offering a practical route to mitigate object hallucination in MLLMs without retraining, with potential applicability to broader multimodal robustness challenges.

title: Detecting RLVR Training Data via Structural Convergence of Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) is central to training modern reasoning models, but the undisclosed training data raises concerns about benchmark contamination. Unlike pretraining methods, which optimize models using token-level probabilities, RLVR fine-tunes models based on reward feedback from self-generated reasoning trajectories, making conventional likelihood-based detection methods less effective. We show that RLVR induces a distinctive behavioral signature: prompts encountered during RLVR training result in more rigid and similar generations, while unseen prompts retain greater diversity. We introduce Min-$k$NN Distance, a simple black-box detector that quantifies this collapse by sampling multiple completions for a given prompt and computing the average of the $k$ smallest nearest-neighbor edit distances. Min-$k$NN Distance requires no access to the reference model or token probabilities. Experiments across multiple RLVR-trained reasoning models show that Min-$k$NN Distance reliably distinguishes RL-seen examples from unseen ones and outperforms existing membership inference and RL contamination detection baselines.

AI.tldr: RLVR training induces a distinctive, collapse-like behavior in prompts: the generated texts become more rigid and similar for RLVR-seen prompts, while unseen prompts remain diverse. The paper proposes Min-kNN Distance, a black-box detector that samples multiple completions per prompt and compares them via edit-distance-based nearest-neighbor measures. It requires no model access or token probabilities and outperforms baselines in identifying RL-seen versus unseen prompts.
AI.motivation: Undisclosed training data on RL-based reward fine-tuning (RLVR) risks benchmark contamination. Traditional likelihood-based detection is ineffective because RLVR optimizes rewards over reasoning trajectories rather than token-level probabilities, necessitating a model-agnostic contamination detector.
AI.method: Introduce Min-kNN Distance, a black-box detector. For a given prompt P, sample multiple completions from the model. For each completion, compute its edit distance to its nearest neighbor among the other completions. Collect these nearest-neighbor distances, take the k smallest, and average them to obtain the Min-kNN score. The method requires no access to the reference model or token probabilities.
AI.result: Across multiple RLVR-trained reasoning models, Min-kNN Distance reliably distinguishes RL-seen examples from unseen ones and outperforms existing membership inference and RL contamination detection baselines.
AI.conclusion: Min-kNN Distance provides a simple, model-agnostic tool to detect RL contamination by exploiting the distinctive generation collapse induced by RLVR, enabling more robust integrity checks for benchmark data.

title: Where Bits Matter in World Model Planning: A Paired Mixed-Bit Study for Efficient Spatial Reasoning
summary: Efficient spatial reasoning requires world models that remain reliable under tight precision budgets. We study whether low-bit planning behavior is determined mostly by total bitwidth or by where bits are allocated across modules. Using DINO-WM on the Wall planning task, we run a paired-goal mixed-bit evaluation across uniform, mixed, asymmetric, and layerwise variants under two planner budgets. We observe a consistent three-regime pattern: 8-bit and 6-bit settings remain close to FP16, 3-bit settings collapse, and 4-bit settings are allocation-sensitive. In that transition region, preserving encoder precision improves planning relative to uniform quantization, and near-size asymmetric variants show the same encoder-side direction. In a later strict 22-cell replication with smaller per-cell episode count, the mixed-versus-uniform INT4 sign becomes budget-conditioned, which further highlights the sensitivity of this transition regime. These findings motivate module-aware, budget-aware quantization policies as a broader research direction for efficient spatial reasoning. Code and run artifacts are available at https://github.com/suraj-ranganath/DINO-MBQuant.

AI.tldr: Bit allocation across modules matters more than total bitwidth for low-bit planning in DINO-WM; a three-regime behavior emerges (8/6-bit near FP16, 3-bit collapse, 4-bit allocation-sensitive), with encoder-precision preservation improving performance in the transition region and budget-conditioned behavior in replication; supports module- and budget-aware quantization for efficient spatial reasoning.
AI.motivation: Question whether overall bitwidth or its distribution across model modules governs robust planning under tight precision budgets; aim to understand how low-bit quantization impacts world-model-based spatial reasoning.
AI.method: Paired-goal mixed-bit evaluation of DINO-WM on the Wall planning task across uniform, mixed, asymmetric, and layerwise quantization variants under two planner budgets; analyze performance across 8/6/4/3-bit regimes; second replication with smaller per-cell episode counts to test budget sensitivity; compare mixed versus uniform INT4.
AI.result: Identified a consistent three-regime pattern: 8-bit and 6-bit settings remain close to FP16, 3-bit settings collapse, and 4-bit settings are allocation-sensitive. In the transition region, preserving encoder precision improves planning relative to uniform quantization, and near-size asymmetric variants align with encoder-side benefits. In a later 22-cell replication with fewer episodes, mixed-vs-uniform INT4 becomes budget-conditioned, underscoring sensitivity in the transition regime.
AI.conclusion: Module-aware, budget-aware quantization policies are a promising direction for efficient spatial reasoning; results emphasize that where bits are allocated matters for performance under tight budgets; code and artifacts are available for replication.

title: Partial GFlowNet: Accelerating Convergence in Large State Spaces via Strategic Partitioning
summary: Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) have shown promising potential to generate high-scoring candidates with probability proportional to their rewards. As existing GFlowNets freely explore in state space, they encounter significant convergence challenges when scaling to large state spaces. Addressing this issue, this paper proposes to restrict the exploration of actor. A planner is introduced to partition the entire state space into overlapping partial state spaces. Given their limited size, these partial state spaces allow the actor to efficiently identify subregions with higher rewards. A heuristic strategy is introduced to switch partial regions thus preventing the actor from wasting time exploring fully explored or low-reward partial regions. By iteratively exploring these partial state spaces, the actor learns to converge towards the high-reward subregions within the entire state space. Experiments on several widely used datasets demonstrate that \modelname converges faster than existing works on large state spaces. Furthermore, \modelname not only generates candidates with higher rewards but also significantly improves their diversity.

AI.tldr: Proposes restricting GFlowNets exploration via a planner that partitions the state space into overlapping partial regions, guiding the actor to high-reward subregions and improving convergence, reward quality, and diversity.
AI.motivation: GFlowNets can converge slowly in large state spaces due to unrestricted exploration; a scalable, region-focused strategy is needed to accelerate convergence while maintaining diversity of generated candidates.
AI.method: Introduce a planner that partitions the full state space into overlapping partial state spaces. The actor operates within these partial spaces; a heuristic switches between partial regions to avoid wasting effort on fully explored or low-reward areas. Iterative exploration within partial spaces guides the actor toward high-reward subregions of the full space.
AI.result: Experiments show faster convergence than baseline GFlowNets on large state spaces; generated candidates achieve higher rewards and exhibit significantly improved diversity.
AI.conclusion: Partial-space planning enables scalable, efficient exploration in GFlowNets, yielding higher-quality and more diverse candidates by concentrating search within high-reward subregions.

title: Adaptive Debiasing Tsallis Entropy for Test-Time Adaptation
comment: Accepted for publication at ICLR 2026; 24 pages; 5 figures
summary: Mainstream Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) methods for adapting vision-language models, e.g., CLIP, typically rely on Shannon Entropy (SE) at test time to measure prediction uncertainty and inconsistency. However, since CLIP has a built-in bias from pretraining on highly imbalanced web-crawled data, SE inevitably results in producing biased estimates of uncertainty entropy. To address this issue, we notably find and demonstrate that Tsallis Entropy (TE), a generalized form of SE, is naturally suited for characterizing biased distributions by introducing a non-extensive parameter q, with the performance of SE serving as a lower bound for TE. Building upon this, we generalize TE into Adaptive Debiasing Tsallis Entropy (ADTE) for TTA, customizing a class-specific parameter q^l derived by normalizing the estimated label bias from continuously incoming test instances, for each category. This adaptive approach allows ADTE to accurately select high-confidence views and seamlessly integrate with a label adjustment strategy to enhance adaptation, without introducing distribution-specific hyperparameter tuning. Besides, our investigation reveals that both TE and ADTE can serve as direct, advanced alternatives to SE in TTA, without any other modifications. Experimental results show that ADTE outperforms state-of-the-art methods on ImageNet and its five variants, and achieves the highest average performance on 10 cross-domain benchmarks, regardless of the model architecture or text prompts used. Our code is available at https://github.com/Jinx630/ADTE.

AI.tldr: Proposes Adaptive Debiasing Tsallis Entropy (ADTE) for test-time adaptation (TTA) of CLIP-like vision–language models to mitigate Shannon entropy bias from imbalanced pretraining. Introduces class-specific q^l by normalizing label bias, enabling adaptive view selection and label adjustment without distribution-specific hyperparameters. Empirically, ADTE outperforms state-of-the-art on ImageNet and 10 cross-domain benchmarks across architectures and prompts; code released.
AI.motivation: CLIP and similar models pretrained on highly imbalanced web data yield biased uncertainty estimates when using Shannon entropy for TTA. Tsallis Entropy (TE) generalizes SE via a non-extensive parameter q, with SE as a lower bound of TE, offering a potential remedy for biased distributions. The goal is to develop an adaptive, distribution-agnostic uncertainty measure that improves test-time adaptation performance.
AI.method: Generalize Tsallis Entropy (TE) with a per-class q^l, derived by normalizing estimated label bias from continuously arriving test instances. This Adaptive Debiasing Tsallis Entropy (ADTE) is used for high-confidence view selection and paired with a label adjustment strategy during TTA. The approach avoids distribution-specific hyperparameter tuning, and TE/ADTE can serve as direct alternatives to Shannon Entropy in TTA.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows ADTE outperforms state-of-the-art TTA methods on ImageNet and its variants, achieving the highest average performance across 10 cross-domain benchmarks, regardless of model architecture or text prompts. The study demonstrates that TE and ADTE can serve as effective direct substitutes for SE in TTA.
AI.conclusion: TE and its adaptive variant ADTE are viable, principled alternatives to SE for test-time adaptation in vision-language models. They enable improved uncertainty estimation and view selection under biased distributions, with robust cross-domain performance and minimal hyperparameter tuning. Code is available at the authors’ repository.

title: Hi-SAM: A Hierarchical Structure-Aware Multi-modal Framework for Large-Scale Recommendation
summary: Multi-modal recommendation has gained traction as items possess rich attributes like text and images. Semantic ID-based approaches effectively discretize this information into compact tokens. However, two challenges persist: (1) Suboptimal Tokenization: existing methods (e.g., RQ-VAE) lack disentanglement between shared cross-modal semantics and modality-specific details, causing redundancy or collapse; (2) Architecture-Data Mismatch: vanilla Transformers treat semantic IDs as flat streams, ignoring the hierarchy of user interactions, items, and tokens. Expanding items into multiple tokens amplifies length and noise, biasing attention toward local details over holistic semantics. We propose Hi-SAM, a Hierarchical Structure-Aware Multi-modal framework with two designs: (1) Disentangled Semantic Tokenizer (DST): unifies modalities via geometry-aware alignment and quantizes them via a coarse-to-fine strategy.
