Shared codebooks distill consensus while modality-specific ones recover nuances from residuals, enforced by mutual information minimization; (2) Hierarchical Memory-Anchor Transformer (HMAT): splits positional encoding into inter- and intra-item subspaces via Hierarchical RoPE to restore hierarchy. It inserts Anchor Tokens to condense items into compact memory, retaining details for the current item while accessing history only through compressed summaries. Experiments on real-world datasets show consistent improvements over SOTA baselines, especially in cold-start scenarios. Deployed on a large-scale social platform serving millions of users, Hi-SAM achieved a 6.55% gain in the core online metric.

AI.tldr: Hi-SAM introduces a hierarchical, structure-aware framework for multi-modal recommendation that disentangles semantic tokens and preserves hierarchy in models, leading to improved cold-start performance and strong real-world gains.
AI.motivation: Two key issues hinder performance: suboptimal tokenization that conflates shared semantics with modality-specific details, and architecture-data mismatch where flat token streams ignore interaction hierarchy, causing noise and bias toward local detail.
AI.method: DST (Disentangled Semantic Tokenizer) uses geometry-aware alignment and a coarse-to-fine quantization with shared codebooks for consensus and modality-specific codebooks for residuals, trained with mutual information minimization. HMAT (Hierarchical Memory-Anchor Transformer) splits positional encoding into inter- and intra-item subspaces via Hierarchical RoPE, and introduces Anchor Tokens to condense items into compact memory, enabling history access only through summaries while preserving current-item details.
AI.result: Empirical results show consistent improvements over SOTA baselines across real-world datasets, with particular gains in cold-start scenarios. In a large-scale deployment serving millions of users, Hi-SAM achieved a 6.55% gain on the core online metric.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a unified, hierarchical approach for multi-modal recommendation that improves tokenization and sequence modeling, yielding robust gains in offline benchmarks and real-world metrics, including cold-start performance.

title: Multi UAVs Preflight Planning in a Shared and Dynamic Airspace
summary: Preflight planning for large-scale Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) fleets in dynamic, shared airspace presents significant challenges, including temporal No-Fly Zones (NFZs), heterogeneous vehicle profiles, and strict delivery deadlines. While Multi-Agent Path Finding (MAPF) provides a formal framework, existing methods often lack the scalability and flexibility required for real-world Unmanned Traffic Management (UTM). We propose DTAPP-IICR: a Delivery-Time Aware Prioritized Planning method with Incremental and Iterative Conflict Resolution. Our framework first generates an initial solution by prioritizing missions based on urgency. Secondly, it computes roundtrip trajectories using SFIPP-ST, a novel 4D single-agent planner (Safe Flight Interval Path Planning with Soft and Temporal Constraints). SFIPP-ST handles heterogeneous UAVs, strictly enforces temporal NFZs, and models inter-agent conflicts as soft constraints. Subsequently, an iterative Large Neighborhood Search, guided by a geometric conflict graph, efficiently resolves any residual conflicts. A completeness-preserving directional pruning technique further accelerates the 3D search. On benchmarks with temporal NFZs, DTAPP-IICR achieves near-100% success with fleets of up to 1,000 UAVs and gains up to 50% runtime reduction from pruning, outperforming batch Enhanced Conflict-Based Search in the UTM context. Scaling successfully in realistic city-scale operations where other priority-based methods fail even at moderate deployments, DTAPP-IICR is positioned as a practical and scalable solution for preflight planning in dense, dynamic urban airspace.

AI.tldr: A scalable delivery-time aware planning pipeline for large UAV fleets in dynamic airspace, combining prioritized initial scheduling, a 4D single-agent planner (SFIPP-ST) with hard temporal NFZs and soft inter-agent constraints, and iterative large-neighborhood search guided by a geometric conflict graph, plus directional pruning.
AI.motivation: Preflight planning for large, heterogeneous UAV fleets in dynamic, shared airspace with temporal NFZs and strict deadlines requires scalable, flexible solutions beyond existing MAPF methods and batch ECB in UTM contexts.
AI.method: 1) Urgency-based priority for initial mission ordering. 2) SFIPP-ST, a 4D single-agent planner that enforces temporal NFZs, handles heterogeneous UAVs, and treats inter-agent conflicts as soft constraints. 3) Iterative Large Neighborhood Search (LNS) guided by a geometric conflict graph to resolve residual conflicts. 4) Completeness-preserving directional pruning to accelerate 3D search.
AI.result: DTAPP-IICR achieves near-100% feasibility for fleets up to 1,000 UAVs on benchmarks with temporal NFZs, with up to 50% runtime reduction due to pruning. It outperforms batch Enhanced Conflict-Based Search in the UTM context and scales to city-scale operations where other priority-based methods fail.
AI.conclusion: DTAPP-IICR is presented as a practical, scalable preflight planning solution for dense, dynamic urban airspace, capable of handling heterogeneity, strict temporal constraints, and large fleet sizes.

title: A Generic Framework for Fair Consensus Clustering in Streams
summary:
Consensus clustering seeks to combine multiple clusterings of the same dataset, potentially derived by considering various non-sensitive attributes by different agents in a multi-agent environment, into a single partitioning that best reflects the overall structure of the underlying dataset. Recent work by Chakraborty et al, introduced a fair variant under proportionate fairness and obtained a constant-factor approximation by naively selecting the best closest fair input clustering; however, their offline approach requires storing all input clusterings, which is prohibitively expensive for most large-scale applications.
  In this paper, we initiate the study of fair consensus clustering in the streaming model, where input clusterings arrive sequentially and memory is limited. We design the first constant-factor algorithm that processes the stream while storing only a logarithmic number of inputs. En route, we introduce a new generic algorithmic framework that integrates closest fair clustering with cluster fitting, yielding improved approximation guarantees not only in the streaming setting but also when revisited offline. Furthermore, the framework is fairness-agnostic: it applies to any fairness definition for which an approximately close fair clustering can be computed efficiently. Finally, we extend our methods to the more general k-median consensus clustering problem.

AI.tldr: Introduces the first streaming fair consensus clustering algorithm with constant-factor approximation using logarithmic memory; extends to k-median; provides a generic, fairness-agnostic framework integrating closest fair clustering with cluster fitting.
AI.motivation: Address memory-constrained streaming setting for consensus clustering with fairness, overcoming the offline bottleneck of storing all input clusterings; ensure fairness definitions can be efficiently approximated.
AI.method: Develops a constant-factor streaming algorithm storing only polylogarithmic inputs; proposes a generic framework combining closest fair clustering with cluster fitting; proves approximation guarantees; extends to k-median.
AI.result: Achieves constant-factor approximation with logarithmic storage; framework improves offline and streaming results; fairness-agnostic; applicable to general fairness definitions.
AI.conclusion: Framework generalizes to various fairness notions that admit efficient approximate closest fair clustering; practical for large-scale streaming data; broadens consensus clustering with fairness in streaming and k-median variants.

title: Code2Worlds: Empowering Coding LLMs for 4D World Generation
summary: Achieving spatial intelligence requires moving beyond visual plausibility to build world simulators grounded in physical laws. While coding LLMs have advanced static 3D scene generation, extending this paradigm to 4D dynamics remains a critical frontier. This task presents two fundamental challenges: multi-scale context entanglement, where monolithic generation fails to balance local object structures with global environmental layouts; and a semantic-physical execution gap, where open-loop code generation leads to physical hallucinations lacking dynamic fidelity. We introduce Code2Worlds, a framework that formulates 4D generation as language-to-simulation code generation. First, we propose a dual-stream architecture that disentangles retrieval-augmented object generation from hierarchical environmental orchestration. Second, to ensure dynamic fidelity, we establish a physics-aware closed-loop mechanism in which a PostProcess Agent scripts dynamics, coupled with a VLM-Motion Critic that performs self-reflection to iteratively refine simulation code. Evaluations on the Code4D benchmark show Code2Worlds outperforms baselines with a 41% SGS gain and 49% higher Richness, while uniquely generating physics-aware dynamics absent in prior static methods. Code: https://github.com/AIGeeksGroup/Code2Worlds. Website: https://aigeeksgroup.github.io/Code2Worlds.

AI.tldr: Proposes Code2Worlds, a language-to-simulation-code framework for 4D dynamic scene generation, addressing multi-scale context entanglement and physics fidelity with a dual-stream retrieval + hierarchical orchestration and a closed-loop physics-aware feedback via PostProcess Agent and VLM-Motion Critic; shows improvement on Code4D benchmark (SGS gain 41%, Richness +49%).
AI.motivation: Current static 3D scene generation by LLMs struggles to produce faithful 4D dynamics due to entangled local/global context and the semantic-physical execution gap; need grounded, dynamic world simulators.
AI.method: Dual-stream architecture separating retrieval-augmented object generation from hierarchical environmental orchestration; physics-aware closed-loop using a PostProcess Agent to script dynamics and a VLM-Motion Critic for self-reflection to iteratively refine simulation code.
AI.result: On Code4D benchmark, Code2Worlds outperforms baselines with 41% SGS gain and 49% higher Richness, uniquely generating physics-aware dynamics not present in prior static methods.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of language-to-simulation-code generation to produce dynamic 4D worlds, offering architecture and evaluation that bridge NLP and physics in world modeling; code released for reproducibility.

title: PuYun-LDM: A Latent Diffusion Model for High-Resolution Ensemble Weather Forecasts
summary: Latent diffusion models (LDMs) suffer from limited diffusability in high-resolution (<=0.25°) ensemble weather forecasting, where diffusability characterizes how easily a latent data distribution can be modeled by a diffusion process. Unlike natural image fields, meteorological fields lack task-agnostic foundation models and explicit semantic structures, making VFM-based regularization inapplicable. Moreover, existing frequency-based approaches impose identical spectral regularization across channels under a homogeneity assumption, which leads to uneven regularization strength under the inter-variable spectral heterogeneity in multivariate meteorological data. To address these challenges, we propose a 3D Masked AutoEncoder (3D-MAE) that encodes weather-state evolution features as an additional conditioning for the diffusion model, together with a Variable-Aware Masked Frequency Modeling (VA-MFM) strategy that adaptively selects thresholds based on the spectral energy distribution of each variable. Together, we propose PuYun-LDM, which enhances latent diffusability and achieves superior performance to ENS at short lead times while remaining comparable to ENS at longer horizons. PuYun-LDM generates a 15-day global forecast with a 6-hour temporal resolution in five minutes on a single NVIDIA H200 GPU, while ensemble forecasts can be efficiently produced in parallel.

AI.tldr: PuYun-LDM is a diffusion-based framework for high-resolution ensemble weather forecasting, integrating a 3D-MAE conditioned latent diffusion model with Variable-Aware Masked Frequency Modeling to adaptively regularize spectral energy per variable, enabling fast, accurate 15-day forecasts with parallelizable ensembles.
AI.motivation: Latent diffusion models have limited diffusability for high-resolution ensemble weather forecasting. Meteorological fields lack task-agnostic foundation models and explicit semantic structures, making VFM-based regularization ineffective. Additionally, existing spectral regularization assumes channel homogeneity, which is inappropriate given inter-variable spectral heterogeneity in multivariate meteorological data.
AI.method: Introduce a 3D Masked AutoEncoder (3D-MAE) to encode weather-state evolution features as conditioning for the diffusion model. Propose Variable-Aware Masked Frequency Modeling (VA-MFM) to adaptively select spectral thresholds based on each variable's spectral energy distribution. Integrate these components into PuYun-LDM to enhance latent diffusability.
AI.result: PuYun-LDM improves latent diffusability and outperforms ENS at short lead times, while remaining comparable to ENS at longer horizons. It enables a 15-day global forecast with 6-hour temporal resolution, produced in five minutes on a single NVIDIA H200 GPU, with ensemble forecasts that can be efficiently parallelized.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework addresses the lack of task-agnostic foundation models and inter-variable spectral heterogeneity in meteorology, delivering fast, accurate high-resolution forecasts and efficient ensemble generation.

title: Calibrating an Imperfect Auxiliary Predictor for Unobserved No-Purchase Choice
summary: Firms typically cannot observe key consumer actions: whether customers buy from a competitor, choose not to buy, or even fully consider the firm's offer. This missing outside-option information makes market-size and preference estimation difficult even in simple multinomial logit (MNL) models, and it is a central obstacle in practice when only transaction data are recorded. Existing approaches often rely on auxiliary market-share, aggregated, or cross-market data. We study a complementary setting in which a black-box auxiliary predictor provides outside-option probabilities, but is potentially biased or miscalibrated because it was trained in a different channel, period, or population, or produced by an external machine-learning system. We develop calibration methods that turn such imperfect predictions into statistically valid no-purchase estimates using purchase-only data from the focal environment. First, under affine miscalibration in logit space, we show that a simple regression identifies outside-option utility parameters and yields consistent recovery of no-purchase probabilities without collecting new labels for no-purchase events. Second, under a weaker nearly monotone condition, we propose a rank-based calibration method and derive finite-sample error bounds that cleanly separate auxiliary-predictor quality from first-stage utility-learning error over observed in-set choices. Our analysis also translates estimation error into downstream decision quality for assortment optimization, quantifying how calibration accuracy affects revenue performance. The bounds provide explicit dependence on predictor alignment and utility-learning error, clarifying when each source dominates. Numerical experiments demonstrate improvements in no-purchase estimation and downstream assortment decisions, and we discuss robust aggregation extensions for combining multiple auxiliary predictors.

AI.tldr: Calibration methods to recover no-purchase probabilities in multinomial logit models using biased auxiliary outside-option predictions; affine miscalibration and a rank-based alternative with finite-sample bounds, plus implications for assortment decisions.
AI.motivation: Missing outside-option information makes market-size and preference estimation difficult in simple MNL, particularly when only transaction data are observed. An auxiliary predictor may provide outside-option probabilities but can be biased or miscalibrated. The work enables turning imperfect external predictions into valid no-purchase estimates using focal-environment purchase data.
AI.method: Two calibration approaches: (1) affine miscalibration in logit space enabling simple regression to identify outside-option utility parameters and consistently recover no-purchase probabilities without new no-purchase labels; (2) under a weaker nearly monotone condition, a rank-based calibration with finite-sample error bounds that separate auxiliary-predictor quality from utility-learning error. The analysis connects estimation error to downstream revenue in assortment optimization and discusses aggregation extensions for combining multiple predictors.
AI.result: Demonstrates identification and consistency under affine miscalibration; derives finite-sample error bounds showing how predictor quality and utility-learning error affect calibration; translates estimation error into revenue impact for assortment decisions; numerical experiments show improved no-purchase estimates and better downstream decisions; discusses robust aggregation for multiple auxiliary predictors.
AI.conclusion: Calibration converts imperfect external predictors into statistically valid no-purchase estimates, improving revenue outcomes for assortment optimization. The results clarify when predictor quality dominates and offer practical aggregation extensions for combining multiple auxiliary predictors.

title: Light4D: Training-Free Extreme Viewpoint 4D Video Relighting
summary: Recent advances in diffusion-based generative models have established a new paradigm for image and video relighting. However, extending these capabilities to 4D relighting remains challenging, due primarily to the scarcity of paired 4D relighting training data and the difficulty of maintaining temporal consistency across extreme viewpoints. In this work, we propose Light4D, a novel training-free framework designed to synthesize consistent 4D videos under target illumination, even under extreme viewpoint changes. First, we introduce Disentangled Flow Guidance, a time-aware strategy that effectively injects lighting control into the latent space while preserving geometric integrity. Second, to reinforce temporal consistency, we develop Temporal Consistent Attention within the IC-Light architecture and further incorporate deterministic regularization to eliminate appearance flickering. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves competitive performance in temporal consistency and lighting fidelity, robustly handling camera rotations from -90 to 90. Code: https://github.com/AIGeeksGroup/Light4D. Website: https://aigeeksgroup.github.io/Light4D.

AI.tldr: A training-free diffusion-based framework (Light4D) enables consistent 4D relighting under extreme viewpoints by injecting lighting control into latent space and enforcing temporal consistency with attention and regularization; it achieves competitive lighting fidelity and stability across -90° to 90° camera rotations.
AI.motivation: 4D relighting with diffusion models is hampered by scarce paired training data and poor temporal consistency across extreme viewpoints; a training-free approach is needed to render temporally coherent 4D relighting under large view changes.
AI.method: Light4D introduces Disentangled Flow Guidance (time-aware, injects lighting control into latent space while preserving geometry) and Temporal Consistent Attention within the IC-Light architecture, plus deterministic regularization to reduce flicker; the framework is training-free.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show competitive temporal consistency and lighting fidelity, robust to camera rotations from -90° to 90°; code and demo are provided.
AI.conclusion: Light4D offers a practical, training-free solution for coherent 4D relighting under wide viewpoint changes, achieving strong temporal stability and lighting accuracy while remaining accessible via code and project page.

title: Predicting LLM Output Length via Entropy-Guided Representations
summary: The long-tailed distribution of sequence lengths in LLM serving and reinforcement learning (RL) sampling causes significant computational waste due to excessive padding in batched inference. Existing methods rely on auxiliary models for static length prediction, but they incur high overhead, generalize poorly, and fail in stochastic "one-to-many" sampling scenarios. We introduce a lightweight framework that reuses the main model's internal hidden states for efficient length prediction. Our framework features two core components: 1) Entropy-Guided Token Pooling (EGTP), which uses on-the-fly activations and token entropy for highly accurate static prediction with negligible cost, and 2) Progressive Length Prediction (PLP), which dynamically estimates the remaining length at each decoding step to handle stochastic generation. To validate our approach, we build and release ForeLen, a comprehensive benchmark with long-sequence, Chain-of-Thought, and RL data. On ForeLen, EGTP achieves state-of-the-art accuracy, reducing MAE by 29.16\% over the best baseline. Integrating our methods with a length-aware scheduler yields significant end-to-end throughput gains. Our work provides a new technical and evaluation baseline for efficient LLM inference.

AI.tldr: A lightweight two-component framework (EGTP + PLP) reuses model hidden states to predict sequence length for LLM inference, enabling better batching and throughput; validated on ForeLen with 29.16% MAE reduction and throughput gains.
AI.motivation: Address the long-tailed distribution of sequence lengths in LLM serving and RL sampling, which causes significant padding waste. Existing static length predictors incur overhead, generalization gaps, and fail under stochastic one-to-many sampling. A low-cost, generalizable method that leverages the main model's hidden states is needed.
AI.method: Entrophy-Guided Token Pooling (EGTP): uses on-the-fly activations and token entropy for accurate static length prediction with negligible cost. Progressive Length Prediction (PLP): dynamically estimates remaining length at each decoding step to handle stochastic generation. Reuses the main model's internal states to avoid extra overhead. ForeLen is released as a benchmark for long-sequence, Chain-of-Thought, and RL data.
AI.result: On ForeLen, EGTP achieves state-of-the-art accuracy, reducing MAE by 29.16% over the best baseline. Integrating the methods with a length-aware scheduler yields significant end-to-end throughput gains. ForeLen provides a new technical and evaluation baseline for efficient LLM inference.
AI.conclusion: The work introduces a practical framework for predicting sequence lengths with minimal overhead and demonstrates its effectiveness on a dedicated benchmark, establishing a new baseline for efficient LLM inference and proving applicability across long sequences, reasoning steps, and RL sampling.

title: RooflineBench: A Benchmarking Framework for On-Device LLMs via Roofline Analysis
summary: The transition toward localized intelligence through Small Language Models (SLMs) has intensified the need for rigorous performance characterization on resource-constrained edge hardware. However, objectively measuring the theoretical performance ceilings of diverse architectures across heterogeneous platforms remains a formidable challenge. In this work, we propose a systematic framework based on the Roofline model that unifies architectural primitives and hardware constraints through the lens of operational intensity (OI). By defining an inference-potential region, we introduce the Relative Inference Potential as a novel metric to compare efficiency differences between Large Language Models (LLMs) on the same hardware substrate. Extensive empirical analysis across diverse compute tiers reveals that variations in performance and OI are significantly influenced by sequence length. We further identify a critical regression in OI as model depth increases. Additionally, our findings highlight an efficiency trap induced by hardware heterogeneity and demonstrate how structural refinements, such as Multi-head Latent Attention (M LA), can effectively unlock latent inference potential across various hardware substrates. These insights provide actionable directions for hardware-software co-design to align neural structures with physical constraints in on-device intelligence. The released code is available in the Appendix C.

AI.tldr: Unified Roofline-based framework for evaluating SLM/LLM inference on edge hardware using operational intensity and a new Relative Inference Potential metric; reveals sequence length and depth effects, heterogeneity-induced efficiency traps, and potential gains from Multi-head Latent Attention; provides code in Appendix C.
AI.motivation: There is a need to objectively characterize theoretical performance ceilings of diverse architectures on resource-constrained edge devices in order to guide hardware-software co-design for on-device intelligence.
AI.method: Adopts the Roofline model to relate compute and memory bounds through operational intensity (OI). Defines an inference-potential region and introduces Relative Inference Potential to compare LLM efficiency on the same hardware. Conducts extensive empirical analysis across diverse compute tiers, varying sequence lengths and model depth, and evaluates a refinement—Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA)—across hardware substrates.
AI.result: Found that performance and OI are highly sensitive to sequence length; OI exhibits a notable regression as model depth increases; hardware heterogeneity creates an efficiency trap; MLA can unlock latent inference potential across different hardware substrates.
AI.conclusion: Provides actionable insights for hardware-software co-design to align neural architectures with physical constraints for on-device intelligence; released code in Appendix C to enable reproducibility.

title: Efficient Segment Anything with Depth-Aware Fusion and Limited Training Data
summary: Segment Anything Models (SAM) achieve impressive universal segmentation performance but require massive datasets (e.g., 11M images) and rely solely on RGB inputs. Recent efficient variants reduce computation but still depend on large-scale training. We propose a lightweight RGB-D fusion framework that augments EfficientViT-SAM with monocular depth priors. Depth maps are generated with a pretrained estimator and fused mid-level with RGB features through a dedicated depth encoder. Trained on only 11.2k samples (less than 0.1\% of SA-1B), our method achieves higher accuracy than EfficientViT-SAM, showing that depth cues provide strong geometric priors for segmentation.

AI.tldr: A lightweight RGB-D fusion framework enhances EfficientViT-SAM by incorporating monocular depth priors, achieving higher segmentation accuracy with only 11.2k training samples (0.1% of SA-1B).
AI.motivation: Reduce data and computation needs of large segmentation models like SAM by leveraging geometric depth cues to provide strong priors.
AI.method: Attach a depth encoder to EfficientViT-SAM; generate depth maps with a pretrained monocular depth estimator; fuse depth features with RGB features at mid-level; train on a small labeled set (11.2k samples).
AI.result: Outperforms EfficientViT-SAM in accuracy, demonstrating depth cues offer robust geometric priors and enabling substantial data efficiency.
AI.conclusion: RGB-D fusion with depth priors is a viable path to maintain or improve segmentation performance while dramatically reducing training data requirements.

title: Revis: Sparse Latent Steering to Mitigate Object Hallucination in Large Vision-Language Models
summary: Despite the advanced capabilities of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs), they frequently suffer from object hallucination. One reason is that visual features and pretrained textual representations often become intertwined in the deeper network layers. To address this, we propose REVIS, a training-free framework designed to explicitly re-activate this suppressed visual information. Rooted in latent space geometry, REVIS extracts the pure visual information vector via orthogonal projection and employs a calibrated strategy to perform sparse intervention only at the precise depth where suppression occurs. This surgical approach effectively restores visual information with minimal computational cost. Empirical evaluations on standard benchmarks demonstrate that REVIS reduces object hallucination rates by approximately 19% compared to state-of-the-art baselines, while preserving general reasoning capabilities.

AI.tldr: REVIS is a training-free framework that reactivates suppressed visual information in LVLMs to reduce object hallucination by ~19% with low computational cost.
AI.motivation: Object hallucination in LVLMs arises from entanglement of visual features and pretrained textual representations in deeper layers; disentangle visual info without retraining.
AI.method: REVIS uses latent-space orthogonal projection to extract a pure visual information vector and applies calibrated, sparse interventions at the depth where suppression occurs, without training.
AI.result: On standard benchmarks, REVIS reduces object hallucination rates by about 19% compared with state-of-the-art baselines while preserving general reasoning capabilities.
AI.conclusion: A training-free, geometry-based approach that restores visual information with minimal cost and preserves reasoning performance in LVLMs.

title: Unifying Stable Optimization and Reference Regularization in RLHF
summary: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) has advanced alignment capabilities significantly but remains hindered by two core challenges: \textbf{reward hacking} and \textbf{stable optimization}. Current solutions independently address these issues through separate regularization strategies, specifically a KL-divergence penalty against a supervised fine-tuned model ($π_0$) to mitigate reward hacking, and policy ratio clipping towards the current policy ($π_t$) to promote stable alignment. However, the implicit trade-off arising from simultaneously regularizing towards both $π_0$ and $π_t$ remains under-explored. In this paper, we introduce a unified regularization approach that explicitly balances the objectives of preventing reward hacking and maintaining stable policy updates. Our simple yet principled alignment objective yields a weighted supervised fine-tuning loss with a superior trade-off, which demonstrably improves both alignment results and implementation complexity. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks validate that our method consistently outperforms RLHF and online preference learning methods, achieving enhanced alignment performance and stability.

AI.tldr: A unified regularization for RLHF balancing reward hacking prevention and stable policy updates, via a weighted supervised fine-tuning loss, outperforming RLHF and online preference learning.
AI.motivation: RLHF struggles with reward hacking and unstable optimization; existing remedies address these separately and their combined trade-off is under-explored; a single objective could improve both alignment quality and training stability while reducing complexity.
AI.method: Introduce a unified regularization that jointly regularizes towards the supervised fine-tuned model and towards the current policy, yielding a weighted supervised fine-tuning loss that trades off prevention of reward hacking and stability of updates.
AI.result: Experiments across diverse benchmarks show consistent improvements in alignment performance and stability, outperforming RLHF and online preference learning, with simpler implementation.
AI.conclusion: Unified regularization achieves a favorable balance between alignment quality and training stability, with lower complexity and broad empirical gains.

title: How to Sample High Quality 3D Fractals for Action Recognition Pre-Training?
comment: 12 pages, 6 figures. To be published in VISAPP
summary: Synthetic datasets are being recognized in the deep learning realm as a valuable alternative to exhaustively labeled real data. One such synthetic data generation method is Formula Driven Supervised Learning (FDSL), which can provide an infinite number of perfectly labeled data through a formula driven approach, such as fractals or contours. FDSL does not have common drawbacks like manual labor, privacy and other ethical concerns. In this work we generate 3D fractals using 3D Iterated Function Systems (IFS) for pre-training an action recognition model. The fractals are temporally transformed to form a video that is used as a pre-training dataset for downstream task of action recognition. We find that standard methods of generating fractals are slow and produce degenerate 3D fractals. Therefore, we systematically explore alternative ways of generating fractals and finds that overly-restrictive approaches, while generating aesthetically pleasing fractals, are detrimental for downstream task performance. We propose a novel method, Targeted Smart Filtering, to address both the generation speed and fractal diversity issue. The method reports roughly 100 times faster sampling speed and achieves superior downstream performance against other 3D fractal filtering methods.

AI.tldr: Using synthetic 3D fractals generated by 3D IFS as pretraining data for action recognition, the authors tackle slow/degenerate fractal generation with a new Targeted Smart Filtering method, achieving ~100x faster sampling and better downstream performance than existing fractal filters.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on labor-intensive real labels and address privacy/ethics concerns in data collection by leveraging formula-driven synthetic datasets (FDSL). Apply 3D fractals to create labeled video data to pretrain action recognition models.
AI.method: Systematically evaluate existing fractal generation approaches, identify that overly-restrictive methods hinder downstream task performance, and introduce Targeted Smart Filtering to balance speed and diversity. Generate temporally transformed 3D fractals into videos for pretraining a deep action recognition model, and compare against other 3D fractal filtering methods.
AI.result: Targeted Smart Filtering yields ~100x faster sampling and superior downstream performance compared with other 3D fractal filtering methods.
AI.conclusion: Targeted Smart Filtering effectively accelerates 3D fractal data generation and enhances action recognition pretraining, supporting the viability of FDSL for downstream video understanding tasks.

title: Prototype Transformer: Towards Language Model Architectures Interpretable by Design
comment: Preprint under review. Equal contribution: Yordan Yordanov and Matteo Forasassi. 39 pages, 25 figures, 22 tables
summary: While state-of-the-art language models (LMs) surpass the vast majority of humans in certain domains, their reasoning remains largely opaque, undermining trust in their output. Furthermore, while autoregressive LMs can output explicit reasoning, their true reasoning process is opaque, which introduces risks like deception and hallucination. In this work, we introduce the Prototype Transformer (ProtoT) -- an autoregressive LM architecture based on prototypes (parameter vectors), posed as an alternative to the standard self-attention-based transformers. ProtoT works by means of two-way communication between the input sequence and the prototypes, and we show that this leads to the prototypes automatically capturing nameable concepts (e.g. "woman") during training. They provide the potential to interpret the model's reasoning and allow for targeted edits of its behavior. Furthermore, by design, the prototypes create communication channels that aggregate contextual information at different time scales, aiding interpretability. In terms of computation scalability, ProtoT scales linearly with sequence length vs the quadratic scalability of SOTA self-attention transformers. Compared to baselines, ProtoT scales well with model and data size, and performs well on text generation and downstream tasks (GLUE). ProtoT exhibits robustness to input perturbations on par or better than some baselines, but differs from them by providing interpretable pathways showing how robustness and sensitivity arises. Reaching close to the performance of state-of-the-art architectures, ProtoT paves the way to creating well-performing autoregressive LMs interpretable by design.

AI.tldr: Prototype Transformer (ProtoT) introduces prototype-based autoregressive LMs with two-way input-prototype communication, enabling interpretability, targeted edits, and linear-scaling computation; prototypes capture nameable concepts and provide interpretable robustness pathways while achieving competitive performance on text generation and GLUE.
AI.motivation: Address opacity and deception risks in autoregressive LMs by enabling interpretable reasoning, while also improving robustness and scalability.
AI.method: ProtoT uses prototypes as parameter vectors with two-way communication between the input sequence and prototypes. During training, prototypes automatically capture nameable concepts (e.g., 'woman'). Prototypes form communication channels that aggregate contextual information at different time scales, enabling interpretability and targeted behavior edits. The architecture scales linearly with sequence length, unlike self-attention's quadratic scaling.
AI.result: ProtoT is competitive with state-of-the-art architectures, performing well on text generation and GLUE tasks. It exhibits robustness to input perturbations comparable to or better than some baselines and provides interpretable pathways illustrating how robustness and sensitivity arise.
AI.conclusion: ProtoT demonstrates that it is possible to design autoregressive LMs that are interpretable by design, with scalable computation and the potential for targeted behavior edits, paving the way for more trustworthy and robust language models.

title: Adaptive Milestone Reward for GUI Agents
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a mainstream paradigm for training Mobile GUI Agents, yet it struggles with the temporal credit assignment problem inherent in long-horizon tasks. A primary challenge lies in the trade-off between reward fidelity and density: outcome reward offers high fidelity but suffers from signal sparsity, while process reward provides dense supervision but remains prone to bias and reward hacking. To resolve this conflict, we propose the Adaptive Milestone Reward (ADMIRE) mechanism. ADMIRE constructs a verifiable, adaptive reward system by anchoring trajectory to milestones, which are dynamically distilled from successful explorations. Crucially, ADMIRE integrates an asymmetric credit assignment strategy that denoises successful trajectories and scaffolds failed trajectories. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ADMIRE consistently yields over 10% absolute improvement in success rate across different base models on AndroidWorld. Moreover, the method exhibits robust generalizability, achieving strong performance across diverse RL algorithms and heterogeneous environments such as web navigation and embodied tasks.

AI.tldr: ADMIRE introduces an adaptive milestone reward to address credit assignment in long-horizon RL tasks by anchoring trajectories to dynamically distilled milestones and applying asymmetric credit assignment to denoise successful trajectories and scaffold failed ones.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon RL tasks suffer from the trade-off between high-fidelity but sparse outcome rewards and dense but biased process rewards. There is a need for a reward signal that is both informative and robust to bias/hacking while enabling scalable credit assignment.
AI.method: ADMIRE constructs verifiable milestones tailored to successful explorations, uses them as anchor points in trajectories, and employs an asymmetric credit assignment scheme that denoises good trajectories and aids learning from bad ones. It is evaluated across AndroidWorld and generalizes to web navigation and embodied tasks with multiple baseline RL algorithms.
AI.result: Empirical results show >10% absolute improvement in success rate over different base models on AndroidWorld; strong generalization across algorithms and environments.
AI.conclusion: ADMIRE provides a robust adaptive reward framework for long-horizon RL by combining milestone-based anchoring with asymmetric credit assignment, improving success rates and generalization across tasks.

title: Talk2DM: Enabling Natural Language Querying and Commonsense Reasoning for Vehicle-Road-Cloud Integrated Dynamic Maps with Large Language Models
comment: Submitted to IEEE TITS. Under review
summary: Dynamic maps (DM) serve as the fundamental information infrastructure for vehicle-road-cloud (VRC) cooperative autonomous driving in China and Japan. By providing comprehensive traffic scene representations, DM overcome the limitations of standalone autonomous driving systems (ADS), such as physical occlusions. Although DM-enhanced ADS have been successfully deployed in real-world applications in Japan, existing DM systems still lack a natural-language-supported (NLS) human interface, which could substantially enhance human-DM interaction. To address this gap, this paper introduces VRCsim, a VRC cooperative perception (CP) simulation framework designed to generate streaming VRC-CP data. Based on VRCsim, we construct a question-answering data set, VRC-QA, focused on spatial querying and reasoning in mixed-traffic scenes. Building upon VRCsim and VRC-QA, we further propose Talk2DM, a plug-and-play module that extends VRC-DM systems with NLS querying and commonsense reasoning capabilities. Talk2DM is built upon a novel chain-of-prompt (CoP) mechanism that progressively integrates human-defined rules with the commonsense knowledge of large language models (LLMs). Experiments on VRC-QA show that Talk2DM can seamlessly switch across different LLMs while maintaining high NLS query accuracy, demonstrating strong generalization capability. Although larger models tend to achieve higher accuracy, they incur significant efficiency degradation. Our results reveal that Talk2DM, powered by Qwen3:8B, Gemma3:27B, and GPT-oss models, achieves over 93\% NLS query accuracy with an average response time of only 2-5 seconds, indicating strong practical potential.

AI.tldr: A framework and dataset for natural-language querying of dynamic- maps in VRC autonomous driving. It introduces VRCsim to simulate VRC-CP data, VRC-QA for spatial query reasoning, and Talk2DM with a chain-of-prompt mechanism to fuse human rules and LLM commonsense. It reports strong generalization across LLMs with over 93% NLS query accuracy and 2–5 s latency, highlighting practicality and trade-offs between model size and speed.
AI.motivation: DM-enabled ADS in China/Japan lack natural-language interfaces; need for realistic data and evaluation for human–DM interaction; enable seamless querying of VRC dynamic maps through LLMs.
AI.method: (1) VRCsim to generate streaming VRC-CP data; (2) VRC-QA dataset focusing on spatial querying and reasoning in mixed-traffic scenes; (3) Talk2DM plug‑and‑play module with a chain-of-prompt that progressively integrates hand-crafted rules with LLM commonsense knowledge; (4) evaluation across multiple LLMs (Qwen3:8B, Gemma3:27B, GPT-oss) measuring NLS query accuracy and response time.
AI.result: Talk2DM achieves over 93% NLS query accuracy with 2–5 s average response time and can switch across LLMs without sacrificing performance; larger models improve accuracy but incur latency penalties.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates strong generalization and practical potential for NL-based human–DM interaction in VRC-DM systems. Highlights a favorable accuracy-speed trade-off and the viability of a CoP-driven approach, while leaving open questions on real-world deployment, dataset realism, and safety.

title: PASCAL: A Phase-Aware Scheduling Algorithm for Serving Reasoning-based Large Language Models
comment: Accepted for publication at the 32nd IEEE International Symposium on High-Performance Computer Architecture (HPCA-32), 2026
summary: The emergence of reasoning-based LLMs leveraging Chain-of-Thought (CoT) inference introduces new serving challenges, as their extended reasoning phases delay user-visible output and inflate Time-To-First-Token (TTFT). Existing LLM serving frameworks fail to distinguish between reasoning and answering phases, leading to performance degradation under GPU memory constraints. We present PASCAL, a phase-aware scheduling algorithm that prioritizes reasoning to reduce TTFT while using controlled preemption and token pacing during answering to preserve Quality-of-Experience (QoE). Our hierarchical scheduler combines instance-level placement with intra-instance execution and enables dynamic migration at phase boundaries to balance load and reduce interference. Across benchmarks using DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B, PASCAL reduces tail TTFT by up to 72% while maintaining answering phase SLO attainment, demonstrating the importance of phase-aware scheduling for reasoning-based LLM deployment.

AI.tldr: Phase-aware scheduling (PASCAL) prioritizes reasoning in LLM serving to reduce TTFT tails, using hierarchical placement, intra-instance execution, and phase-boundary migration with token pacing during answering; achieves up to 72% tail TTFT reduction without sacrificing answer-time SLA on large models.
AI.motivation: Reasoning-based LLMs with Chain-of-Thought extend reasoning phases, delaying user-visible output and increasing Time-To-First-Token. Existing serving frameworks treat all phases equally, causing memory and interference issues under GPU constraints.
AI.method: Introduce PASCAL, a phase-aware scheduler with hierarchical placement (instance-level) and intra-instance execution, dynamic migration at phase boundaries, and mechanisms to preemptively manage reasoning vs. answering. Prioritize reasoning, apply controlled preemption and token pacing during answering to preserve QoE.
AI.result: In benchmarks with DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B, PASCAL reduces tail TTFT by up to 72% while maintaining answering phase SLO attainment, demonstrating effective phase-aware scheduling for reasoning-based LLM deployment.
AI.conclusion: Phase-aware scheduling is essential for efficient deployment of reasoning-based LLMs; explicit separation and management of reasoning vs. answering phases can significantly improve tail latency and QoE under memory constraints.

title: WorldTree: Towards 4D Dynamic Worlds from Monocular Video using Tree-Chains
summary: Dynamic reconstruction has achieved remarkable progress, but there remain challenges in monocular input for more practical applications. The prevailing works attempt to construct efficient motion representations, but lack a unified spatiotemporal decomposition framework, suffering from either holistic temporal optimization or coupled hierarchical spatial composition. To this end, we propose WorldTree, a unified framework comprising Temporal Partition Tree (TPT) that enables coarse-to-fine optimization based on the inheritance-based partition tree structure for hierarchical temporal decomposition, and Spatial Ancestral Chains (SAC) that recursively query ancestral hierarchical structure to provide complementary spatial dynamics while specializing motion representations across ancestral nodes. Experimental results on different datasets indicate that our proposed method achieves 8.26% improvement of LPIPS on NVIDIA-LS and 9.09% improvement of mLPIPS on DyCheck compared to the second-best method. Code: https://github.com/iCVTEAM/WorldTree.

AI.tldr: WorldTree offers a unified spatiotemporal decomposition for monocular dynamic reconstruction through Temporal Partition Tree (coarse-to-fine temporal optimization) and Spatial Ancestral Chains (recursive ancestral spatial dynamics), achieving state-of-the-art perceptual gains.
AI.motivation: Current monocular dynamic reconstruction methods struggle because they optimize either over time globally or rely on fixed spatial hierarchies; a unified, inheritance-based framework can better capture multi-scale temporal variation and spatial motion dependencies.
AI.method: Propose WorldTree with Temporal Partition Tree (TPT) enabling inheritance-based temporal partitioning and coarse-to-fine optimization; Spatial Ancestral Chains (SAC) that recursively query ancestral spatial structure to provide complementary dynamics and specialize representations across ancestral nodes.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on NVIDIA-LS and DyCheck shows 8.26% LPIPS improvement and 9.09% mLPIPS improvement over the second-best method; code released at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: WorldTree delivers a unified, scalable framework for monocular dynamic reconstruction with improved perceptual quality, validating the benefits of hierarchical spatiotemporal decomposition.

title: Intelligent AI Delegation
summary: AI agents are able to tackle increasingly complex tasks. To achieve more ambitious goals, AI agents need to be able to meaningfully decompose problems into manageable sub-components, and safely delegate their completion across to other AI agents and humans alike. Yet, existing task decomposition and delegation methods rely on simple heuristics, and are not able to dynamically adapt to environmental changes and robustly handle unexpected failures. Here we propose an adaptive framework for intelligent AI delegation - a sequence of decisions involving task allocation, that also incorporates transfer of authority, responsibility, accountability, clear specifications regarding roles and boundaries, clarity of intent, and mechanisms for establishing trust between the two (or more) parties. The proposed framework is applicable to both human and AI delegators and delegatees in complex delegation networks, aiming to inform the development of protocols in the emerging agentic web.

AI.tldr: Proposes an adaptive, governance-focused framework for task delegation among AI agents and humans, emphasizing authority transfer, accountability, clear roles, and trust to enable robust, dynamic problem decomposition in agentic networks.
AI.motivation: Current task decomposition/delegation methods rely on static heuristics and fail to adapt to changing environments or handle failures robustly; there is a need for principled protocols governing delegation across mixed human/AI agents.
AI.method: Introduction of an adaptive framework for intelligent AI delegation that sequences decisions on task allocation and incorporates mechanisms for transfer of authority, accountability, and boundary clarity; designed to be applicable to both human and AI delegators and delegatees within complex delegation networks.
AI.result: Conceptual framework with defined components (roles, boundaries, intent, trust) and a path toward protocols for the agentic web; no empirical results reported in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The paper contributes a structured, adaptable delegation framework intended to inform governance and protocol development for multi-agent systems, including humans and AI, in complex tasks.

title: AltTS: A Dual-Path Framework with Alternating Optimization for Multivariate Time Series Forecasting
summary: Multivariate time series forecasting involves two qualitatively distinct factors: (i) stable within-series autoregressive (AR) dynamics, and (ii) intermittent cross-dimension interactions that can become spurious over long horizons. We argue that fitting a single model to capture both effects creates an optimization conflict: the high-variance updates needed for cross-dimension modeling can corrupt the gradients that support autoregression, resulting in brittle training and degraded long-horizon accuracy. To address this, we propose ALTTS, a dual-path framework that explicitly decouples autoregression and cross-relation (CR) modeling. In ALTTS, the AR path is instantiated with a linear predictor, while the CR path uses a Transformer equipped with Cross-Relation Self-Attention (CRSA); the two branches are coordinated via alternating optimization to isolate gradient noise and reduce cross-block interference. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks show that ALTTS consistently outperforms prior methods, with the most pronounced improvements on long-horizon forecasting. Overall, our results suggest that carefully designed optimization strategies, rather than ever more complex architectures, can be a key driver of progress in multivariate time series forecasting.

AI.tldr: ALTTS decouples autoregression and cross-dimension relations in multivariate time series forecasting via a dual-path model: a linear AR path and a Transformer CR path with Cross-Relation Self-Attention, trained via alternating optimization to reduce gradient interference and improve long-horizon accuracy.
AI.motivation: To address the optimization conflict when fitting a single model to capture both stable autoregressive dynamics and intermittent cross-dimension interactions, which can corrupt gradients and degrade long-horizon performance.
AI.method: A dual-path framework where the AR path is a linear predictor and the CR path is a Transformer augmented with Cross-Relation Self-Attention (CRSA). The two branches are trained with alternating optimization to isolate gradient noise and reduce cross-block interference.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks show ALTTS consistently outperforms prior methods, with the largest gains on long-horizon forecasting.
AI.conclusion: Carefully designed optimization strategies, rather than ever more complex architectures, can drive progress in multivariate time series forecasting by effectively decoupling autoregression from cross-dimension interactions.

title: Free Lunch for Stabilizing Rectified Flow Inversion
summary: Rectified-Flow (RF)-based generative models have recently emerged as strong alternatives to traditional diffusion models, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance across various tasks. By learning a continuous velocity field that transforms simple noise into complex data, RF-based models not only enable high-quality generation, but also support training-free inversion, which facilitates downstream tasks such as reconstruction and editing. However, existing inversion methods, such as vanilla RF-based inversion, suffer from approximation errors that accumulate across timesteps, leading to unstable velocity fields and degraded reconstruction and editing quality. To address this challenge, we propose Proximal-Mean Inversion (PMI), a training-free gradient correction method that stabilizes the velocity field by guiding it toward a running average of past velocities, constrained within a theoretically derived spherical Gaussian. Furthermore, we introduce mimic-CFG, a lightweight velocity correction scheme for editing tasks, which interpolates between the current velocity and its projection onto the historical average, balancing editing effectiveness and structural consistency. Extensive experiments on PIE-Bench demonstrate that our methods significantly improve inversion stability, image reconstruction quality, and editing fidelity, while reducing the required number of neural function evaluations. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on the PIE-Bench with enhanced efficiency and theoretical soundness.

AI.tldr: Proximal-Mean Inversion (PMI) stabilizes Rectified-Flow (RF) inversions by training-free gradient correction toward a running average of past velocities within a spherical Gaussian, improving stability, reconstruction, and editing; mimic-CFG provides lightweight velocity correction for editing. Demonstrates state-of-the-art results on PIE-Bench with better efficiency.
AI.motivation: RF-based inversion suffers from accumulation of approximation errors across timesteps, causing unstable velocity fields and degraded reconstruction/editing quality.
AI.method: PMI applies a training-free gradient correction guiding the velocity toward a running average of past velocities, constrained within a theoretically derived spherical Gaussian. mimic-CFG interpolates between the current velocity and its projection onto the historical average to balance editing effectiveness and structure consistency.
AI.result: Significant improvements in inversion stability, image reconstruction quality, and editing fidelity, with fewer neural function evaluations. Achieves state-of-the-art performance on PIE-Bench with enhanced efficiency and theoretical soundness.
AI.conclusion: PMI and mimic-CFG offer stable, efficient, and theoretically grounded corrections for RF-based generative models, improving inversion, reconstruction, and editing tasks; supported by strong PIE-Bench results.

title: From Atoms to Trees: Building a Structured Feature Forest with Hierarchical Sparse Autoencoders
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have proven effective for extracting monosemantic features from large language models (LLMs), yet these features are typically identified in isolation. However, broad evidence suggests that LLMs capture the intrinsic structure of natural language, where the phenomenon of "feature splitting" in particular indicates that such structure is hierarchical. To capture this, we propose the Hierarchical Sparse Autoencoder (HSAE), which jointly learns a series of SAEs and the parent-child relationships between their features. HSAE strengthens the alignment between parent and child features through two novel mechanisms: a structural constraint loss and a random feature perturbation mechanism. Extensive experiments across various LLMs and layers demonstrate that HSAE consistently recovers semantically meaningful hierarchies, supported by both qualitative case studies and rigorous quantitative metrics. At the same time, HSAE preserves the reconstruction fidelity and interpretability of standard SAEs across different dictionary sizes. Our work provides a powerful, scalable tool for discovering and analyzing the multi-scale conceptual structures embedded in LLM representations.

AI.tldr: Proposes Hierarchical Sparse Autoencoder (HSAE) to jointly learn multiple sparse autoencoders and their parent–child feature relations, revealing multi‑scale semantic hierarchies in LLM representations while preserving reconstruction. Two novel mechanisms—structural constraint loss and random feature perturbation—enforce hierarchy alignment; validated across several LLMs and layers with qualitative and quantitative evidence.
AI.motivation: LLMs encode the intrinsic hierarchical structure of natural language. Traditional sparse autoencoders identify features in isolation, which misses multi‑scale organization. A method that jointly learns hierarchical features could reveal deeper, scalable representations and improve interpretability.
AI.method: Train a cascade/stack of sparse autoencoders that instantiate parent–child feature relationships. Introduce a structural constraint loss to align related features across levels and a random feature perturbation mechanism to enhance robustness of hierarchical links. Evaluate across multiple LLMs and layers, using qualitative case studies and quantitative metrics, and test across different dictionary sizes to assess fidelity and interpretability.
AI.result: HSAE consistently uncovers semantically meaningful hierarchies in LLM representations, as evidenced by qualitative case studies and robust quantitative metrics. It maintains reconstruction fidelity and interpretability comparable to standard SAEs across various dictionary sizes and model layers.
AI.conclusion: HSAE provides a scalable, effective tool for discovering and analyzing multi‑scale conceptual structures embedded in LLM representations, enabling better understanding of how high‑level and low‑level features relate within LLMs.

title: Krause Synchronization Transformers
summary: Self-attention in Transformers relies on globally normalized softmax weights, causing all tokens to compete for influence at every layer. When composed across depth, this interaction pattern induces strong synchronization dynamics that favor convergence toward a dominant mode, a behavior associated with representation collapse and attention sink phenomena. We introduce Krause Attention, a principled attention mechanism inspired by bounded-confidence consensus dynamics. Krause Attention replaces similarity-based global aggregation with distance-based, localized, and selectively sparse interactions, promoting structured local synchronization instead of global mixing. We relate this behavior to recent theory modeling Transformer dynamics as interacting particle systems, and show how bounded-confidence interactions naturally moderate attention concentration and alleviate attention sinks. Restricting interactions to local neighborhoods also reduces runtime complexity from quadratic to linear in sequence length. Experiments across vision (ViT on CIFAR/ImageNet), autoregressive generation (MNIST/CIFAR-10), and large language models (Llama/Qwen) demonstrate consistent gains with substantially reduced computation, highlighting bounded-confidence dynamics as a scalable and effective inductive bias for attention.

AI.tldr: Krause Attention uses local, distance-based, sparsified interactions inspired by bounded-confidence dynamics to replace global softmax. It mitigates attention sinks and reduces complexity, achieving gains across vision, autoregressive generation, and LLMs.
AI.motivation: Global softmax attention enforces universal competition among tokens, leading to depth-wise synchronization, dominant modes, representation collapse, and attention sinks. A scalable, stable inductive bias is needed.
AI.method: Replace global similarity-based aggregation with distance-based, localized, sparsely connected interactions (bounded-confidence mechanism). Frames Transformer dynamics as interacting particle systems to promote local synchronization and limit concentration. Reduces per-layer complexity from quadratic to linear in sequence length.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations across ViT on CIFAR/ImageNet, autoregressive generation on MNIST/CIFAR-10, and Llama/Qwen show consistent gains with substantially reduced computation, indicating the approach’s broad effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Bounding attention interactions locally via bounded-confidence dynamics provides a scalable, effective inductive bias for attention, alleviating sinks and improving efficiency across domains.

title: Zooming without Zooming: Region-to-Image Distillation for Fine-Grained Multimodal Perception
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) excel at broad visual understanding but still struggle with fine-grained perception, where decisive evidence is small and easily overwhelmed by global context. Recent "Thinking-with-Images" methods alleviate this by iteratively zooming in and out regions of interest during inference, but incur high latency due to repeated tool calls and visual re-encoding. To address this, we propose Region-to-Image Distillation, which transforms zooming from an inference-time tool into a training-time primitive, thereby internalizing the benefits of agentic zooming into a single forward pass of an MLLM. In particular, we first zoom in to micro-cropped regions to let strong teacher models generate high-quality VQA data, and then distill this region-grounded supervision back to the full image. After training on such data, the smaller student model improves "single-glance" fine-grained perception without tool use. To rigorously evaluate this capability, we further present ZoomBench, a hybrid-annotated benchmark of 845 VQA data spanning six fine-grained perceptual dimensions, together with a dual-view protocol that quantifies the global--regional "zooming gap". Experiments show that our models achieve leading performance across multiple fine-grained perception benchmarks, and also improve general multimodal cognition on benchmarks such as visual reasoning and GUI agents. We further discuss when "Thinking-with-Images" is necessary versus when its gains can be distilled into a single forward pass. Our code is available at https://github.com/inclusionAI/Zooming-without-Zooming.

AI.tldr: Region-to-Image Distillation internalizes zooming behavior into a single forward pass, enabling small MLLMs to achieve fine-grained perception without iterative tools; supported by ZoomBench, a six-dacet fine-grained VQA benchmark, showing strong gains and a Zooming gap analysis.
AI.motivation: Fine-grained visual perception in multimodal LLMs is hampered by reliance on global context; iterativeThinking-with-Images improves accuracy but incurs high latency. Distilling zoom-driven guidance into training promises single-pass performance with lower latency.
AI.method: Apply high-quality region-level supervision by zooming into micro-crops with strong teachers to generate region-grounded VQA data, then distill this into the full-image distillation target for a smaller student model. Train on this augmented data to achieve single-glance, tool-free perception. Introduce ZoomBench (~845 VQA items) with six fine-grained perceptual dimensions and a dual-view protocol to quantify global-regional zooming gaps.
AI.result: The student models achieve leading performance on fine-grained perception benchmarks and show improvements in general multimodal cognition (e.g., visual reasoning, GUI agents), outperforming baselines that rely on iterative zooming.
AI.conclusion: Region-to-Image Distillation successfully transfers zooming benefits into a single-forward-pass model, enabling efficient, fine-grained perception without tool usage; ZoomBench provides a rigorous evaluation of global vs. regional perception and clarifies when additional zooming is necessary versus distillable.

title: When Should LLMs Be Less Specific? Selective Abstraction for Reliable Long-Form Text Generation
summary: LLMs are widely used, yet they remain prone to factual errors that erode user trust and limit adoption in high-risk settings. One approach to mitigate this risk is to equip models with uncertainty estimation mechanisms that abstain when confidence is low. However, this binary "all-or-nothing" approach is excessively restrictive in long-form settings, often discarding valuable information. We introduce Selective Abstraction (SA), a framework that enables LLMs to trade specificity for reliability by selectively reducing the detail of uncertain content. We first formalize SA through the lenses of selective risk and coverage. We then propose Atom-wise Selective Abstraction, a claim-level instantiation that decomposes responses into atomic claims (short, self-contained statements each expressing a single fact) and replaces uncertain atoms with higher confidence, less specific abstractions. To evaluate this framework, we develop a novel end-to-end pipeline for open-ended generation that instantiates risk as factual correctness and measures coverage using an information-theoretic measure of retained information. Across six open-source models on the FactScore and LongFact-Objects benchmarks, atom-wise SA consistently outperforms existing baselines, improving the area under the risk-coverage curve (AURC) by up to 27.73% over claim removal, demonstrating that reducing specificity can boost accuracy and reliability while preserving most of their original meaning.

AI.tldr: Introduces Selective Abstraction (SA) for LLMs to trade specificity for reliability in long-form generation; atom-wise SA decomposes outputs into atomic claims and substitutes uncertain ones with higher-confidence abstractions; demonstrates up to 27.73% improvement in AURC on six models across FactScore and LongFact-Objects.
AI.motivation: LLMs frequently produce factual errors that erode trust. Traditional abstention (to abstain when uncertain) is too coarse for long-form content, discarding valuable information. A nuanced approach is needed to maintain information while improving reliability in high-stakes, long-form generation.
AI.method: Formalize SA using selective risk and coverage; propose Atom-wise Selective Abstraction (claim-level) that breaks responses into atomic claims and replaces uncertain atoms with less specific abstractions. Develop an end-to-end generation pipeline with risk as factual correctness and information-theoretic coverage as retained information. Evaluate on six open-source models using FactScore and LongFact-Objects benchmarks, using AURC as the primary metric.
AI.result: Atom-wise SA consistently outperforms baselines, achieving up to a 27.73% improvement in AURC over claim removal. The approach increases factual reliability while preserving most of the original meaning by reducing specificity only where uncertainty is present.
AI.conclusion: Selective Abstraction provides a practical framework to balance risk and information retention in long-form LLM outputs. The atom-wise, claim-level instantiation demonstrates that targeted abstraction can enhance accuracy and trustworthiness across multiple models and benchmarks.

title: AlphaPROBE: Alpha Mining via Principled Retrieval and On-graph biased evolution
summary: Extracting signals through alpha factor mining is a fundamental challenge in quantitative finance. Existing automated methods primarily follow two paradigms: Decoupled Factor Generation, which treats factor discovery as isolated events, and Iterative Factor Evolution, which focuses on local parent-child refinements. However, both paradigms lack a global structural view, often treating factor pools as unstructured collections or fragmented chains, which leads to redundant search and limited diversity. To address these limitations, we introduce AlphaPROBE (Alpha Mining via Principled Retrieval and On-graph Biased Evolution), a framework that reframes alpha mining as the strategic navigation of a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG). By modeling factors as nodes and evolutionary links as edges, AlphaPROBE treats the factor pool as a dynamic, interconnected ecosystem. The framework consists of two core components: a Bayesian Factor Retriever that identifies high-potential seeds by balancing exploitation and exploration through a posterior probability model, and a DAG-aware Factor Generator that leverages the full ancestral trace of factors to produce context-aware, nonredundant optimizations. Extensive experiments on three major Chinese stock market datasets against 8 competitive baselines demonstrate that AlphaPROBE significantly gains enhanced performance in predictive accuracy, return stability and training efficiency. Our results confirm that leveraging global evolutionary topology is essential for efficient and robust automated alpha discovery. We have open-sourced our implementation at https://github.com/gta0804/AlphaPROBE.

AI.tldr: Introduces AlphaPROBE, a DAG-based framework for alpha mining using Bayesian retrieval and DAG-aware generation to globally navigate factor space, improving predictive performance, stability, and efficiency; code released.
AI.motivation: Existing factor mining methods lack a global structural view of the factor ecosystem, leading to redundant search and limited diversity; a global topology-guided approach could yield more diverse, efficient discovery of predictive alphas.
AI.method: Models factors as nodes and evolutionary relations as edges in a DAG. Proposes two components: (1) Bayesian Factor Retriever to select high-potential seeds via a posterior probability model balancing exploitation and exploration; (2) DAG-aware Factor Generator that uses full ancestral traces to generate context-aware, nonredundant optimizations. Evaluated on three Chinese stock market datasets against 8 baselines; reports improvements in predictive accuracy, return stability, and training efficiency; open-sourced implementation.
AI.result: Empirical results show AlphaPROBE achieving significant gains in predictive accuracy, return stability, and training efficiency over baselines, validating the utility of leveraging global evolutionary topology for alpha discovery.
AI.conclusion: Global, topology-informed alpha mining is beneficial and robust; the DAG framing and two-component design effectively explore and refine factor pools. The authors provide an open-source implementation for reproducibility.

title: Native Reasoning Models: Training Language Models to Reason on Unverifiable Data
summary: The prevailing paradigm for training large reasoning models--combining Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR)--is fundamentally constrained by its reliance on high-quality, human-annotated reasoning data and external verifiers. This dependency incurs significant data-collection costs, risks embedding human cognitive biases, and confines the reinforcement learning stage to objectively assessable domains like mathematics and coding, leaving a wide range of unverifiable tasks beyond its scope. To overcome these limitations, we introduce NRT (Native Reasoning Training), a novel framework that cultivates complex reasoning by having the model generate its own reasoning traces using only standard question-answer pairs, thereby obviating the need for expert-written demonstrations. NRT reframes the training problem by treating the reasoning process as a latent variable. It employs a unified training objective that models reasoning as an optimization problem, intrinsically rewarding paths that increase the model's likelihood of producing the ground-truth answer. This unified perspective allows us to analyze intrinsic failure modes of prior methods, such as policy collapse, and systematically design more robust reward aggregation functions, creating a self-reinforcing feedback loop where the model learns to think in ways that resolve its own uncertainty. Empirical evaluation on Llama and Mistral model families demonstrates that NRT achieves state-of-the-art performance among verifier-free methods, significantly outperforming standard SFT baselines and prior verifier-free RL methods. Our approach yields particularly strong performance gains in complex reasoning domains and exhibits high robustness to policy collapse, offering a general, scalable path toward building more powerful and broadly applicable reasoning systems.

AI.tldr: NRT reframes reasoning as a latent variable and learns verifier-free, self-generated reasoning traces from standard QA data, achieving state-of-the-art performance among verifier-free methods on Llama/Mistral and robustness to policy collapse.
AI.motivation: Remove dependence on costly, biased human-annotated reasoning data and external verifiers, which confine RL to verifiable domains and limit applicability to unverifiable tasks.
AI.method: Treat reasoning as a latent variable and train with a unified objective that rewards reasoning paths increasing the likelihood of the ground-truth answer; model self-generated traces without expert demonstrations; analyze prior failure modes like policy collapse and design robust reward aggregation, creating a self-reinforcing loop that improves reasoning to resolve uncertainty.
AI.result: Empirical results on Llama and Mistral show state-of-the-art performance among verifier-free methods, outperforming SFT baselines and prior verifier-free RL, with strong gains in complex reasoning and high robustness to policy collapse.
AI.conclusion: NRT offers a general, scalable path to more capable, broadly applicable reasoning systems without external verifiers, reducing data costs and biases and extending verifier-free reasoning beyond traditional domains.

title: SynthRAR: Ring Artifacts Reduction in CT with Unrolled Network and Synthetic Data Training
summary: Defective and inconsistent responses in CT detectors can cause ring and streak artifacts in the reconstructed images, making them unusable for clinical purposes. In recent years, several ring artifact reduction solutions have been proposed in the image domain or in the sinogram domain using supervised deep learning methods. However, these methods require dedicated datasets for training, leading to a high data collection cost. Furthermore, existing approaches focus exclusively on either image-space or sinogram-space correction, neglecting the intrinsic correlations from the forward operation of the CT geometry. Based on the theoretical analysis of non-ideal CT detector responses, the RAR problem is reformulated as an inverse problem by using an unrolled network, which considers non-ideal response together with linear forward-projection with CT geometry. Additionally, the intrinsic correlations of ring artifacts between the sinogram and image domains are leveraged through synthetic data derived from natural images, enabling the trained model to correct artifacts without requiring real-world clinical data. Extensive evaluations on diverse scanning geometries and anatomical regions demonstrate that the model trained on synthetic data consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: An unrolled network-based inverse problem formulation for ring artifact reduction (RAR) that integrates non-ideal detector response with forward CT projection, trained with synthetic data derived from natural images to exploit sinogram-image correlations, and achieving superior performance without real clinical data across diverse geometries and anatomies.
AI.motivation: Ring artifacts arise from defective/inconsistent detectors. Existing deep learning solutions operate in image or sinogram domains and require labeled clinical data, limiting data efficiency and ignoring the CT forward operator relationships.
AI.method: An unrolled network that models the non-ideal detector response and linear forward projection of CT geometry; leverages synthetic data from natural images to encode cross-domain correlations between sinogram and image domains; trained without real-world clinical data; evaluated across diverse scanning geometries and anatomical regions.
AI.result: The model trained on synthetic data consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods across varied geometries and anatomical regions.
AI.conclusion: Joint forward-model-informed unrolled architecture with synthetic data enables robust ring artifact reduction without clinical data, suggesting broader potential for CT artifact correction with reduced data requirements.

title: MEME: Modeling the Evolutionary Modes of Financial Markets
summary: LLMs have demonstrated significant potential in quantitative finance by processing vast unstructured data to emulate human-like analytical workflows. However, current LLM-based methods primarily follow either an Asset-Centric paradigm focused on individual stock prediction or a Market-Centric approach for portfolio allocation, often remaining agnostic to the underlying reasoning that drives market movements. In this paper, we propose a Logic-Oriented perspective, modeling the financial market as a dynamic, evolutionary ecosystem of competing investment narratives, termed Modes of Thought.
