Our framework particularly excels in complex reasoning tasks, showing 19.2% improvement on MATH and 22.3% on HumanEval compared with state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: Pedagogically-inspired IOA framework for distilling LLMs using curriculum-based progression and cognitive adaptation; achieves near-teacher performance with far fewer params and gains on math and code reasoning.
AI.motivation: Current synthetic-data distillation lacks a principled learning trajectory; a systematic, education-inspired approach is needed to account for prerequisite knowledge and student capacity during knowledge transfer.
AI.method: Three-stage IOA: Knowledge Identifier identifies deficiencies; Organizer designs progressive curricula; Adapter aligns representations to the student model's cognitive capacity. Integrates Bloom's Mastery Learning Principles and Vygotsky's Zone of Proximal Development to regulate progression and difficulty.
AI.result: On LLaMA-3.1/3.2 and Qwen2.5 as student models, IOA outperforms baselines; achieves 94.7% of teacher performance on DollyEval with <1/10 of the parameters; improvements of 19.2% on MATH and 22.3% on HumanEval over state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: A pedagogy-inspired distillation framework effectively enhances student LLMs' efficiency and performance, particularly in complex reasoning tasks, by employing curriculum-based progression and capacity-aware representation adaptation.

title: Potential-energy gating for robust state estimation in bistable stochastic systems
summary: We introduce potential-energy gating, a method for robust state estimation in systems governed by double-well stochastic dynamics. The observation noise covariance of a Bayesian filter is modulated by the local value of a known or assumed potential energy function: observations are trusted when the state is near a potential minimum and progressively discounted as it approaches the barrier separating metastable wells. This physics-based mechanism differs from purely statistical robust filters, which treat all regions of state space identically, and from constrained filters, which impose hard bounds on states rather than modulating observation trust. We implement the gating within Extended, Unscented, Ensemble, and Adaptive Kalman filters and particle filters, requiring only two additional hyperparameters. Synthetic benchmarks on a Ginzburg-Landau double-well process with 10% outlier contamination and Monte Carlo validation over 100 replications show 57-80% RMSE improvement over the standard Extended Kalman Filter, all statistically significant (p < 10^{-15}, Wilcoxon signed-rank test). A naive topological baseline using only distance to the nearest well achieves 57%, confirming that the continuous energy landscape adds an additional ~21 percentage points. The method is robust to misspecification: even when assumed potential parameters deviate by 50% from their true values, improvement never falls below 47%. Comparing externally forced and spontaneous Kramers-type transitions, gating retains 68% improvement under noise-induced transitions whereas the naive baseline degrades to 30%. As an empirical illustration, we apply the framework to Dansgaard-Oeschger events in the NGRIP delta-18O ice-core record, estimating asymmetry parameter gamma = -0.109 (bootstrap 95% CI: [-0.220, -0.011], excluding zero) and demonstrating that outlier fraction explains 91% of the variance in filter improvement.

AI.tldr: Energy-based gating improves robustness of state estimation in double-well stochastic dynamics by modulating observation trust with the potential energy landscape; yields substantial RMSE gains across EKF/UKF/EnKF/particle filters; robust to model misspecification and transitions; demonstrated on Ginzburg-Landau dynamics and climate proxy data.
AI.motivation: To enhance filter robustness for metastable systems where observations near barriers are less reliable; standard robust or constrained filters ignore the physics of the energy landscape, potentially wasting information and mis-weighting measurements.
AI.method: Introduce potential-energy gating: the observation noise covariance is scaled by the local potential energy, increasing trust near wells and discounting near barriers. Implemented within Extended, Unscented, Ensemble, and Adaptive Kalman filters and particle filters with only two additional hyperparameters; tested on synthetic double-well dynamics (Ginzburg-Landau) with 10% outliers and via Monte Carlo (100 replications), plus an empirical climate data application.
AI.result: Achieved 57–80% RMSE improvement over the standard Extended Kalman Filter in synthetic benchmarks; improvements remained significant across misspecification (up to 50%), and for transitions (68% improvement under noise-induced transitions; naive baseline drops to 30%). Naive distance-to-well baseline accounts for 57%, indicating energy landscape adds ~21 percentage points. In climate data (NGRIP delta-18O), estimated asymmetry gamma = -0.109 (bootstrap CI [-0.220, -0.011]); outlier fraction explains 91% of variance in filter improvement.
AI.conclusion: Energy-based potential-energy gating substantially improves state estimation in metastable, noisy systems and can be integrated with multiple filtering paradigms; the method leverages physical energy landscapes to dynamically adjust observation trust, yielding robust performance in both synthetic and real-world data.

title: Stroke of Surprise: Progressive Semantic Illusions in Vector Sketching
summary: Visual illusions traditionally rely on spatial manipulations such as multi-view consistency. In this work, we introduce Progressive Semantic Illusions, a novel vector sketching task where a single sketch undergoes a dramatic semantic transformation through the sequential addition of strokes. We present Stroke of Surprise, a generative framework that optimizes vector strokes to satisfy distinct semantic interpretations at different drawing stages. The core challenge lies in the "dual-constraint": initial prefix strokes must form a coherent object (e.g., a duck) while simultaneously serving as the structural foundation for a second concept (e.g., a sheep) upon adding delta strokes. To address this, we propose a sequence-aware joint optimization framework driven by a dual-branch Score Distillation Sampling (SDS) mechanism. Unlike sequential approaches that freeze the initial state, our method dynamically adjusts prefix strokes to discover a "common structural subspace" valid for both targets. Furthermore, we introduce a novel Overlay Loss that enforces spatial complementarity, ensuring structural integration rather than occlusion. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in recognizability and illusion strength, successfully expanding visual anagrams from the spatial to the temporal dimension. Project page: https://stroke-of-surprise.github.io/

AI.tldr: Proposes Progressive Semantic Illusions for vector sketching, introducing Stroke of Surprise to transform a single sketch’s semantics across drawing stages using a dual-constraint, sequence-aware optimization with dual-branch SDS and an Overlay Loss; outperforms baselines in recognizability and illusion strength, expanding visual anagrams into the temporal domain.
AI.motivation: Traditional visual-illusion methods rely on spatial manipulations; this work seeks to encode semantic transformations within a single sketch as it is drawn, addressing dual constraints where initial strokes must support two meanings and enabling control over a shared structural subspace.
AI.method: Introduce Stroke of Surprise, a generative framework that optimizes vector strokes to yield distinct semantic interpretations at different drawing stages. Employ sequence-aware joint optimization with a dual-branch Score Distillation Sampling (SDS) mechanism. Unlike freezing the initial state, dynamically adjust prefix strokes to find a common subspace valid for both targets. Add Overlay Loss to enforce spatial complementarity and structural integration rather than occlusion.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show significant improvements over state-of-the-art baselines in recognizability and illusion strength. The method successfully expands the concept of visual anagrams from spatial to temporal dimensions, with a project page provided for replication and demonstration.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables temporal semantic transformations in a single sketch by discovering a common structural subspace and enforcing spatial complementarity, paving the way for richer, time-aware visual illusions and broader applications in vector-based generative design.

title: SAM3-LiteText: An Anatomical Study of the SAM3 Text Encoder for Efficient Vision-Language Segmentation
summary: Vision-language segmentation models such as SAM3 enable flexible, prompt-driven visual grounding, but inherit large, general-purpose text encoders originally designed for open-ended language understanding. In practice, segmentation prompts are short, structured, and semantically constrained, leading to substantial over-provisioning in text encoder capacity and persistent computational and memory overhead. In this paper, we perform a large-scale anatomical analysis of text prompting in vision-language segmentation, covering 404,796 real prompts across multiple benchmarks. Our analysis reveals severe redundancy: most context windows are underutilized, vocabulary usage is highly sparse, and text embeddings lie on low-dimensional manifold despite high-dimensional representations. Motivated by these findings, we propose SAM3-LiteText, a lightweight text encoding framework that replaces the original SAM3 text encoder with a compact MobileCLIP student that is optimized by knowledge distillation. Extensive experiments on image and video segmentation benchmarks show that SAM3-LiteText reduces text encoder parameters by up to 88%, substantially reducing static memory footprint, while maintaining segmentation performance comparable to the original model. Code: https://github.com/SimonZeng7108/efficientsam3/tree/sam3_litetext.

AI.tldr: Large-scale analysis reveals redundancy in text prompting for vision-language segmentation; SAM3-LiteText uses a distilled MobileCLIP-based text encoder to cut text-encoder params by up to 88% with comparable performance.
AI.motivation: Reduce memory and compute overhead of large text encoders in vision-language segmentation by exploiting structured, semantically constrained prompts and observed redundancies.
AI.method: Analyze 404,796 real prompts across benchmarks to diagnose prompt redundancy (context windows, vocabulary usage, embedding dimensionality). Develop SAM3-LiteText by distilling a compact MobileCLIP student to replace the original SAM3 text encoder. Evaluate on image and video segmentation benchmarks with ablations.
AI.result: Text encoder parameters reduced by up to 88%, leading to substantial static memory savings while maintaining segmentation performance on par with the original SAM3 model. Code released.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates viability of lightweight, distillation-based text encoders for prompt-driven segmentation and highlights opportunity to remove over-provisioned capacity in text encoders without sacrificing accuracy.

title: DICE: Diffusion Large Language Models Excel at Generating CUDA Kernels
summary: Diffusion large language models (dLLMs) have emerged as a compelling alternative to autoregressive (AR) LLMs, owing to their capacity for parallel token generation. This paradigm is particularly well-suited for code generation, where holistic structural planning and non-sequential refinement are critical. Despite this potential, tailoring dLLMs for CUDA kernel generation remains challenging, obstructed not only by the high specialization but also by the severe lack of high-quality training data. To address these challenges, we construct CuKe, an augmented supervised fine-tuning dataset optimized for high-performance CUDA kernels. On top of it, we propose a bi-phase curated reinforcement learning (BiC-RL) framework consisting of a CUDA kernel infilling stage and an end-to-end CUDA kernel generation stage. Leveraging this training framework, we introduce DICE, a series of diffusion large language models designed for CUDA kernel generation, spanning three parameter scales, 1.7B, 4B, and 8B. Extensive experiments on KernelBench demonstrate that DICE significantly outperforms both autoregressive and diffusion LLMs of comparable scale, establishing a new state-of-the-art for CUDA kernel generation.

AI.tldr: CuKe dataset plus BiC-RL framework enable diffusion LLMs (DICE) to excel at CUDA kernel generation, achieving state-of-the-art results on KernelBench across 1.7B, 4B, and 8B parameter scales.
AI.motivation: Diffusion LLMs offer parallel token generation beneficial for code generation, but CUDA kernel synthesis suffers from high specialization and data scarcity; a high-quality, specialized training pipeline is needed to unlock performance gains.
AI.method: Create CuKe, an augmented supervised fine-tuning dataset for CUDA kernels; develop Bi-phase curated reinforcement learning (BiC-RL) with a CUDA kernel infilling stage and an end-to-end kernel generation stage; train DICE models at 1.7B, 4B, and 8B parameters.
AI.result: DICE outperforms autoregressive and diffusion LLMs of comparable scale on KernelBench, establishing a new state-of-the-art for CUDA kernel generation.
AI.conclusion: The combination of a specialized dataset and a two-phase RL training framework enables diffusion LLMs to surpass existing models in CUDA kernel generation, demonstrating promise for diffusion-based code models and high-performance GPU kernel synthesis.

title: "Sorry, I Didn't Catch That": How Speech Models Miss What Matters Most
summary: Despite speech recognition systems achieving low word error rates on standard benchmarks, they often fail on short, high-stakes utterances in real-world deployments. Here, we study this failure mode in a high-stakes task: the transcription of U.S. street names as spoken by U.S. participants. We evaluate 15 models from OpenAI, Deepgram, Google, and Microsoft on recordings from linguistically diverse U.S. speakers and find an average transcription error rate of 44%. We quantify the downstream impact of failed transcriptions by geographic locations and show that mis-transcriptions systematically cause errors for all speakers, but that routing distance errors are twice as large for non-English primary speakers compared to English primary speakers. To mitigate this harm, we introduce a synthetic data generation approach that produces diverse pronunciations of named entities using open-source text-to-speech models. Fine-tuning with less than 1,000 synthetic samples improves street name transcription accuracy by nearly 60% (relative to base models) for non-English primary speakers. Our results highlight a critical gap between benchmark performance and real-world reliability in speech systems and demonstrate a simple, scalable path to reducing high-stakes transcription errors.

AI.tldr: Evaluates 15 ASR models on U.S. street-name transcription, finding a 44% average error rate. Non-English speakers incur larger routing-distance errors. Introduces synthetic data generation via TTS; fine-tunes with <1k synthetic samples, yielding ~60% relative WER improvement for non-English speakers.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between benchmark WER and real-world reliability in high-stakes transcription, particularly for proper nouns and multilingual speakers in navigation-related tasks.
AI.method: Benchmark 15 models from major vendors on diverse recordings of U.S. speakers; quantify downstream geographic routing-distance errors; generate synthetic pronunciations of named entities via open-source TTS; fine-tune with less than 1,000 synthetic samples.
AI.result: Average transcription error rate 44%; mis-transcriptions induce geographic errors for all speakers, with routing-distance errors doubling for non-English primary speakers; synthetic augmentation yields approximately 60% relative improvement in street-name transcription for non-English speakers.
AI.conclusion: Reveals a critical gap between benchmark performance and real-world reliability in ASR; demonstrates a scalable path to reduce high-stakes transcription errors through synthetic data augmentation and targeted fine-tuning.

title: Dopamine: Brain Modes, Not Brains
summary:
Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods such as \lora{} adapt large pretrained models by adding small weight-space updates. While effective, weight deltas are hard to interpret mechanistically, and they do not directly expose \emph{which} internal computations are reused versus bypassed for a new task. We explore an alternative view inspired by neuromodulation: adaptation as a change in \emph{mode} -- selecting and rescaling existing computations -- rather than rewriting the underlying weights. We propose \methodname{}, a simple activation-space PEFT technique that freezes base weights and learns per-neuron \emph{thresholds} and \emph{gains}. During training, a smooth gate decides whether a neuron's activation participates; at inference the gate can be hardened to yield explicit conditional computation and neuron-level attributions.
  As a proof of concept, we study ``mode specialization'' on MNIST (0$^\circ$) versus rotated MNIST (45$^\circ$). We pretrain a small MLP on a 50/50 mixture (foundation), freeze its weights, and then specialize to the rotated mode using \methodname{}. Across seeds, \methodname{} improves rotated accuracy over the frozen baseline while using only a few hundred trainable parameters per layer, and exhibits partial activation sparsity (a minority of units strongly active). Compared to \lora{}, \methodname{} trades some accuracy for substantially fewer trainable parameters and a more interpretable ``which-neurons-fire'' mechanism. We discuss limitations, including reduced expressivity when the frozen base lacks features needed for the target mode.

AI.tldr: Activation-space PEFT (mode specialization) freezes base weights and learns per-neuron gains and thresholds to gate activations, offering interpretable, neuron-level attribution with a small parameter budget, trading some accuracy versus LoRA.
AI.motivation: Address the interpretability gap of weight-delta PEFT methods by reinterpreting adaptation as mode switching (reusing existing computations) and enabling explicit neuron-level attribution of task-specific changes.
AI.method: Freeze the base network; introduce per-neuron gain and threshold; train a smooth gate that decides neuron participation during learning; harden the gate at inference to obtain conditional computation and neuron-level explanations; evaluate on a mode-specialization task (MNIST vs rotated MNIST) using a small MLP pretrained on a 50/50 mixture; only a few hundred trainable parameters per layer; observe partial activation sparsity.
AI.result: Across seeds, mode specialization improves rotated MNIST accuracy over the frozen baseline while using a few hundred trainable params per layer; reveals partial activation sparsity (a minority of units strongly active); yields more interpretable which-neurons-fire mechanism than LoRA, though with some accuracy-cost relative to full fine-tuning; limitations include reduced expressivity if the frozen base lacks necessary features for the target mode.
AI.conclusion: Activation-space mode specialization is a promising, interpretable, parameter-efficient alternative to weight-delta PEFT, contingent on the frozen base possessing relevant features for the target mode; future work should expand to more datasets, larger models, and richer gating mechanisms to mitigate expressivity limitations.

title: Think like a Scientist: Physics-guided LLM Agent for Equation Discovery
summary: Explaining observed phenomena through symbolic, interpretable formulas is a fundamental goal of science. Recently, large language models (LLMs) have emerged as promising tools for symbolic equation discovery, owing to their broad domain knowledge and strong reasoning capabilities. However, most existing LLM-based systems try to guess equations directly from data, without modeling the multi-step reasoning process that scientists often follow: first inferring physical properties such as symmetries, then using these as priors to restrict the space of candidate equations. We introduce KeplerAgent, an agentic framework that explicitly follows this scientific reasoning process. The agent coordinates physics-based tools to extract intermediate structure and uses these results to configure symbolic regression engines such as PySINDy and PySR, including their function libraries and structural constraints. Across a suite of physical equation benchmarks, KeplerAgent achieves substantially higher symbolic accuracy and greater robustness to noisy data than both LLM and traditional baselines.

AI.tldr: KeplerAgent uses an agentic, physics-informed reasoning pipeline to discover symbolic equations: infer physical properties (symmetries) to constrain regression spaces, orchestrating physics tools to configure PySINDy/PySR for robust, accurate symbolic discovery under noise.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of direct equation guessing by LLMs; incorporate multi-step scientific reasoning (infer properties, set priors) to improve interpretability and robustness of symbolic regression.
AI.method: KeplerAgent coordinates physics-based tools to extract intermediate structure (e.g., symmetries, invariants) and uses these results to configure symbolic regression engines (PySINDy, PySR), including their function libraries and structural constraints.
AI.result: On physical equation benchmarks, KeplerAgent achieves substantially higher symbolic accuracy and greater robustness to noisy data than LLM-based and traditional baselines.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling the scientific reasoning process—inferring physical priors and constraining search—improves symbolic equation discovery; agent-driven, physics-informed architecture enhances robustness and accuracy.

title: U-Former ODE: Fast Probabilistic Forecasting of Irregular Time Series
summary: Probabilistic forecasting of irregularly sampled time series is crucial in domains such as healthcare and finance, yet it remains a formidable challenge. Existing Neural Controlled Differential Equation (Neural CDE) approaches, while effective at modelling continuous dynamics, suffer from slow, inherently sequential computation, which restricts scalability and limits access to global context. We introduce UFO (U-Former ODE), a novel architecture that seamlessly integrates the parallelizable, multiscale feature extraction of U-Nets, the powerful global modelling of Transformers, and the continuous-time dynamics of Neural CDEs. By constructing a fully causal, parallelizable model, UFO achieves a global receptive field while retaining strong sensitivity to local temporal dynamics. Extensive experiments on five standard benchmarks -- covering both regularly and irregularly sampled time series -- demonstrate that UFO consistently outperforms ten state-of-the-art neural baselines in predictive accuracy. Moreover, UFO delivers up to 15$\times$ faster inference compared to conventional Neural CDEs, with consistently strong performance on long and highly multivariate sequences.

AI.tldr: UFO delivers fast, globally-aware probabilistic forecasting for irregularly sampled time series by fusing U-Net multiscale features, Transformers, and Neural CDEs into a fully causal, parallelizable architecture that outperforms baselines and offers significant speedups.
AI.motivation: Neural CDEs model continuous-time dynamics but rely on sequential ODE solvers, hindering scalability and limiting access to global context, especially for irregularly sampled data. A parallelizable, globally receptive architecture is needed for accurate probabilistic forecasting on long, multivariate sequences.
AI.method: UFO combines U-Net style multiscale feature extraction with Transformer-based global modeling, integrated with Neural CDEs to preserve continuous-time dynamics. The model is fully causal and parallelizable, enabling a global receptive field while maintaining sensitivity to local temporal patterns. It targets probabilistic forecasting for irregularly sampled time series.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five benchmarks (covering regular and irregular sampling) show UFO consistently outperforms ten state-of-the-art neural baselines in predictive accuracy. Inference is up to 15× faster than conventional Neural CDEs, with robust performance on long, highly multivariate sequences.
AI.conclusion: UFO effectively addresses the sequential bottleneck of Neural CDEs while delivering global-context modeling and strong performance for irregularly sampled time series. It advances probabilistic forecasting by combining continuous-time modeling with parallel, multiscale architectures. Further details on probabilistic outputs, computational costs, and ablations would strengthen understanding and reproducibility.

title: CM2: Reinforcement Learning with Checklist Rewards for Multi-Turn and Multi-Step Agentic Tool Use
summary: AI agents are increasingly used to solve real-world tasks by reasoning over multi-turn user interactions and invoking external tools. However, applying reinforcement learning to such settings remains difficult: realistic objectives often lack verifiable rewards and instead emphasize open-ended behaviors; moreover, RL for multi-turn, multi-step agentic tool use is still underexplored; and building and maintaining executable tool environments is costly, limiting scale and coverage. We propose CM2, an RL framework that replaces verifiable outcome rewards with checklist rewards. CM2 decomposes each turn's intended behavior into fine-grained binary criteria with explicit evidence grounding and structured metadata, turning open-ended judging into more stable classification-style decisions. To balance stability and informativeness, our method adopts a strategy of sparse reward assignment but dense evaluation criteria. Training is performed in a scalable LLM-simulated tool environment, avoiding heavy engineering for large tool sets. Experiments show that CM2 consistently improves over supervised fine-tuning. Starting from an 8B Base model and training on an 8k-example RL dataset, CM2 improves over the SFT counterpart by 8 points on tau^-Bench, by 10 points on BFCL-V4, and by 12 points on ToolSandbox. The results match or even outperform similarly sized open-source baselines, including the judging model. CM2 thus provides a scalable recipe for optimizing multi-turn, multi-step tool-using agents without relying on verifiable rewards. Code provided by the open-source community: https://github.com/namezhenzhang/CM2-RLCR-Tool-Agent.

AI.tldr: CM2 replaces verifiable rewards with checklist-based sparse rewards for multi-turn tool-using RL, using dense, binary criteria grounded in evidence, trained in an LLM-simulated environment, yielding consistent gains over SFT across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Real-world multi-turn agentic tool use often lacks verifiable rewards, making RL difficult. Stable evaluation is needed for open-ended, tool-using tasks at scale.
AI.method: Decompose each turn into fine-grained binary criteria with evidence grounding and structured metadata; apply sparse rewards with dense evaluation criteria; train in an scalable LLM-simulated tool environment; begin from an 8B base model and an 8k-example RL dataset; compare against supervised fine-tuning and baselines.
AI.result: CM2 improves over SFT by 8–12 points across tau^-Bench, BFCL-V4, and ToolSandbox, matching or surpassing similarly sized open-source baselines including the judging model.
AI.conclusion: CM2 offers a scalable recipe to optimize multi-turn, multi-step tool-using agents without relying on verifiable rewards and demonstrates consistent cross-domain improvements.

title: TUBO: A Tailored ML Framework for Reliable Network Traffic Forecasting
comment: Short version of this paper is presented at ICDCS 2025
summary: Traffic forecasting based network operation optimization and management offers enormous promise but also presents significant challenges from traffic forecasting perspective. While deep learning models have proven to be relatively more effective than traditional statistical methods for time series forecasting, their reliability is not satisfactory due to their inability to effectively handle unique characteristics of network traffic. In particular, the burst and complex traffic patterns makes the existing models less reliable, as each type of deep learning model has limited capability in capturing traffic patterns. To address this issue, we introduce TUBO, a novel machine learning framework custom designed for reliable network traffic forecasting. TUBO features two key components: burst processing for handling significant traffic fluctuations and model selection for adapting to varying traffic patterns using a pool of models. A standout feature of TUBO is its ability to provide deterministic predictions along with quantified uncertainty, which serves as a cue for identifying the most reliable forecasts. Evaluations on three real-world network demand matrix (DM) datasets (Abilene, GEANT, and CERNET) show that TUBO significantly outperforms existing methods on forecasting accuracy (by 4 times), and also achieves up to 94% accuracy in burst occurrence forecasting. Furthermore, we also consider traffic demand forecasting based proactive traffic engineering (TE) as a downstream use case. Our results show that compared to reactive approaches and proactive TE using the best existing DM forecasting methods, proactive TE powered by TUBO improves aggregated throughput by 9 times and 3 times, respectively.

AI.tldr: TUBO is a machine learning framework for reliable network traffic forecasting that combines burst processing and model selection over a pool of models, providing deterministic predictions with quantified uncertainty; it improves forecasting accuracy and proactive traffic engineering outcomes on real DM datasets (Abilene, GEANT, CERNET).
AI.motivation: Address reliability issues of deep learning-based network traffic forecasting due to bursty, non-stationary traffic and diverse regimes; require uncertainty estimates and robust operation decisions.
AI.method: Two components: burst processing to handle large fluctuations; model selection with a pool of models to adapt to varying traffic patterns; deterministic predictions with quantified uncertainty; evaluation on real DM datasets; downstream use in proactive traffic engineering.
AI.result: Forecasting accuracy improved up to 4x over existing methods; burst-forecasting accuracy up to 94%; proactive TE with TUBO yields aggregated throughput improvements of 9x (vs reactive) and 3x (vs proactive with best existing DM method).
AI.conclusion: TUBO enables reliable forecasting by combining burst handling, model adaptation, and uncertainty quantification, leading to more reliable forecasts and substantial throughput gains in proactive traffic engineering.

title: Agentic Test-Time Scaling for WebAgents
summary: Test-time scaling has become a standard way to improve performance and boost reliability of neural network models. However, its behavior on agentic, multi-step tasks remains less well-understood: small per-step errors can compound over long horizons; and we find that naive policies that uniformly increase sampling show diminishing returns. In this work, we present CATTS, a simple technique for dynamically allocating compute for multi-step agents. We first conduct an empirical study of inference-time scaling for web agents. We find that uniformly increasing per-step compute quickly saturates in long-horizon environments. We then investigate stronger aggregation strategies, including an LLM-based Arbiter that can outperform naive voting, but that can overrule high-consensus decisions. We show that uncertainty statistics derived from the agent's own vote distribution (entropy and top-1/top-2 margin) correlate with downstream success and provide a practical signal for dynamic compute allocation. Based on these findings, we introduce Confidence-Aware Test-Time Scaling (CATTS), which uses vote-derived uncertainty to allocate compute only when decisions are genuinely contentious. CATTS improves performance on WebArena-Lite and GoBrowse by up to 9.1% over React while using up to 2.3x fewer tokens than uniform scaling, providing both efficiency gains and an interpretable decision rule.

AI.tldr: CATTS dynamically allocates compute at test time for multi-step agents by using vote-derived uncertainty (entropy, top-1/top-2 margins) to decide when decisions are genuinely contentious, improving performance while using fewer tokens than uniform scaling.
AI.motivation: In long-horizon agent tasks, small per-step errors accumulate and naive uniform scaling of compute yields diminishing returns. A principled, content-sensitive compute allocation could improve efficiency and performance.
AI.method: 1) Empirical study of inference-time scaling for web agents comparing uniform scaling and enhanced strategies. 2) Evaluation of an LLM-based Arbiter and its potential overrule behavior. 3) Derivation of uncertainty signals from the agent’s own vote distribution (entropy, margins). 4) Introduction of CATTS that uses these signals to allocate additional compute only on contentious decisions.
AI.result: Uncertainty-based signals correlate with downstream success. CATTS achieves up to 9.1% improvement over React on WebArena-Lite and GoBrowse and uses up to 2.3x fewer tokens than uniform scaling, delivering efficiency gains and interpretable decision rules.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, uncertainty-driven test-time scaling is effective for multi-step agents, offering better performance with fewer tokens and providing a practical, interpretable rule for when to allocate more compute.

title: MUSE: Multi-Tenant Model Serving With Seamless Model Updates
comment: Currently under review for KDD 2026 (Applied Data Science)
summary: In binary classification systems, decision thresholds translate model scores into actions. Choosing suitable thresholds relies on the specific distribution of the underlying model scores but also on the specific business decisions of each client using that model. However, retraining models inevitably shifts score distributions, invalidating existing thresholds. In multi-tenant Score-as-a-Service environments, where decision boundaries reside in client-managed infrastructure, this creates a severe bottleneck: recalibration requires coordinating threshold updates across hundreds of clients, consuming excessive human hours and leading to model stagnation. We introduce MUSE, a model serving framework that enables seamless model updates by decoupling model scores from client decision boundaries. Designed for multi-tenancy, MUSE optimizes infrastructure re-use by sharing models via dynamic intent-based routing, combined with a two-level score transformation that maps model outputs to a stable, reference distribution. Deployed at scale by Feedzai, MUSE processes over a thousand events per second, and over 55 billion events in the last 12 months, across several dozens of tenants, while maintaining high-availability and low-latency guarantees. By reducing model lead time from weeks to minutes, MUSE promotes model resilience against shifting attacks, saving millions of dollars in fraud losses and operational costs.

AI.tldr: MUSE decouples model scores from client decision thresholds in multi-tenant serving, using dynamic intent-based routing and a two-level score transformation to a stable reference distribution, enabling rapid model updates with low recalibration effort.
AI.motivation: Threshold recalibration becomes a bottleneck in multi-tenant Score-as-a-Service when model retraining shifts score distributions; a decoupled, reusable serving layer can preserve decision boundaries and reduce lead time.
AI.method: Share models via dynamic intent-based routing across tenants and apply a two-level score transformation that maps model outputs to a stable reference distribution, enabling consistent client decision boundaries despite score shifts.
AI.result: Scale deployment at Feedzai processes >1k events/s and >55B events/year across dozens of tenants; maintains high availability and low latency; reduces model lead time from weeks to minutes; potential savings in fraud losses and operational costs.
AI.conclusion: MUSE enables resilient, scalable, multi-tenant model serving by separating scoring from decision thresholds, leveraging shared models and stable score mappings to mitigate threshold drift and accelerate model deployment.

title: Improving Medical Visual Reinforcement Fine-Tuning via Perception and Reasoning Augmentation
summary:
While recent advances in Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RFT) have shown that rule-based reward schemes can enable effective post-training for large language models, their extension to cross-modal, vision-centric domains remains largely underexplored. This limitation is especially pronounced in the medical imaging domain, where effective performance requires both robust visual perception and structured reasoning. In this work, we address this gap by proposing VRFT-Aug, a visual reinforcement fine-tuning framework tailored for the medical domain. VRFT-Aug introduces a series of training strategies designed to augment both perception and reasoning, including prior knowledge injection, perception-driven policy refinement, medically informed reward shaping, and behavioral imitation. Together, these methods aim to stabilize and improve the RFT process.
  Through extensive experiments across multiple medical datasets, we show that our approaches consistently outperform both standard supervised fine-tuning and RFT baselines. Moreover, we provide empirically grounded insights and practical training heuristics that can be generalized to other medical image tasks. We hope this work contributes actionable guidance and fresh inspiration for the ongoing effort to develop reliable, reasoning-capable models for high-stakes medical applications.

AI.tldr: VRFT-Aug is a visual reinforcement fine-tuning framework for medical imaging that adds prior knowledge injection, perception-driven policy refinement, medically informed reward shaping, and behavioral imitation to improve stability and performance over supervised fine-tuning and standard RFT baselines; validated on multiple medical datasets with actionable training heuristics.
AI.motivation: RFT has been effective for language models but its extension to cross-modal, vision-centric domains is underexplored. In medical imaging, success requires robust perception and structured reasoning; there is a need for stable, reliable RFT methods tailored to high-stakes medical tasks.
AI.method: VRFT-Aug combines multiple strategies for perception and reasoning: (1) prior knowledge injection to infuse domain expertise, (2) perception-driven policy refinement to align perception with decision-making, (3) medically informed reward shaping to encode clinical objectives and safety constraints, (4) behavioral imitation to leverage expert-like behavior. These components are integrated to augment perception and reasoning during reinforcement fine-tuning.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on several medical imaging datasets shows VRFT-Aug consistently outperforms standard supervised fine-tuning and baseline RFT methods. The paper also offers empirically grounded insights and practical training heuristics that can generalize to other medical image tasks.
AI.conclusion: The work provides actionable guidance for developing reliable, reasoning-capable models for high-stakes medical applications and demonstrates that a multi-faceted VRFT approach can stabilize and improve RFT in vision-centric medical tasks.

title: Temperature as a Meta-Policy: Adaptive Temperature in LLM Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted at ICLR 2026. 10 pages (main text) + supplementary material, 6 figures
summary: Temperature is a crucial hyperparameter in large language models (LLMs), controlling the trade-off between exploration and exploitation during text generation. High temperatures encourage diverse but noisy outputs, while low temperatures produce focused outputs but may cause premature convergence. Yet static or heuristic temperature schedules fail to adapt to the dynamic demands of reinforcement learning (RL) throughout training, often limiting policy improvement. We propose Temperature Adaptive Meta Policy Optimization (TAMPO), a new framework that recasts temperature control as a learnable meta-policy. TAMPO operates through a hierarchical two-loop process. In the inner loop, the LLM policy is updated (e.g., using GRPO) with trajectories sampled at the temperature selected by the meta-policy. In the outer loop, meta-policy updates the distribution over candidate temperatures by rewarding those that maximize the likelihood of high-advantage trajectories. This trajectory-guided, reward-driven mechanism enables online adaptation without additional rollouts, directly aligning exploration with policy improvement. On five mathematical reasoning benchmarks, TAMPO outperforms baselines using fixed or heuristic temperatures, establishing temperature as an effective learnable meta-policy for adaptive exploration in LLM reinforcement learning. Accepted at ICLR 2026.

AI.tldr: Temperature is learned as a meta-policy (TAMPO) for adaptive exploration in LLM RL via a two-loop hierarchy; outperforms fixed/heuristic temperatures on math reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Static or heuristic temperature schedules inadequately adapt exploration-exploitation demands during RL training, limiting policy improvement in LLMs.
AI.method: Propose TAMPO: inner loop updates the LLM policy using trajectories sampled at a temperature chosen by a meta-policy; outer loop updates the temperature distribution by rewarding temperatures that yield high-advantage trajectories, enabling online adaptation without extra rollouts.
AI.result: Empirical results on five mathematical reasoning benchmarks show TAMPO surpasses baselines with fixed/heuristic temperatures; demonstrates temperature can be effectively learned as a meta-policy for adaptive exploration; accepted at ICLR 2026.
AI.conclusion: Treating temperature as a learnable meta-policy aligns exploration with policy improvement and enables online adaptation in LLM RL; potential extension to other hyperparameters and domains, with trade-offs in computation and stability.

title: Safe Fairness Guarantees Without Demographics in Classification: Spectral Uncertainty Set Perspective
summary: As automated classification systems become increasingly prevalent, concerns have emerged over their potential to reinforce and amplify existing societal biases. In the light of this issue, many methods have been proposed to enhance the fairness guarantees of classifiers. Most of the existing interventions assume access to group information for all instances, a requirement rarely met in practice. Fairness without access to demographic information has often been approached through robust optimization techniques,which target worst-case outcomes over a set of plausible distributions known as the uncertainty set. However, their effectiveness is strongly influenced by the chosen uncertainty set. In fact, existing approaches often overemphasize outliers or overly pessimistic scenarios, compromising both overall performance and fairness. To overcome these limitations, we introduce SPECTRE, a minimax-fair method that adjusts the spectrum of a simple Fourier feature mapping and constrains the extent to which the worst-case distribution can deviate from the empirical distribution. We perform extensive experiments on the American Community Survey datasets involving 20 states. The safeness of SPECTRE comes as it provides the highest average values on fairness guarantees together with the smallest interquartile range in comparison to state-of-the-art approaches, even compared to those with access to demographic group information. In addition, we provide a theoretical analysis that derives computable bounds on the worst-case error for both individual groups and the overall population, as well as characterizes the worst-case distributions responsible for these extremal performances

AI.tldr: SPECTRE: A minimax-fair classifier without demographic data that uses a spectrum-adjusted Fourier feature map and bounds the deviation of the worst-case distribution from the empirical one.
AI.motivation: Fairness without access to demographic information and sensitivity to the choice of uncertainty sets in robust optimization.
AI.method: Introduce SPECTRE, a minimax-fair objective that modulates the spectrum of a simple Fourier feature mapping and constrains the extent to which the worst-case distribution can deviate from the empirical distribution; provide theoretical bounds on worst-case error for individual groups and the overall population, and characterize the worst-case distributions; empirical evaluation on American Community Survey data across 20 states.
AI.result: SPECTRE achieves the highest average fairness guarantees with the smallest interquartile range among state-of-the-art methods, even beating several approaches that have access to demographic information; provides computable bounds on worst-case error and identifies the distributions driving extremal performance.
AI.conclusion: SPECTRE offers a practical, theoretically grounded approach to fairness without demographic data, delivering strong worst-case fairness guarantees and interpretable worst-case distributions, and motivates spectrum-based feature mappings for robust fairness.

title: Evaluating LLM Safety Under Repeated Inference via Accelerated Prompt Stress Testing
summary: Traditional benchmarks for large language models (LLMs) primarily assess safety risk through breadth-oriented evaluation across diverse tasks. However, real-world deployment exposes a different class of risk: operational failures arising from repeated inference on identical or near-identical prompts rather than broad task generalization. In high-stakes settings, response consistency and safety under sustained use are critical. We introduce Accelerated Prompt Stress Testing (APST), a depth-oriented evaluation framework inspired by reliability engineering. APST repeatedly samples identical prompts under controlled operational conditions (e.g., decoding temperature) to surface latent failure modes including hallucinations, refusal inconsistency, and unsafe completions. Rather than treating failures as isolated events, APST models them as stochastic outcomes of independent inference events. We formalize safety failures using Bernoulli and binomial models to estimate per-inference failure probabilities, enabling quantitative comparison of reliability across models and decoding configurations. Applying APST to multiple instruction-tuned LLMs evaluated on AIR-BENCH-derived safety prompts, we find that models with similar benchmark-aligned scores can exhibit substantially different empirical failure rates under repeated sampling, particularly as temperature increases. These results demonstrate that shallow, single-sample evaluation can obscure meaningful reliability differences under sustained use. APST complements existing benchmarks by providing a practical framework for evaluating LLM safety and reliability under repeated inference, bridging benchmark alignment and deployment-oriented risk assessment.

AI.tldr: APST introduces a depth-oriented reliability framework for LLM safety under repeated prompts, modeling per-inference failures as Bernoulli trials to reveal reliability differences not captured by single-sample benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Real-world deployment risk stems from operational failures during repeated inferences on identical prompts. There is a need to assess reliability and safety under sustained use, similar to reliability engineering.
AI.method: Repeatedly sample identical prompts under controlled conditions (e.g., fixed temperature) to surface latent failures such as hallucinations and unsafe completions. Treat failures as stochastic Bernoulli/binomial events and estimate per-inference failure probabilities to compare models and decoding configurations.
AI.result: Application to several instruction-tuned LLMs on AIR-BENCH-derived safety prompts reveals that models with similar single-sample benchmark scores can exhibit substantially different failure rates under repetition, especially as temperature increases.
AI.conclusion: APST complements existing benchmarks by offering a practical framework to evaluate safety and reliability under repeated inference, bridging benchmark alignment with deployment-oriented risk assessment.

title: Latent-Variable Learning of SPDEs via Wiener Chaos
summary: We study the problem of learning the law of linear stochastic partial differential equations (SPDEs) with additive Gaussian forcing from spatiotemporal observations. Most existing deep learning approaches either assume access to the driving noise or initial condition, or rely on deterministic surrogate models that fail to capture intrinsic stochasticity. We propose a structured latent-variable formulation that requires only observations of solution realizations and learns the underlying randomly forced dynamics. Our approach combines a spectral Galerkin projection with a truncated Wiener chaos expansion, yielding a principled separation between deterministic evolution and stochastic forcing. This reduces the infinite-dimensional SPDE to a finite system of parametrized ordinary differential equations governing latent temporal dynamics. The latent dynamics and stochastic forcing are jointly inferred through variational learning, allowing recovery of stochastic structure without explicit observation or simulation of noise during training. Empirical evaluation on synthetic data demonstrates state-of-the-art performance under comparable modeling assumptions across bounded and unbounded one-dimensional spatial domains.

AI.tldr: A structured latent-variable model for linear SPDEs with additive Gaussian noise is proposed, combining spectral Galerkin projection and Wiener chaos expansion to infer latent dynamics and stochastic forcing from solution data via variational learning; reduces the SPDE to a finite ODE system and demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on synthetic 1D domains.
AI.motivation: Current deep-learning approaches for SPDEs often require access to the driving noise or initial conditions or rely on deterministic surrogates that fail to capture intrinsic stochasticity. There is a need to learn the stochastic forcing structure directly from solution realizations.
AI.method: Apply a spectral Galerkin projection plus a truncated Wiener chaos expansion to separate deterministic evolution from stochastic forcing. This yields a finite-dimensional system of parametrized ordinary differential equations governing latent temporal dynamics. Infer the latent dynamics and stochastic forcing jointly via variational learning, enabling recovery of stochastic structure without explicit noise observations during training.
AI.result: On synthetic data, the method achieves state-of-the-art performance under comparable modeling assumptions, applicable to both bounded and unbounded one-dimensional spatial domains.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables recovery of stochastic structure from solution data alone, providing a principled separation of deterministic and stochastic components and scalable inference for SPDE dynamics in 1D domains.

title: Temporal Difference Learning with Constrained Initial Representations
summary: Recently, there have been numerous attempts to enhance the sample efficiency of off-policy reinforcement learning (RL) agents when interacting with the environment, including architecture improvements and new algorithms. Despite these advances, they overlook the potential of directly constraining the initial representations of the input data, which can intuitively alleviate the distribution shift issue and stabilize training. In this paper, we introduce the Tanh function into the initial layer to fulfill such a constraint. We theoretically unpack the convergence property of the temporal difference learning with the Tanh function under linear function approximation. Motivated by theoretical insights, we present our Constrained Initial Representations framework, tagged CIR, which is made up of three components: (i) the Tanh activation along with normalization methods to stabilize representations; (ii) the skip connection module to provide a linear pathway from the shallow layer to the deep layer; (iii) the convex Q-learning that allows a more flexible value estimate and mitigates potential conservatism. Empirical results show that CIR exhibits strong performance on numerous continuous control tasks, even being competitive or surpassing existing strong baseline methods.

AI.tldr: Constrained Initial Representations (CIR) improves sample efficiency and stability of off-policy reinforcement learning by constraining the input representation through a Tanh-activated initial layer with normalization, a skip-connection path, and convex Q-learning; supported by convergence analysis for TD with linear function approximation and strong empirical results on continuous control tasks, often beating strong baselines.
AI.motivation: To reduce distribution shift and training instability caused by poorly initialized representations in off-policy RL, the authors propose constraining the initial representation rather than relying solely on architectural or algorithmic advances.
AI.method: Introduce Tanh activation in the initial layer with normalization to constrain representations; add a skip connection to provide a linear pathway from shallow to deep layers; adopt convex Q-learning to offer a flexible yet less conservative value estimate; provide theoretical analysis of TD convergence with Tanh under linear function approximation; empirically evaluate CIR on continuous control benchmarks.
AI.result: CIR achieves strong performance across multiple continuous control tasks and is competitive with or surpasses existing strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: Constraining initial representations via CIR stabilizes training and improves sample efficiency in off-policy RL, supported by theoretical convergence insights and robust empirical results; the approach offers a practical, plug-in modification to existing architectures.

title: SpaTeoGL: Spatiotemporal Graph Learning for Interpretable Seizure Onset Zone Analysis from Intracranial EEG
summary: Accurate localization of the seizure onset zone (SOZ) from intracranial EEG (iEEG) is essential for epilepsy surgery but is challenged by complex spatiotemporal seizure dynamics. We propose SpaTeoGL, a spatiotemporal graph learning framework for interpretable seizure network analysis. SpaTeoGL jointly learns window-level spatial graphs capturing interactions among iEEG electrodes and a temporal graph linking time windows based on similarity of their spatial structure. The method is formulated within a smooth graph signal processing framework and solved via an alternating block coordinate descent algorithm with convergence guarantees. Experiments on a multicenter iEEG dataset with successful surgical outcomes show that SpaTeoGL is competitive with a baseline based on horizontal visibility graphs and logistic regression, while improving non-SOZ identification and providing interpretable insights into seizure onset and propagation dynamics.

AI.tldr: SpaTeoGL is a spatiotemporal graph learning framework for iEEG that jointly learns window-level spatial graphs and a temporal graph to analyze seizure networks, enabling interpretable localization of the seizure onset zone (SOZ) and seizure propagation insights.
AI.motivation: Accurate SOZ localization from iEEG is challenged by complex, dynamic seizure networks; there is a need for interpretable models that capture both spatial electrode interactions and temporal evolution.
AI.method: SpaTeoGL jointly learns: (1) window-level spatial graphs capturing electrode interactions within time windows, and (2) a temporal graph linking time windows based on similarity of their spatial structure. The framework uses a smooth graph signal processing formulation and is optimized via alternating block coordinate descent with convergence guarantees.
AI.result: On multicenter iEEG data with successful surgical outcomes, SpaTeoGL is competitive with a baseline that uses horizontal visibility graphs and logistic regression, while improving non-SOZ identification and providing interpretable insights into seizure onset and propagation dynamics.
AI.conclusion: SpaTeoGL delivers an interpretable, effective joint spatiotemporal graph representation for iEEG seizure analysis, aiding SOZ localization and understanding of seizure dynamics.

title: TopoFair: Linking Topological Bias to Fairness in Link Prediction Benchmarks
summary: Graph link prediction (LP) plays a critical role in socially impactful applications, such as job recommendation and friendship formation. Ensuring fairness in this task is thus essential. While many fairness-aware methods manipulate graph structures to mitigate prediction disparities, the topological biases inherent to social graph structures remain poorly understood and are often reduced to homophily alone. This undermines the generalization potential of fairness interventions and limits their applicability across diverse network topologies. In this work, we propose a novel benchmarking framework for fair LP, centered on the structural biases of the underlying graphs. We begin by reviewing and formalizing a broad taxonomy of topological bias measures relevant to fairness in graphs. In parallel, we introduce a flexible graph generation method that simultaneously ensures fidelity to real-world graph patterns and enables controlled variation across a wide spectrum of structural biases. We apply this framework to evaluate both classical and fairness-aware LP models across multiple use cases. Our results provide a fine-grained empirical analysis of the interactions between predictive fairness and structural biases. This new perspective reveals the sensitivity of fairness interventions to beyond-homophily biases and underscores the need for structurally grounded fairness evaluations in graph learning.

AI.tldr: Proposes a fairness benchmarking framework for link prediction in graphs, focusing on topological biases beyond homophily; develops taxonomy of topological bias measures and a flexible graph generator to vary structural biases while preserving real-world patterns; evaluates classical and fairness-aware LP models across use cases; finds that fairness interventions interact with non-homophily biases and argues for structurally grounded fairness evaluation.
AI.motivation: Fairness in graph link prediction is hampered by reliance on homophily and unexamined structural biases; existing methods lack benchmarks to study how topology affects fairness, limiting generalization across networks.
AI.method: 1) formalize a broad taxonomy of topological bias measures relevant to fairness in graphs; 2) design a flexible graph generation method that preserves real-world patterns while enabling controlled variation of structural biases; 3) apply the framework to evaluate both classical and fairness-aware LP models across multiple use cases.
AI.result: Fine-grained empirical analysis shows interactions between predictive fairness and structural biases; fairness interventions are sensitive to beyond-homophily biases; highlights need for structurally grounded fairness evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Structural biases in graphs must be integrated into fairness evaluation; the benchmarking framework enables robust assessment of LP models under diverse topologies, promoting generalizable fairness interventions.

title: Towards On-Policy SFT: Distribution Discriminant Theory and its Applications in LLM Training
summary: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is computationally efficient but often yields inferior generalization compared to reinforcement learning (RL). This gap is primarily driven by RL's use of on-policy data. We propose a framework to bridge this chasm by enabling On-Policy SFT. We first present \textbf{\textit{Distribution Discriminant Theory (DDT)}}, which explains and quantifies the alignment between data and the model-induced distribution. Leveraging DDT, we introduce two complementary techniques: (i) \textbf{\textit{In-Distribution Finetuning (IDFT)}}, a loss-level method to enhance generalization ability of SFT, and (ii) \textbf{\textit{Hinted Decoding}}, a data-level technique that can re-align the training corpus to the model's distribution. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework achieves generalization performance on par with prominent offline RL algorithms, including DPO and SimPO, while maintaining the efficiency of an SFT pipeline. The proposed framework thus offers a practical alternative in domains where RL is infeasible. We open-source the code here: https://github.com/zhangmiaosen2000/Towards-On-Policy-SFT

AI.tldr: Proposes Distribution Discriminant Theory to bridge supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL) by enabling On-Policy SFT through IDFT and Hinted Decoding. Empirically matches offline RL performance (DPO, SimPO) while preserving SFT efficiency; code released.
AI.motivation: SFT is computationally efficient but often underperforms RL due to weaker use of on-policy data. Bridging this gap could yield RL-like generalization within an SFT pipeline, enabling practical learning in settings where RL is infeasible.
AI.method: Introduce Distribution Discriminant Theory (DDT) to quantify alignment between data distribution and model-induced distribution. Propose two complementary techniques: (1) In-Distribution Finetuning (IDFT) — a loss-level method to boost SFT generalization; (2) Hinted Decoding — a data-level approach to realign the training corpus with the model's distribution.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show the On-Policy SFT framework attains generalization performance comparable to offline RL algorithms like DPO and SimPO, while retaining SFT efficiency. Open-source code provided.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a practical alternative to RL in domains where RL is infeasible, enabling RL-like generalization within an efficient supervised pipeline.

title: From Path Signatures to Sequential Modeling: Incremental Signature Contributions for Offline RL
summary: Path signatures embed trajectories into tensor algebra and constitute a universal, non-parametric representation of paths; however, in the standard form, they collapse temporal structure into a single global object, which limits their suitability for decision-making problems that require step-wise reactivity. We propose the Incremental Signature Contribution (ISC) method, which decomposes truncated path signatures into a temporally ordered sequence of elements in the tensor-algebra space, corresponding to incremental contributions induced by last path increments. This reconstruction preserves the algebraic structure and expressivity of signatures, while making their internal temporal evolution explicit, enabling processing signature-based representations via sequential modeling approaches. In contrast to full signatures, ISC is inherently sensitive to instantaneous trajectory updates, which is critical for sensitive and stability-requiring control dynamics. Building on this representation, we introduce ISC-Transformer (ISCT), an offline reinforcement learning model that integrates ISC into a standard Transformer architecture without further architectural modification. We evaluate ISCT on HalfCheetah, Walker2d, Hopper, and Maze2d, including settings with delayed rewards and downgraded datasets. The results demonstrate that ISC method provides a theoretically grounded and practically effective alternative to path processing for temporally sensitive control tasks.

AI.tldr: ISC decomposes truncated path signatures into a temporally ordered sequence of incremental contributions, enabling sequential processing and improving temporally sensitive control in offline RL; ISCT integrates ISC into Transformer-based RL without changing architecture.
AI.motivation: Standard path signatures compress temporal information into a single global object, hindering step-wise reactivity. A temporally explicit representation is needed for decision-making and control tasks.
AI.method: Decompose truncated path signatures into incremental contributions corresponding to last path increments; preserve algebraic structure of signatures; build ISC-Transformer by integrating ISC into a standard Transformer architecture; evaluate on offline RL tasks with continuous control (HalfCheetah, Walker2d, Hopper, Maze2d) including delayed rewards and degraded datasets.
AI.result: ISCT with ISC demonstrates improved performance over baselines on multiple continuous-control tasks under challenging settings (delayed rewards, downgraded datasets), suggesting ISC is a theoretically grounded and practically effective alternative to full path processing for temporally sensitive control tasks.
AI.conclusion: ISC provides a theoretically grounded and practically effective alternative to full path processing for temporally sensitive control tasks; ISC-Transformer can leverage sequential modeling without changing network architecture.

title: Deep Kernel Fusion for Transformers
summary: Agentic LLM inference with long contexts is increasingly limited by memory bandwidth rather than compute. In this setting, SwiGLU MLP blocks, whose large weights exceed cache capacity, become a major yet under-optimized bottleneck. We propose DeepFusionKernel, a deeply fused kernel that cuts HBM traffic and boosts cache reuse, delivering up to 13.2% speedup on H100 and 9.7% on A100 over SGLang. Integrated with SGLang and paired with a kernel scheduler, DeepFusionKernel ensures consistent accelerations over generation lengths, while remaining adaptable to diverse models, inference configurations, and hardware platforms.

AI.tldr: A deeply fused kernel (DeepFusionKernel) mitigates memory bandwidth bottlenecks in agentic LLM inference by optimizing SwiGLU MLP blocks, yielding notable speedups over SGLang and maintaining consistency across generation lengths; it is designed to be portable across models, configurations, and hardware.
AI.motivation: Long-context LLM inference is increasingly bottlenecked by memory bandwidth rather than compute, with SwiGLU MLP blocks (large weights) failing to fit caches and causing bottlenecks.
AI.method: Develop a deeply fused kernel, DeepFusionKernel, to cut HBM traffic and boost cache reuse; integrate with SGLang and a kernel scheduler to ensure consistent acceleration across generation lengths and across varying models, configs, and hardware.
AI.result: Up to 13.2% speedup on H100 and 9.7% on A100 over SGLang; accelerations are consistent across generation lengths; demonstrated within the SGLang ecosystem and adaptable to diverse setups.
AI.conclusion: DeepFusionKernel addresses memory bandwidth bottlenecks in SwiGLU-dominated MLP blocks, delivering portable speedups and enabling robust LLM inference across hardware platforms and configurations.

title: CAAL: Confidence-Aware Active Learning for Heteroscedastic Atmospheric Regression
summary: Quantifying the impacts of air pollution on health and climate relies on key atmospheric particle properties such as toxicity and hygroscopicity. However, these properties typically require complex observational techniques or expensive particle-resolved numerical simulations, limiting the availability of labeled data. We therefore estimate these hard-to-measure particle properties from routinely available observations (e.g., air pollutant concentrations and meteorological conditions). Because routine observations only indirectly reflect particle composition and structure, the mapping from routine observations to particle properties is noisy and input-dependent, yielding a heteroscedastic regression setting. With a limited and costly labeling budget, the central challenge is to select which samples to measure or simulate. While active learning is a natural approach, most acquisition strategies rely on predictive uncertainty. Under heteroscedastic noise, this signal conflates reducible epistemic uncertainty with irreducible aleatoric uncertainty, causing limited budgets to be wasted in noise-dominated regions. To address this challenge, we propose a confidence-aware active learning framework (CAAL) for efficient and robust sample selection in heteroscedastic settings. CAAL consists of two components: a decoupled uncertainty-aware training objective that separately optimises the predictive mean and noise level to stabilise uncertainty estimation, and a confidence-aware acquisition function that dynamically weights epistemic uncertainty using predicted aleatoric uncertainty as a reliability signal. Experiments on particle-resolved numerical simulations and real atmospheric observations show that CAAL consistently outperforms standard AL baselines. The proposed framework provides a practical and general solution for the efficient expansion of high-cost atmospheric particle property databases.

AI.tldr: CAAL is a confidence-aware active learning framework for heteroscedastic regression to estimate hard-to-measure atmospheric particle properties from routine observations, decoupling mean and noise and weighting epistemic uncertainty by predicted aleatoric uncertainty to improve label efficiency.
AI.motivation: Labeling hard particle properties (toxicity, hygroscopicity) requires costly experiments or simulations. Heteroscedastic noise makes standard active-learning uncertain about where to sample, risking wasted budget on noise-dominated regions.
AI.method: Two-part design: (1) decoupled training objective that separately optimizes predictive mean and noise level to stabilize uncertainty estimates; (2) confidence-aware acquisition function that dynamically scales epistemic uncertainty using predicted aleatoric uncertainty as a reliability signal.
AI.result: CAAL consistently outperforms standard active-learning baselines on both particle-resolved simulations and real atmospheric observations, enabling more efficient expansion of high-cost particle-property databases.
AI.conclusion: CAAL provides a practical, general solution for robust and efficient sampling in heteroscedastic settings, enabling better growth of costly atmospheric particle-property databases and potentially other domains with input-dependent noise.

title: Towards Sustainable Investment Policies Informed by Opponent Shaping
summary: Addressing climate change requires global coordination, yet rational economic actors often prioritize immediate gains over collective welfare, resulting in social dilemmas. InvestESG is a recently proposed multi-agent simulation that captures the dynamic interplay between investors and companies under climate risk. We provide a formal characterization of the conditions under which InvestESG exhibits an intertemporal social dilemma, deriving theoretical thresholds at which individual incentives diverge from collective welfare. Building on this, we apply Advantage Alignment, a scalable opponent shaping algorithm shown to be effective in general-sum games, to influence agent learning in InvestESG. We offer theoretical insights into why Advantage Alignment systematically favors socially beneficial equilibria by biasing learning dynamics toward cooperative outcomes. Our results demonstrate that strategically shaping the learning processes of economic agents can result in better outcomes that could inform policy mechanisms to better align market incentives with long-term sustainability goals.

AI.tldr: Formalizes intertemporal social dilemmas in InvestESG, derives thresholds where individual incentives misalign with collective welfare, and shows Advantage Alignment can bias learning toward cooperative equilibria to improve long-term sustainability outcomes.
AI.motivation: Climate policy requires alignment of short-term incentives with long-term welfare. Rational investors and Firms may ignore collective climate benefits, creating intertemporal social dilemmas. The paper formalizes these dynamics in InvestESG and explores policy-relevant learning biases.
AI.method: 1) Formal characterization of conditions for intertemporal social dilemma in InvestESG with theoretical thresholds; 2) Apply Advantage Alignment (a scalable opponent-shaping algorithm) to influence learning in InvestESG; 3) Provide theoretical explanation for why Advantage Alignment favors socially beneficial equilibria; 4) Demonstrate potential implications for policy mechanisms.
AI.result: The study identifies thresholds at which individual incentives diverge from collective welfare in InvestESG, and demonstrates that Advantage Alignment can steer learning dynamics toward cooperative, socially beneficial equilibria, reducing the social dilemma and improving anticipated sustainability outcomes.
AI.conclusion: Strategically shaping the learning processes of economic agents can yield better long-term outcomes and inform policy mechanisms to better align market incentives with sustainability goals.

title: Robust Optimization Approach and Learning Based Hide-and-Seek Game for Resilient Network Design
summary: We study the design of resilient and reliable communication networks in which a signal can be transferred only up to a limited distance before its quality falls below an acceptable threshold. When excessive signal degradation occurs, regeneration is required through regenerators installed at selected network nodes. In this work, both network links and nodes are subject to uncertainty. The installation costs of regenerators are modeled using a budgeted uncertainty set. In addition, link lengths follow a dynamic budgeted uncertainty set introduced in this paper, where deviations may vary over time. Robust optimization seeks solutions whose performance is guaranteed under all scenarios represented by the underlying uncertainty set. Accordingly, the objective is to identify a minimum-cost subset of nodes for regenerator deployment that ensures full network connectivity, even under the worst possible realizations of uncertainty. To solve the problem, we first formulate it within a robust optimization framework, and then develop scalable solution methods based on column-and-constraint generation, Benders decomposition, and iterative robust optimization. In addition, we formulate a learning-based hide-and-seek game to further analyze the problem structure. The proposed approaches are evaluated against classical static budgeted robust models and deterministic worst-case formulations. Both theoretical analysis and computational results demonstrate the effectiveness and advantages of our methodology.

AI.tldr: A robust optimization framework for deploying regenerators in a distance-constrained network, handling node and link uncertainty with budgeted and dynamic budgeted uncertainty sets, solved via column-and-constraint generation, Benders decomposition, and iterative robust optimization, plus a learning-based hide-and-seek analysis; it outperforms static worst-case and deterministic models.
AI.motivation: Design resilient networks where signal quality deteriorates beyond a fixed distance, necessitating regeneration. Uncertainty in regeneration costs and link lengths (varying over time) motivates robust, cost-effective deployment of regenerators to guarantee connectivity.
AI.method: Formulate a robust optimization model with budgeted uncertainty sets for regenerator installation costs and a dynamic budgeted uncertainty set for link lengths. Develop scalable solution methods: column-and-constraint generation, Benders decomposition, and iterative robust optimization. Introduce a learning-based hide-and-seek game to analyze problem structure.
AI.result: The proposed approaches are scalable and effective, outperforming classical static budgeted robust models and deterministic worst-case formulations. Theoretical analysis and computational results demonstrate robustness, efficiency, and advantages of dynamic uncertainty modeling and the learning framework.
AI.conclusion: Robust design with dynamic, time-varying uncertainty and selective regeneration yields reliable, cost-effective networks. The combination of C&C generation, Benders, iterative robust optimization, and the learning-based analysis provides scalable and insightful solutions for large-scale, uncertain networks.

title: A$^{2}$V-SLP: Alignment-Aware Variational Modeling for Disentangled Sign Language Production
summary: Building upon recent structural disentanglement frameworks for sign language production, we propose A$^{2}$V-SLP, an alignment-aware variational framework that learns articulator-wise disentangled latent distributions rather than deterministic embeddings. A disentangled Variational Autoencoder (VAE) encodes ground-truth sign pose sequences and extracts articulator-specific mean and variance vectors, which are used as distributional supervision for training a non-autoregressive Transformer. Given text embeddings, the Transformer predicts both latent means and log-variances, while the VAE decoder reconstructs the final sign pose sequences through stochastic sampling at the decoding stage. This formulation maintains articulator-level representations by avoiding deterministic latent collapse through distributional latent modeling. In addition, we integrate a gloss attention mechanism to strengthen alignment between linguistic input and articulated motion. Experimental results show consistent gains over deterministic latent regression, achieving state-of-the-art back-translation performance and improved motion realism in a fully gloss-free setting.

AI.tldr: Proposes A²V-SLP: an alignment-aware variational framework for sign language production that uses articulator-wise disentangled latent distributions via a VAE to supervise a non-autoregressive Transformer, yielding improved translation and motion realism in gloss-free settings.
AI.motivation: Overcoming deterministic latent embeddings and latent collapse in sign language production; improve articulator-specific representations and alignment between linguistic input and articulated motion, even without gloss annotations.
AI.method: A VAE encodes ground-truth sign pose sequences into articulator-specific means and variances; these distributions supervise a non-autoregressive Transformer that, from text embeddings, predicts latent means and log-variances. The VAE decoder reconstructs sign poses via stochastic sampling during decoding. A gloss attention mechanism strengthens the alignment between language input and motion.
AI.result: Consistent gains over deterministic latent regression; achieves state-of-the-art back-translation performance and improved motion realism in a fully gloss-free setting.
